descriptionQuality.justification: The coded courses have substantive descriptions (HIST 310 Co-op, HIST 490 Research Seminar, HSS 404 Senior Seminar, MGMT 290 Business Law); the one gap is HIST 312 Professional Development in Law, which dangles and shows no description.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions, and the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=2) are a GER 200 placeholder and HIST 312, so the capstone chain (ENGL 102 -> GER 200 -> upper-level -> HSS 404 -> HIST 490) is unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: The concentration is defined largely by science requirements; no dedicated patent or IP course is visible even though HIST 384 Invention and Regulation exists and would supply the doctrinal core.

missingTopics.topic: Patent drafting, claims, and USPTO practice
missingTopics.rationale: There is no course teaching patent-agent skills (claim drafting, prosecution, USPTO procedure), which are the concrete deliverables of a patent-law track.
USPTO patent-bar eligibility rewards substantial engineering/science coursework; the plan provides Calculus and generic natural-science electives but no engineering/CS depth pathway.
Modern patent eligibility turns on Alice/Mayo (software) and biotech questions; no course addresses these decisive doctrines.
The plan lacks a technology-transfer/licensing course connecting patents to startups and commercialization careers.

emergingTrends.rationale: Questions like inventorship for AI (the DABUS cases) are reshaping patent law and are a natural signature topic for this concentration.
The frontier of patentable subject matter sits in biotech and software; pairing the science spine with these doctrines would be distinctive.

emergingTrends.trend: Global IP harmonization and standard-essential patents
emergingTrends.rationale: Cross-border patent strategy and SEP/FRAND disputes are growing areas the track could address.

emergingTrends.trend: AI-assisted prior-art search and patent analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: Patent search and drafting are being transformed by AI tooling; exposure would modernize the professional-development component (HIST 312).
University and startup IP commercialization is an expanding career destination the co-op (HIST 310) could be oriented toward.

topSuggestions: Require a named patent/IP course (e.g., HIST 384 Invention and Regulation) so the concentration delivers explicit IP doctrine, not just technical eligibility.
Add a defined engineering/CS technical-elective pathway to strengthen USPTO patent-bar readiness beyond Calculus and generic science.
Resolve the HIST 312 dangling reference and the GER 200 placeholder, and assign credits to the null-credit science/elective rows.
Publish program learning outcomes that tie the science requirements to patent-practice readiness.
Clean the plan's presentation (duplicated 'Elective 1' labels, trailing footnote digits in course titles) and name the generic legal-core electives.

b-a-in-theatre-arts-and-technology-444.name: B.A. in Theatre Arts and Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan reconciles and shows a recognizable theatre-arts spine -- an introduction (THTR 101 Living Theatre, THTR 102 Acting Fundamentals), theatre history, playwriting (THTR 365), special topics and practicum (THTR 411, THTR 480), and an HSS 404 capstone. Two coherence problems stand out. First, Theatre History II (THTR 315) is scheduled in Year 2 before Theatre History I (THTR 310) in Year 3, an ordering inversion. Second, the 'and Technology' half of the degree is delivered mainly through cross-listed Rutgers R088 production courses (Theater Tech I, Production I/II/III, all null-code rows) and a generic CS intro, so NJIT's own required courses are history/acting/playwriting-centered and the technology identity is thin.
dimensions.suggestion: Swap the Theatre History I/II ordering and add NJIT-owned theatre-technology courses (lighting, sound, digital/projection design) so 'and Technology' is delivered by the program rather than outsourced to cross-listed production credits.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 4 and every 300-level THTR course resolves to the same 'ENGL 102 + 200-level History/Humanities GER' gate, so the graph is clean but flat with little intra-major sequencing (the History II-before-History I placement confirms the courses are not truly ordered). missingCourseCount is 2, both requirement-category placeholders (GER 200, GER 300); the Rutgers R088 production courses carry null codes and sit outside the prerequisite graph entirely.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce genuine prerequisites within the theatre sequence (e.g. THTR 310 before THTR 315, a design course before the practicum) and give the R088 cross-listed courses resolvable NJIT-equivalent codes.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data. For a degree whose distinctive promise is the fusion of theatre arts with technology, the absence of an outcomes statement leaves the intended arts-plus-technology competency undefined.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-7 learning outcomes spanning performance/production, dramatic literature and history, playwriting, and theatrical technology/design.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and terms are steady at 13-17 credits. Transparency is weakened by a large number of unspecified 'Theatre Requirement Elective', 'Theatre Elective', and 'Free Elective' slots plus an embedded '446 Communication Minor' requirement and null-credit cross-listed rows, so a substantial share of the degree is undefined on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Enumerate the Theatre Requirement vs Theatre Elective lists and specify the credits carried by the embedded Communication Minor so the distribution is legible.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and the plan reconciles, but readability suffers from many null-code rows: the Rutgers R088 courses (Theater Tech I, Production I/II/III), 'or PHYS 203', 'or THTR 212', 'Select one of the following' blocks, and a '446 Communication Minor' line that references another program by number. The visible Theatre History II-before-I ordering also reads as an error.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the R088 cross-listed courses proper codes/titles, resolve the 'Select one of the following' and 'or ...' rows into single choice lines, and correct the history-course ordering.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program has real production exposure through the Rutgers R088 courses, a practicum (THTR 480), and a differentiating technology gesture (a required CS intro and an embedded Communication Minor). But the modern theatrical-technology core that the degree name promises -- lighting, sound, projection/digital scenography, stage management -- is largely absent from NJIT's own required courses, and contemporary digital/virtual production is missing entirely, so the 'Technology' brand is under-delivered.
dimensions.suggestion: Build (or require) NJIT theatre-technology courses in lighting, sound, and digital/projection design so the degree's technology promise is met by current, program-owned coursework.

descriptionQuality.justification: NJIT's own THTR courses are well described (THTR 310/315 on Euro-American theatre history, THTR 365 on writing and staging short plays, THTR 480 on leading a mainstage production). Description quality drops for the technology half of the degree, which is carried by cross-listed Rutgers R088 rows that appear with null codes and no description text, so the 'Technology' content is the least documented part of the plan.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites for the NJIT THTR courses are explicit and consistent (ENGL 102 + a 200-level GER, with THTR 212 showing a concurrent-co-requisite option). Clarity is undercut by the many null-code Rutgers R088 courses whose prerequisites are not surfaced in NJIT terms and by the uniform gate strings that give no sense of relative course level.

missingTopics.rationale: The degree promises technology but requires no NJIT lighting, sound, or scenic-design course; design is left to cross-listed R088 production credits, leaving the core theatre-tech competency unguaranteed.
No course addresses projection mapping, media servers, or virtual/LED-volume production, which is now the leading edge of theatre technology and the natural home for a tech-university theatre degree.
The plan has a practicum but no stage-management course covering scheduling, calling cues, and running a production -- a standard professional competency.
Beyond history and playwriting, there is no dramaturgy/script-analysis course to bridge dramatic literature and production practice.
COM 241 Sound Communication exists elsewhere in the college but is not required; dedicated sound/audio design is missing from a program branded around technology.

emergingTrends.trend: Virtual production and projection mapping (Unreal Engine, LED volumes)
emergingTrends.rationale: As a technological university's theatre degree, positioning around real-time/virtual production would make the 'and Technology' name distinctive rather than aspirational.
THTR 411 Special Topics could host immersive/interactive performance work, a fast-growing form that blends staging with interactive technology.
The embedded Communication Minor already exposes students to digital video (COM 350) and media design; tightening that link would modernize the production strand.

emergingTrends.trend: Motion capture and digital puppetry/performance capture
emergingTrends.rationale: The CS requirement and cross-listed tech courses give a foundation to add performance-capture work bridging theatre and computing.

emergingTrends.trend: Accessibility and inclusive design in performance
emergingTrends.rationale: Captioning, audio description, and accessible staging are becoming standard practice and would give the technology emphasis a socially relevant application.

topSuggestions: Deliver the 'and Technology' promise with NJIT-owned theatre-technology courses (lighting, sound, digital/projection design) instead of outsourcing it to cross-listed Rutgers R088 production credits.
Correct the Theatre History II-before-History I ordering and introduce real prerequisites so the theatre sequence is genuinely progressive.
Publish explicit learning outcomes that define the arts-plus-technology competency the degree certifies.
Clean the plan's presentation: give the R088 cross-listed courses resolvable codes and resolve the 'Select one of the following' / 'or ...' rows.
Add a modern virtual-production or projection-mapping course to make the technology brand current and distinctive.

b-s-general-engineering-676.name: B.S. General Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The freshman/sophomore spine is coherent and well-ordered: CHEM 121/122 (or 125/126), MATH 111->112->211->222/333, PHYS 111/121, one of CS 101/106/115, FED 101, and ENGR 330 (Microcontrollers/IoT) build a solid math-science-design foundation that culminates in the ENGR 400 multidisciplinary capstone. The weakness is that essentially the entire junior and senior year is filled with undifferentiated 'General Engineering Elective (200/300/400 level)' and lab-elective placeholders, so there is no defined disciplinary depth or guaranteed upper-division progression beyond the capstone.
dimensions.suggestion: Define one or two named required upper-division threads (e.g. add ENGR 301 Engineering Applications of Data Science and a systems/controls course) so the plan shows genuine 300/400-level progression rather than a wall of generic 'General Engineering Elective' rows.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is shallow but sensible for a broad first-two-years plan: deepestPrereqChain is 4 (MATH 111->112->211/222/333 and PHYS 111->121) with clear grade conditions ('C or better'). The six missingCoursesSample entries (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are co-requisite lab sections and GER category placeholders rather than genuinely broken chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the co-requisite lab codes (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A) and the ENGR 211 professional-skills course in the catalog, or annotate them as co-reqs, to clear the dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the data; only a term-by-term plan of study is provided. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, especially for an ABET-context engineering degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of ABET-style student outcomes (the ABET (1)-(7) outcomes) and program educational objectives on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0; summedTermCredits 120 vs statedTotalCredits 120) and terms are evenly loaded (12-17 credits). Gen-ed (ENGL 101/102, GER history/humanities/social-science/senior-seminar), the math-science core, and the engineering block are all transparently represented.
dimensions.suggestion: Because the 'major' is largely a flexible elective block, add a note on how many credits must be engineering vs lab vs free electives so students can see the true core-to-elective ratio.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page is well structured: eight clearly labeled terms with credits, named required courses, and legible 'Select one of the following' choice groups. A handful of rows are null-coded placeholders ('Social Science GER', 'General Engineering Elective') but they are understandable in context.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the repeated 'General Engineering Elective' rows footnoted eligibility lists (which subject prefixes/levels qualify) so the placeholder rows are actionable.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: ENGR 330 (Applications of Microcontrollers and IoT devices) and the ENGR 400 multidisciplinary capstone are current and applied, but the degree's identity rests almost entirely on unspecified electives, so currency depends on what those electives happen to be. There is no guaranteed exposure to data/computational methods, controls, or sustainability in the required core.
dimensions.suggestion: Move ENGR 301 (Engineering Applications of Data Science) into the required core so every graduate gains modern computational/data skills regardless of elective choices.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sample course descriptions (e.g. CHEM 121, MATH 211, PHYS 121, ENGR 400, ENGR 330) are specific and informative, naming concrete topics and deliverables rather than placeholder text; none are thin.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with grade conditions ('C or better') and sensible chains (PHYS 121 requires PHYS 111/111A and MATH 111). The only issues are co-requisite lab codes (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A) that read as dangling because they are not separately cataloged.

missingTopics.topic: Depth in computing/programming beyond a single intro course
missingTopics.rationale: The plan requires only one of CS 101/106/115 and then relies on generic electives; a modern engineer needs more than one semester of computation, yet nothing beyond ENGR 330 is guaranteed.

missingTopics.topic: Engineering statistics and data analysis applied to design
missingTopics.rationale: MATH 333 provides theory but the core never guarantees applied data analysis; ENGR 301 exists in the catalog but is only an elective here.
No feedback-control or systems course is required, a notable gap for a 'general' engineering degree that should expose students to dynamic-system thinking.
The required core (chemistry, physics, math, capstone) contains no explicit sustainability or lifecycle-analysis content expected of contemporary engineering programs.

emergingTrends.rationale: With ENGR 330 (IoT/microcontrollers) already required, a required data-science course (ENGR 301) would let students apply ML to sensor/IoT data, matching where general engineering practice is heading.
The ENGR 400 capstone would be strengthened by pairing it with simulation/CAE electives so students design against virtual models, now standard in industry.
ENGR 330 gives an entry point; positioning a required cyber-physical-systems thread would align the flexible upper division with embedded/edge-computing growth.
A general-engineering identity is a natural home for cross-cutting energy-transition and sustainable-design content that employers increasingly expect.

topSuggestions: Replace part of the generic 300/400-level 'General Engineering Elective' wall with 2-3 named required courses (e.g. ENGR 301 Data Science, a controls/systems course) to create real upper-division progression.
Publish explicit ABET-style student outcomes and program educational objectives on the catalog page.
Add a required computational/data-science course (ENGR 301) so all graduates gain modern data skills.
Clear dangling references by cataloging the co-req labs (CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A) and ENGR 211.
Footnote which subject prefixes/levels satisfy each 'General Engineering Elective' slot.

b-s-general-engineering-concentration-in.name: B.S. General Engineering - Concentration in Chemical Processing

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This concentration has a genuine chemical-engineering spine that progresses cleanly: CHE 101 (intro) -> CHE 201 (Material & Energy Balances) -> CHE 230 (Thermo I) -> CHE 260 (Fluid Flow) -> CHE 342 (Thermo II), supported by CHEM 125/126, CHEM 243/244A (Organic), CHEM 236/339 (Physical Chemistry), and MTEN 201 (materials), then wrapped with a manufacturing/quality layer (IE 331, IE 461, IE 459, ENGR 430) and the ENGR 400 capstone. The main gap is the absence of the two classic chemE completions -- reaction engineering/kinetics and separations/mass-transfer -- so the transport/thermo sequence ends without reactor or unit-operations design.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a reaction-engineering/kinetics course and a separations (mass-transfer/unit-operations) course to complete the CHE 201->230->260->342 sequence, which currently ends at thermodynamics without reactor or separations design.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and healthy for a technical track: deepestPrereqChain is 6 (e.g. CHEM 125/126 -> CHE 201/230 -> CHE 260 -> CHE 342; CHEM 236 -> CHEM 339) with explicit grade and co-requisite conditions. The seven missingCoursesSample entries (CHE 101, CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are the intro CHE seminar, co-req labs, and GER placeholders rather than broken technical chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog CHE 101, CHEM 125A, and ENGR 211 (or mark them as co-reqs/1-credit seminars) so the dangling references clear and the strong CHE chain reads cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data; only the plan of study is given. This is a real omission for a technical concentration whose graduates target the process industries.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish concentration-specific learning outcomes (e.g. ability to perform material/energy balances, analyze fluid/thermal systems, and apply statistical quality methods) alongside the general-engineering outcomes.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0; 120 summed vs 120 stated) with well-loaded 12-17 credit terms, and it transparently balances a heavy chemistry/CHE technical core against IE quality/economics courses and GER requirements.
dimensions.suggestion: State the credit split among the chemistry core, the CHE process core, and the IE manufacturing/quality layer so students see how 'chemical processing' is weighted against 'general engineering'.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is clean, labeled, and legible, with credits on nearly every row and clear substitution notes (e.g. 'or CS 106'). Only the usual GER category rows are null-coded.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line concentration overview describing the process-industries career target, since the course list alone must currently carry that message.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concentration aligns well with modern process/manufacturing practice: ENGR 301 (Engineering Applications of Data Science), IE 331/461 (applied statistics, product quality assurance), IE 459 (supply chain), ENGR 320 (Prototyping), and ENGR 430 (Quality & Reliability) reflect data-driven, quality-focused process engineering. It nonetheless lacks explicit process-control/automation and process-safety content that industry now treats as essential.
dimensions.suggestion: Add CHE 312 (Chemical Process Safety) and a process-control/instrumentation course to cover the safety and automation competencies process employers require.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions for the CHE and CHEM courses (CHE 201 material/energy balances, CHE 260 fluid flow, CHE 342 thermodynamics II, CHEM 236 physical chemistry) are specific and topic-rich; supporting IE and ENGR descriptions are likewise informative with no thin/placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are detailed and internally consistent, including multi-course conjunctions and co-requisites (e.g. CHE 342 requires CHE 230, MATH 211/213, CHEM 236; CHE 230 lists a MATH 211 co-requisite). Only the intro CHE 101 and lab co-reqs read as dangling because they are not separately cataloged.

missingTopics.topic: Reaction engineering and kinetics / reactor design
missingTopics.rationale: The CHE sequence covers balances, fluids, and thermodynamics but no kinetics/reactor course, the core design capstone of chemical processing.
Distillation/absorption/extraction unit operations are absent, leaving the transport thread (CHE 260 fluid flow) without its mass-transfer counterpart.
No control/automation course appears; modern process plants are defined by their control and instrumentation layer.
CHE 312 (Chemical Process Safety) exists in the catalog but is not in the plan; process safety is now a baseline expectation in the process industries.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-driven process optimization and digital plants
emergingTrends.rationale: ENGR 301 is already included; extending it toward process analytics, soft sensors, and model-based optimization matches the industry shift to digital/smart plants.
Green chemistry, electrification, and carbon-capture-ready process design are reshaping the field and are absent from the current chemistry/CHE core.
New Jersey's pharma/biotech corridor makes bioprocess unit operations a high-value addition to a chemical-processing track built on CHE 201/230/260.

emergingTrends.trend: Inherently safer and quantitative process safety
emergingTrends.rationale: Pairing CHE 312-style safety with data methods reflects the field's move toward quantitative, sensor-driven risk management.

topSuggestions: Complete the chemE core by adding reaction-engineering/kinetics and separations/mass-transfer courses to finish the CHE 201->230->260->342 sequence.
Add CHE 312 (Chemical Process Safety) and a process-control course to close the safety and automation gaps industry expects.
Publish explicit concentration-level learning outcomes alongside the general-engineering outcomes.
Catalog the dangling co-req/intro codes (CHE 101, CHEM 125A, ENGR 211) to clean the prerequisite references.
Extend ENGR 301 toward process analytics/optimization to position graduates for digital-plant roles.

b-s-general-engineering-concentration-in.name: B.S. General Engineering - Concentration in Engineering Innovation and Intellectual Property

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The concentration's identity -- ENGR 350 (Intellectual Property for Engineers), ENTR 210/330/440 (entrepreneurship, strategy, lean startup), IE 447 (Legal Aspects of Engineering), IE 463 (Invention and Entrepreneurship), and HIST 320 (Law and Evidence) -- is visible only in the referenced sampleCourses, not in the displayed plan of study. The parsed plan instead shows a chemistry/CHE/materials/IE sequence (CHEM 243/236/339, CHE 260/342, MTEN 201/305) that belongs to other concentrations, so what a reader sees does not cohere with an innovation/IP theme.
dimensions.suggestion: Republish the plan of study so the innovation/IP courses (ENGR 350, ENTR 210/330/440, IE 447/463) form the visible required thread rather than a chemical-processing sequence that appears mismatched to the concentration name.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Where technical courses appear the chains are sensible (deepestPrereqChain 6, e.g. CHEM 126 -> CHE 230/CHEM 236 -> CHE 342), and the innovation courses appropriately carry light prerequisites (ENTR 210/440 have none; ENTR 330 requires ENTR 210). However eight codes are missing (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, GER 300, MTEN 101, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A), and the truncated plan makes the overall structure hard to verify.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog the missing prerequisite/co-req codes (notably ENGR 350's own prerequisite path and MTEN 101) and restore the full plan so the prerequisite graph can be validated end to end.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are stated; the data provides only a (truncated) plan of study. This is a real omission for a concentration whose value proposition is commercialization/IP competency.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes such as 'evaluate patentability and freedom-to-operate' and 'develop and validate a technology business model' so the concentration's distinctive skills are documented.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: creditReconcileDelta is -49 with only summedTermCredits 71 against a stated 120, because only five terms were parsed and the sequence begins mid-program at '2nd Semester'. Balance across gen-ed, technical core, and the innovation/IP block therefore cannot be verified from the data -- a data-consistency caveat as much as a design finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing first-year and any dropped terms so the plan sums to 120 credits and the innovation/IP vs technical-core balance becomes transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page as captured is incomplete: it starts at '2nd Semester', shows only five requirement groups, and displays a course sequence that does not match the concentration title. A prospective student could not follow a coherent four-year path from this page.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the page so all eight terms render in order from the first semester, and confirm the displayed courses correspond to Engineering Innovation & IP rather than to chemical processing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concentration's actual course set is highly current for a commercialization track: ENGR 350 (IP for Engineers), ENTR 210/330/440 (entrepreneurship, strategy, lean startup), IE 463 (Invention & Entrepreneurship), IE 447 (Legal Aspects), and ENGR 301 (Data Science) map directly to how engineers bring technology to market today.
dimensions.suggestion: Foreground ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) and a design-thinking course so the innovation pipeline (ideation -> IP -> venture funding) is complete and visibly modern.

descriptionQuality.justification: The referenced course descriptions are specific and informative -- ENGR 350 (patents/IP realization), ENTR 210/440 (opportunity recognition, lean-startup customer discovery), IE 463 (creativity through product design), and HIST 320 (evidence in the legal system) each convey concrete content with no thin text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clearly stated where present and internally consistent (ENGR 350 lists FED 101 or MET 103; ENTR 330 requires ENTR 410 or 210). Clarity is nonetheless weakened by the eight dangling codes and by the truncated plan, which leaves the intended prerequisite sequencing partly unverifiable.

missingTopics.rationale: ENGR 350 covers IP fundamentals but the plan lacks explicit tech-transfer/licensing and commercialization-agreement content that turns a patent into revenue.
The pipeline jumps from IP to entrepreneurship (ENTR 210/440) without a structured front-end innovation/ideation method course.
IE 463 addresses invention and product design, but ongoing product-lifecycle and roadmap management is absent.

missingTopics.topic: Engineering finance and venture economics depth
missingTopics.rationale: ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) exists in the catalog yet is not surfaced in the plan; financial modeling of a venture is core to the concentration's promise.

emergingTrends.trend: AI-assisted IP analytics and patent search
emergingTrends.rationale: As ENGR 350 teaches IP, generative-AI patent search, prior-art analytics, and drafting tools are rapidly changing IP practice and would modernize the concentration.

emergingTrends.trend: Lean/agile startup methods and evidence-based venture building
emergingTrends.rationale: ENTR 440 (Lean Startup Accelerator) already anchors this; deepening customer-discovery and metrics-driven venture building matches current startup practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Venture financing and alternative capital models
emergingTrends.rationale: Surfacing ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) aligns the track with today's crowdfunding, SAFE/venture, and grant-funding landscape.
ESG-oriented and mission-driven venture design is a growing expectation that pairs naturally with the ENTR strategy sequence.

topSuggestions: Fix the data/page so the full eight-term plan renders and the displayed courses match the Innovation & IP theme rather than a chemical-processing sequence (creditReconcileDelta -49 signals the plan is truncated).
Make ENGR 350, ENTR 210/330/440, and IE 447/463 the visible required thread of the plan of study.
Publish explicit innovation/IP learning outcomes (patentability analysis, business-model validation).
Add ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) and a design-thinking course to complete the ideation-to-funding pipeline.
Catalog the missing prerequisite codes (ENGR 350's path, MTEN 101, co-req labs) to clear dangling references.

b-s-general-engineering-concentration-in.name: B.S. General Engineering - Concentration in Materials Manufacturing Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: There is a title-to-content mismatch: for a 'Materials Manufacturing Systems' concentration the plan is dominated by innovation/business/legal courses -- ENTR 210/330/440, ENGR 350 (IP), IE 447 (Legal Aspects), IE 463, IE 492 (Engineering Management), MGMT 290 (Business Law), HIST 320 (Law and Evidence), PHIL 310/334 -- while the materials-science spine is thin. The plan carries mechanics (MECH 234/237), CAD (ME 430), circuits (ECE 231), and ENGR 330 (IoT), but no dedicated materials-science course (MTEN 201 is only referenced/missing as MTEN 101) and no manufacturing-processes course, so the sequence does not build materials or manufacturing depth term over term.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a materials-science foundation (e.g. MTEN 201 or CHE 375 Structure/Properties/Processing) and a manufacturing-processes course, and trim the heavy legal/entrepreneurship load, so the curriculum actually progresses through materials and manufacturing systems.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The technical prerequisites that exist are reasonable (deepestPrereqChain 6; MECH 234 -> MECH 237; ECE 231 with PHYS 122/MATH 112), but eight codes are missing (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, GER 300, MTEN 101, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) -- and the missing MTEN 101 is significant because it is the materials-science entry point the concentration should be built on.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog MTEN 101/201 and the co-req/prof-skills codes (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211/312, PHYS 111A/121A) so the materials-science prerequisite chain is present and the dangling references clear.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data; only the plan of study is given. This is a real gap for a concentration that should certify materials-selection and manufacturing competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes (e.g. 'select materials from structure-property-processing relationships' and 'plan and control a manufacturing process') to define the concentration's technical claims.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0; 120 vs 120) and terms are well loaded, so numerically it is balanced. However the mix is skewed: a large share of upper-division credits goes to entrepreneurship/legal/management courses rather than materials or manufacturing technical content, which is imbalanced for the stated concentration.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebalance upper-division credits toward materials/manufacturing technical courses (characterization, processing, metrology) relative to the current heavy business/legal elective load.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is complete, clearly labeled, and legible, with credits on nearly every row; only standard GER category rows are null-coded. As a page it reads cleanly even though its content raises coherence questions.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a concentration overview stating the materials/manufacturing career target so readers can reconcile the many business/legal courses with the concentration's stated purpose.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Some modern manufacturing elements are present -- ME 430 (CAD), IE 455 (Robotics & PLCs), ENGR 330 (IoT), and ENGR 301 (Data Science) -- but for a materials-manufacturing track the plan omits materials characterization (MTEN 305), advanced/additive manufacturing (ENGR 425 exists in the catalog), and metrology (ENGR 360), so it under-represents current materials-manufacturing practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Bring MTEN 305 (Materials Characterization), ENGR 425 (Advanced Manufacturing Rotation), and ENGR 360 (GD&T & Applied Metrology) into the plan to reflect contemporary manufacturing systems.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative across the mix -- MECH 237 (stress/deformation), ME 430 (geometric modeling/optimization), ENGR 360 (GD&T and applied metrology), and ENTR 210 (opportunity recognition, business-model validation) all carry concrete content with no thin/placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear and consistent where present (MECH 237 requires MECH 234 and MATH 112/PHYS; ENGR 360 requires FED 101 or MET 103), but the many junior/senior business/legal courses list no prerequisites and the materials entry course (MTEN 101) is missing, which weakens the overall prerequisite picture for a technical concentration.

missingTopics.rationale: No dedicated materials-science course sits in the plan; MTEN 201/CHE 375 exist in the catalog but MTEN 101 is only a dangling reference, leaving the concentration without its core subject.
The plan has CAD (ME 430) and robotics/PLC (IE 455) but no processes course covering how materials are actually shaped into products.
MTEN 305 (Materials Characterization Methods) exists in the catalog yet is absent from the plan, so students never systematically learn to measure material structure/properties.
ENGR 425 (Advanced Manufacturing Rotation) is available but not in the plan; additive/advanced manufacturing is now central to materials-manufacturing systems.

emergingTrends.rationale: ENGR 330 (IoT) and IE 455 (PLCs) provide a base to build toward connected, sensor-instrumented smart factories, the direction of modern manufacturing systems.

emergingTrends.trend: Materials informatics and ML for materials
emergingTrends.rationale: With ENGR 301 (Data Science) present, data-driven materials discovery and process optimization would modernize a materials concentration.
Recyclability, remanufacturing, and lifecycle-aware materials selection are fast-growing expectations absent from the current plan.
ENGR 360 (GD&T & Applied Metrology) would connect design intent to inspection, reflecting the shift to model-based definition and digital metrology.

topSuggestions: Add a materials-science foundation (MTEN 201 / CHE 375) plus a manufacturing-processes course so the concentration actually builds materials and manufacturing depth.
Bring MTEN 305 (characterization), ENGR 425 (advanced manufacturing), and ENGR 360 (metrology) into the plan and reduce the heavy legal/entrepreneurship load.
Publish explicit materials/manufacturing learning outcomes.
Catalog MTEN 101/201 and the co-req/prof-skills codes to clear the dangling references.
Leverage ENGR 301 toward materials informatics / manufacturing analytics for currency.

b-s-general-engineering-concentration-in.name: B.S. General Engineering -Concentration in Mechatronics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most coherent of the concentrations: it integrates the three mechatronics domains in a well-ordered sequence -- mechanical (MECH 234 -> MECH 236 -> ME 231 -> ME 305 System Dynamics; MECH 237), electrical (ECE 231 Circuits), and computing (CS 100, MATH 337 Linear Algebra, ENGR 301 Data Science) -- and lands them in applied courses ENGR 330 (Microcontrollers/IoT), IE 455 (Robotics & PLCs), ENGR 423 (Drone Science), ENGR 424 (Robotics Science), CS 440 (Computer Vision), and the ENGR 400 design project. The main gap is the lack of a dedicated feedback-control course; ME 305 (system dynamics) and ENGR 424 (motion control) touch it but no explicit controls course completes the loop.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a dedicated feedback-control course (ECE 431 Introduction to Feedback Control Systems exists in the catalog) after ME 305 so the mechatronics loop -- sense, model, control, actuate -- is fully closed.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and well-formed (deepestPrereqChain 6, e.g. MECH 234 -> MECH 236/237 -> ME 231 -> ME 305 with MATH 222) and the robotics courses gate sensibly (ENGR 424 requires a programming course). Six codes are missing (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) but these are co-req labs, prof-skills, and GER placeholders, not broken technical chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog the co-req/prof-skills codes (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211/312, PHYS 111A/121A) to clear the dangling references; the technical chains themselves need no change.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are provided in the data, only the plan of study. This is a real omission for an integrative concentration whose graduates should demonstrate multi-domain system design.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes such as 'design and integrate mechanical, electrical, and embedded subsystems' and 'implement sensing and motion control for a robotic system'.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0; 120 vs 120) with well-loaded 12-17 credit terms and a strong, transparent technical-core-to-gen-ed ratio; the mechanical/electrical/computing split is visibly balanced.
dimensions.suggestion: State the credit weighting of the mechanical vs electrical vs computing threads so students can see the mechatronics balance at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is largely clean and legible with credits, but a parsing artifact leaked a stray row titled '684 B.S. General Engineering - Concentration in Quality and Reliability Engineering' into Third Year 1st Semester, which would confuse a reader. Otherwise labeling is clear.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the stray cross-reference row ('684 ... Quality and Reliability Engineering') that bled into Third Year 1st Semester so the mechatronics plan is not mixed with another concentration's heading.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concentration is strongly current: CS 440 (Computer Vision), ENGR 423 (Drone Science), ENGR 424 (Robotics Science), ENGR 330 (IoT/microcontrollers), ME 441 (CAE simulation), and ENGR 301 (Data Science) reflect where mechatronics and robotics practice is today.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a robot software-framework element (e.g. ROS) and hardware-in-the-loop simulation within ENGR 424/ME 441 so students use the toolchains industry expects.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and technical -- ME 305 (dynamic-system modeling and simulation), ME 231 (kinematic analysis of mechanisms), CS 440 (computer-vision methods), ENGR 424 (robot sensing, motion control, motion planning) -- with no thin or placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously and are internally consistent, including multi-course conjunctions (ME 305 requires MATH 222, MECH 236, ME 231; ME 231 requires MATH 213 and a C in MECH 234). Only the co-req lab/prof-skills codes read as dangling.

missingTopics.rationale: ME 305 (system dynamics) and ENGR 424 (motion control) approach control, but no standalone controls course (ECE 431 exists in the catalog) closes the sense-model-control-actuate loop.
ENGR 330 (microcontrollers/IoT) is an entry point, but real-time firmware, RTOS, and detailed sensor/actuator interfacing are not developed further.
Robotics perception (CS 440) and system dynamics assume signal fundamentals that no dedicated DSP course provides.
ENGR 400 is a general multidisciplinary capstone; a mechatronics-specific integration project would consolidate the mechanical/electrical/computing threads.

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 440 (Computer Vision) and ENGR 424 (Robotics Science) position the track for ML-based perception, SLAM, and autonomous behavior, the frontier of robotics.
Adding ROS/Gazebo tooling around ENGR 424 aligns graduates with the de-facto industry robotics software stack.
Collaborative robotics and safe human-robot interaction are a fast-growing industrial segment the IE 455/ENGR 424 base could extend into.
ME 441 (CAE simulation) is a natural springboard to digital-twin and HIL workflows now standard in mechatronic development.

topSuggestions: Add a dedicated feedback-control course (ECE 431) after ME 305 to close the mechatronics control loop.
Remove the stray '684 Quality and Reliability' row that leaked into the Third Year plan.
Publish explicit multi-domain mechatronics learning outcomes.
Introduce ROS/hardware-in-the-loop tooling within ENGR 424/ME 441 for industry-standard toolchains.
Catalog the co-req/prof-skills codes (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211/312, PHYS 111A/121A) to clear dangling references.

b-s-general-engineering-concentration-in.name: B.S. General Engineering - Concentration in Quality and Reliability Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The displayed plan of study is essentially a duplicate of the mechatronics sequence (ECE 231, MECH 234/236/237, ME 231/305/430/441, ENGR 423/424, CS 440, IE 455) and does not contain the courses that define a Quality & Reliability concentration. The signature courses -- ENGR 430 (Engineering for Quality and Reliability), IE 461 (Product Quality Assurance), IE 331 (Applied Statistical Methods), MATH 244 (Probability Theory), ENGR 360 (GD&T & Applied Metrology), and ENGR 425 (Advanced Manufacturing Rotation) -- appear only in the referenced sampleCourses, so the plan a reader sees is incoherent with the concentration's purpose.
dimensions.suggestion: Republish the plan so the quality/reliability core (ENGR 430, IE 461, IE 331/MATH 244, ENGR 360, ENGR 425) is the visible required sequence instead of the mechatronics course list currently shown.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The referenced quality/reliability courses form a sound statistical chain (MATH 244/IE 331 -> IE 461 Product Quality Assurance; deepestPrereqChain 6 overall), and grade conditions are explicit. However seven codes are missing (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A), and because the displayed plan is the mechatronics one, the intended reliability prerequisite path is not actually shown to students.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the statistics->quality chain (MATH 244/IE 331 -> IE 461) in the plan and catalog the missing co-req codes (including PHYS 231A) so the reliability prerequisite path is both present and complete.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data; only a (mismatched) plan of study is given. This is a real omission for a concentration whose whole point is measurable quality and reliability competency.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes such as 'apply statistical process control and design of experiments' and 'perform reliability/life-data analysis and FMEA'.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Numerically the plan reconciles (creditReconcileDelta 0; 120 vs 120) with well-loaded terms, but because the displayed sequence is the mechatronics plan, the credit distribution shown is not the quality/reliability distribution -- the balance cannot be trusted to represent this concentration.
dimensions.suggestion: After restoring the correct courses, state the credit split among the statistics core, the quality/reliability courses, and the supporting engineering electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page displays the wrong sequence: it shows the mechatronics/robotics courses rather than the Quality & Reliability courses, so a prospective student cannot see the concentration's actual requirements. This is a data-consistency problem, not merely cosmetic, since the defining course ENGR 430 never appears in the plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the page so the Quality & Reliability plan of study renders (with ENGR 430 and the IE quality courses) rather than a copy of the mechatronics sequence.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concentration's actual referenced course set is well aligned with current practice: IE 331 (Applied Statistical Methods), IE 461 (Product Quality Assurance), ENGR 430 (Quality & Reliability), ENGR 360 (GD&T & Metrology), IE 355 (Human Factors), ENGR 425 (Advanced Manufacturing), and ENGR 301 (Data Science) cover statistical quality, reliability, and metrology as industry expects.
dimensions.suggestion: Add explicit design-of-experiments and Six Sigma content, and connect ENGR 301 to predictive/condition-based maintenance so the reliability track reflects data-driven quality practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: The referenced quality/reliability descriptions are specific and informative -- IE 461 (defect prevention, process capability, inspection data), ENGR 430 (principles and tools of quality and reliability), IE 331 (statistical inference, hypothesis testing), ENGR 360 (GD&T and applied metrology) -- with no thin text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clear and internally consistent for the quality/statistics courses (IE 461 requires IE 331; IE 331 requires MATH 211), but clarity for the concentration is undercut because those courses are not in the displayed plan and seven codes (including PHYS 231A) are dangling.

missingTopics.rationale: The referenced statistics courses (IE 331, MATH 244) provide inference, but structured DOE -- central to quality engineering -- is not explicitly covered.
ENGR 430 introduces reliability broadly, yet dedicated life-data analysis and failure-mode/fault-tree methods are not surfaced as their own competency.

missingTopics.topic: Statistical process control and Six Sigma
missingTopics.rationale: IE 461 touches process capability, but a structured SPC/Six Sigma sequence -- the working language of quality organizations -- is missing from the visible plan.
ENGR 360 (GD&T & Applied Metrology) exists in the catalog but is not in the displayed plan, leaving measurement-system competency out of the concentration a reader sees.

emergingTrends.rationale: With ENGR 301 (Data Science) available, ML-based defect prediction and condition-based maintenance are reshaping quality engineering and fit this track directly.
Sensor-instrumented, real-time quality monitoring (Quality 4.0) extends IE 461/ENGR 360 toward where manufacturing quality is heading.
CS 440 (Computer Vision) in the course pool is a natural bridge to automated visual inspection, a fast-growing quality-assurance method.
Reliability prediction from simulation/digital-twin models is emerging practice that would modernize the ENGR 430 reliability content.

topSuggestions: Correct the plan of study so the Quality & Reliability core (ENGR 430, IE 461, IE 331/MATH 244, ENGR 360, ENGR 425) is shown instead of the duplicated mechatronics sequence.
Add explicit design-of-experiments, SPC/Six Sigma, and reliability (Weibull/FMEA/FTA) content.
Publish explicit quality/reliability learning outcomes.
Connect ENGR 301 (Data Science) to predictive/condition-based maintenance for data-driven quality.
Catalog the missing co-req codes (including PHYS 231A) and surface the statistics->quality prerequisite chain.

b-s-in-applied-physics-488.name: B.S. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is a model of ordered progression: an introductory physics and calculus spine (PHYS 111 -> 121 -> 234 with MATH 111 -> 112 -> 213 -> 222 -> 328) feeds the upper-level theory core (PHYS 335 Thermodynamics, PHYS 430 Classical Mechanics, PHYS 432/433 Electromagnetism I/II, PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics) and a coherent astrophysics thread (PHYS 320 -> 321 -> 322 Astronomy and Astrophysics, PHYS 420 Special and 421 General Relativity), capped by PHYS 450 Advanced Physics Lab. Prerequisite depths climb smoothly to 7 with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Label the astrophysics-heavy upper-level courses (PHYS 320/321/322, 420/421) as a named concentration so students can see it as one of several possible applied-physics paths.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=7 is appropriate for a four-year physics degree, and the 7 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: CHEM 125A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 225, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) are co-requisite chemistry and physics labs, a one-credit statistics survey, and general-education placeholders rather than physics-core breaks, so the core chain is intact.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or code mappings for the physics/chemistry labs (PHYS 111A/121A/231A, CHEM 125A) and MATH 225, and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the provided data; only the term plan and per-course descriptions are present. For a full B.S. this absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes stating what a graduate can model, measure, compute, and analyze in physics.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta=0.0 -- summedTermCredits (120.0) matches statedTotalCredits (120.0) exactly across eight well-balanced ~15-credit terms with a clear mix of general-education, major, and elective slots. This is exemplary internal consistency.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the reconciliation and optionally add a credit-category summary (major versus general-education versus electives) so the balance is explicit at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan is complete and legible: eight terms labeled by year and semester, reconciled credits, and substantive course descriptions. It is lightly blemished by several 'or'-alternative rows with null codes and by general-education placeholders.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the 'or'-alternative course rows and the GER placeholders more explicitly so every line resolves to a concrete option.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The theory core (mechanics, electromagnetism, quantum, thermodynamics, optics, advanced lab) is solid and durable, but for an 'Applied' physics degree the required upper level is dominated by astronomy/astrophysics and relativity rather than industrial/applied physics; there is no required computational-physics, condensed-matter/materials, modern-electronics, or photonics/semiconductor course.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or require computational physics, condensed-matter/materials, and modern electronics/instrumentation to strengthen the degree's applied identity.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive across the physics and math spine (PHYS 430 Classical Mechanics I, PHYS 432 Electromagnetism I, PHYS 442 Introduction to Quantum Mechanics, PHYS 450 Advanced Physics Lab), with no thin or placeholder excerpts among sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear course logic and 'grade C or better' conditions, and the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=7) are co-requisite labs, a one-credit statistics survey, and GER placeholders rather than physics-course breaks, so the core chain is unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: The plan has MATH 328 mathematical methods and PHYS 114 data reduction but no dedicated computational-physics/simulation course, now standard in applied physics.
Solid-state physics (PHYS 446) exists in the catalog but is not required; condensed-matter/materials is central to applied and industrial physics and is absent from the required plan.
PHYS 450 Advanced Physics Lab touches electronics, but there is no standalone electronics/instrumentation course despite its importance for applied-physics employment.
PHYS 335 is introductory; a fuller statistical-mechanics course would support condensed-matter and modern-physics topics.
PHYS 418 Fundamentals of Optical Imaging is a start, but applied optics/photonics (e.g., OPSE 402 high-power laser applications) is a growth-area gap in the required plan.

emergingTrends.rationale: PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics is the natural springboard; quantum computing and sensing are the fastest-growing applied-physics areas.
Pairing PHYS 114 data reduction and MATH 328 with modern simulation and machine-learning methods reflects current physics practice.
Extending PHYS 418/446 toward device physics addresses strong employer demand.
Applied physics is increasingly tied to energy technologies, a direction not represented in the required plan.
Given the astrophysics thread (PHYS 320-322), connecting it to modern detectors, instrumentation, and data pipelines matches contemporary practice.

topSuggestions: Strengthen the 'applied' identity by adding or requiring computational physics, condensed-matter/materials, and modern electronics/instrumentation -- the required upper level is currently astrophysics/relativity-heavy.
Add quantum-information/quantum-technology content building on PHYS 442 to position graduates for the fastest-growing applied-physics sector.
Name the astrophysics upper-level courses (PHYS 320/321/322, 420/421) as a formal concentration so alternative applied tracks are visible.
Publish program-level learning outcomes for the degree.
Resolve the 7 dangling lab/GER/MATH-225 references so the otherwise fully reconciled 120-credit plan is complete.

b-s-in-applied-physics-b-s-in-mathematic.name: B.S. in Applied Physics & B.S. in Mathematical Sciences, Applied Mathematics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Within the shown terms the sequence builds logically -- an applied-math spine (MATH 222 -> MATH 331/337/340 -> MATH 332/450 -> MATH 451 research) runs alongside a life-science spine (BIOL 201 -> CHEM 243/244 -> CHEM 473 Biochemistry) and a mathematical-biology thread (MATH 374 Stochastic and Discrete Models in Biology, MATH 430 Analytical and Computational Neuroscience). However, the parsed plan starts at 'Summer / Second Year' with the entire first year missing, and the biology/organic-chemistry/computational-neuroscience emphasis shown does not match the 'Applied Physics' half of the program title (only introductory PHYS 111/114/121 appear), so the combined structure is hard to read as titled.
dimensions.suggestion: Present both degrees' full four-year sequences and reconcile the title with the actual content -- either surface the upper-level physics core or relabel the record to reflect its applied-math/mathematical-biology (pre-health) emphasis.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=7 is reasonable, but there are 8 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: BIOL 202, BIOL 206, CHEM 125A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) -- co-requisite biology and chemistry labs, physics labs, and general-education placeholders. These are support/lab items rather than core breaks, but they are more numerous than in peer programs.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or code mappings for the paired labs (BIOL 202/206, CHEM 125A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; only a partial term plan and course descriptions are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes, especially clarifying how the combined applied-physics and applied-mathematics degrees serve the accelerated pre-health pathway this record is filed under.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta=-38.0 (summedTermCredits 71.0 against statedTotalCredits 109.0). Because this is a combined-degree record whose first year is not shown, the gap is largely a data-consistency artifact rather than a designed shortfall, but as presented the plan does not reconcile and the full credit distribution cannot be read.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the complete combined plan so credits sum to the stated 109 (and confirm 109 is the correct total for a genuine dual B.S.).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The record is hard to read end to end: the plan begins at Summer of Year 2 with the first year absent, the title conflicts with the biology/chemistry-heavy content shown, and general-education placeholders plus 8 dangling references clutter the listing.
dimensions.suggestion: Show the complete term-by-term plan for both degrees with all years labeled, and align the program title with the courses actually required.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The courses shown are current and strong for a computational and mathematical-biology / pre-health track: MATH 374 Stochastic and Discrete Models in Biology, MATH 430 Analytical and Computational Neuroscience, MATH 450/451 applied-mathematics research, and CHEM 473 Biochemistry connect quantitative methods to modern life-science problems. The gap is on the physics side -- upper-level mechanics, electromagnetism, and quantum mechanics are absent from the shown plan despite the 'Applied Physics' title.
dimensions.suggestion: If the applied-physics degree is genuine, surface its upper-level physics core (mechanics, E&M, quantum); if the record is really the accelerated math-biology pre-health track, relabel it accordingly.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are substantive across the shown spine (CHEM 473 Biochemistry, MATH 374 Stochastic and Discrete Models in Biology, MATH 430 Analytical and Computational Neuroscience, MATH 450 Methods of Applied Mathematics), with no thin or placeholder excerpts among sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites carry clear 'grade C or better' conditions, but the 8 dangling references (co-requisite BIOL/CHEM/PHYS labs and GER 200/300 placeholders) reduce internal consistency more than in peer programs, so clarity is only adequate.

missingTopics.rationale: For an 'Applied Physics' degree, classical mechanics, electromagnetism, and quantum mechanics (PHYS 430/432/442) do not appear in the shown plan; only introductory PHYS 111/114/121 are present.

missingTopics.topic: Statistics and biostatistics for the life sciences
missingTopics.rationale: The plan includes MATH 333 Probability and Statistics but no regression or biostatistics course (e.g., MATH 344 Regression Analysis) despite its heavy biology emphasis.
With BIOL 201 cell/molecular biology, CHEM 473 Biochemistry, and MATH 374 molecular-evolution modeling present, a bioinformatics/sequence-analysis course is a natural but absent complement.
CS 100 Roadmap to Computing is the only computing course, yet MATH 340, 430, and 450 are computation-heavy; a fuller scientific-programming/numerical-computing course is needed.
MATH 450/451 applied-methods research is present, but an explicit optimization or dynamical-systems/chaos course (e.g., MATH 473) would round out the applied-mathematics core.

emergingTrends.rationale: MATH 430/431 already anchor computational neuroscience; extending toward neural networks and systems dynamics matches a fast-growing research area.

emergingTrends.trend: Machine learning in biology and medicine
emergingTrends.rationale: The math-biology and pre-health framing calls for machine learning applied to genomics and medical imaging, none of which appears in the shown plan.
MATH 374 already covers infectious-disease modeling; recent demand makes a dedicated epidemiological-modeling course timely.
Bridging the physics and applied-math halves via physics-informed machine learning or simulation would unify the dual degree around a modern method.
With heavy modeling coursework (MATH 340/430/450), training in reproducible workflows and version control is increasingly expected of quantitative graduates.

topSuggestions: Reconcile the program record: the title says 'Applied Physics & Applied Mathematics' but the shown plan and department are a biology/chemistry-heavy pre-health, mathematical-biology track with no upper-level physics -- relabel it or restore the missing physics and first-year content.
Publish the complete combined plan so credits reconcile to the stated 109 (only 71 are currently shown, a -38 delta).
Resolve the 8 dangling references (BIOL 202/206, CHEM 125A, GER 200/300, PHYS 111A/121A/231A).
Add a statistics/biostatistics course and a bioinformatics or scientific-computing course to match the biology emphasis.
Publish program-level learning outcomes clarifying the dual-degree and pre-health goals.

b-s-in-architecture-167.name: B.S. in Architecture

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The first two years mirror the professional B.Arch and are well-ordered (studios ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296, Construction I/II ARCH 223/224, History I/II ARCH 210/211), but coherence weakens sharply in the upper years: Studio V (ARCH 395) is the last required studio and years three and four dissolve into undifferentiated 'ARCH Elective' and 'Free Elective' rows with no required Structures, ECS, or professional-practice core and no culminating studio.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required upper-level integrative studio and pull core technical courses (Structures, ECS) into the required sequence rather than leaving the entire junior/senior experience elective-driven.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is internally consistent but shallow (deepestPrereqChain 4), ending at Studio V (ARCH 395) because the deeper studio spine is not required here. All 3 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A) are gen-ed placeholders and a physics-lab co-requisite, not genuine architecture breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the PHYS 102A lab and GER 200/300 placeholder references, and if deeper studios are intended, restore the prerequisite chain into the upper years.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the provided data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. This absence is a real catalog finding, and it matters more here because a pre-professional degree must clearly signal how it differs from the accredited B.Arch.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes that distinguish this non-licensure pre-professional B.S. from the B.Arch (e.g., breadth, path to an accredited M.Arch).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and terms are evenly loaded at 12-16 credits. The distribution is transparent, but it is heavily weighted toward open electives in the upper years (multiple 'ARCH Elective' plus 'Free Elective' slots per term), which is flexible but under-structured for a design degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Cap or define the free-elective share and convert some open ARCH-elective slots into declared technology/history-theory requirements so the upper-year balance is intentional.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is clean, with labeled terms and per-term credits, and the shared ARCH descriptions are mostly substantive. The main weakness is the large number of unresolved placeholder rows ('ARCH Elective', 'Free Elective', GER lines with null credits) that make the upper years read as a template rather than a plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate each elective slot with its category and credit rule (e.g., '300+ ARCH Technology elective, 3 cr') so placeholder rows are self-explanatory.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As a non-licensure pre-professional degree the flexibility is defensible, but making Structures, ECS, and Professional Practice optional means graduates can complete the major without core technical or practice currency, and the only required computing course is the dated CS 104. Digital tools (BIM ARCH 337, fabrication) and sustainability remain elective.
dimensions.suggestion: Embed a minimum required technology core (one structures, one environmental-systems, one BIM/computational course) so every graduate leaves with current technical fluency.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are specific for the foundational courses (ARCH 210/211 History, ARCH 223/224 Construction give real topic content), but the continuation studios are thin or boilerplate-contaminated (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195. ARCH 2**...', ARCH 296, ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and the MATH entries are terse topic lists.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit, but the retained studio prereqs carry ambiguous compound logic (ARCH 395 'ARCH 224, and (ARCH 296 or ARCH 264), and ARCH 211 or (ARCH 381 and ARCH 382)' mixes precedence), and ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252' is unparenthesized. Dangling GER/PHYS references are minor.

missingTopics.topic: Required building technology core (structures and environmental systems)
missingTopics.rationale: Structures I/II (ARCH 303/304) and ECS I/II (ARCH 309/314) exist in the catalog but are not required here, so a B.S. graduate can bypass load paths and building energy entirely, a serious gap for any architecture degree.
With only the dated CS 104 required, BIM (ARCH 337) and digital fabrication (ARCH 301/335) remain optional; parametric/Grasshopper modeling that employers expect is absent from the required core.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability and environmental performance as a required thread
missingTopics.rationale: Sustainability appears only in electives such as ARCH 571 Sustainable City; a degree in architecture should require at least one environmental-performance course given climate-driven practice.
Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) are not required in this plan, leaving graduates without exposure to project delivery, contracts, or firm operations.
No course addresses code compliance or universal design; even a non-licensure degree should introduce accessibility and life-safety principles.

emergingTrends.rationale: ML-driven form-finding and text-to-image tools are entering studio pedagogy; the Tools and Techniques sequence (ARCH 110/156) should incorporate them and displace the aging CS 104 computing requirement.
Digital electives (ARCH 301/335/337) show the capacity exists; making a parametric/BIM course required would align this flexible degree with contemporary practice.
ARCH 571 Sustainable City demonstrates faculty strength that could anchor a required environmental-performance thread for climate-era practice.
Courses like ARCH 361 Adaptive Paradigms point to responsive/smart-building interest that an elective-heavy degree could formalize as a concentration.
Landscape and Urbanism (ARCH 324) plus urban electives could extend toward GIS/data-driven site analysis, an increasingly expected skill.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes that clearly position this pre-professional B.S. relative to the accredited B.Arch/M.Arch path.

Introduce a required upper-level integrative studio and a required building-technology core (structures, environmental systems, BIM/computational) instead of leaving juniors and seniors on open electives.
Require at least one sustainability/environmental-performance course (e.g., building ECS or ARCH 571) and modernize the dated CS 104 toward computational design.
Convert generic 'ARCH Elective'/'Free Elective' slots into declared categories with credit rules to give the upper-year plan intentional structure.
Resolve the 3 dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A) and clean the thin/boilerplate continuation-studio descriptions.

b-s-in-architecture-and-m-b-a-in-managem.name: B.S. in Architecture and M.B.A. in Management of Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate architecture spine visible in the course list is well ordered: the studio sequence ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395 carries a clean depth chain (deepestPrereqChain 4), paired with History of Architecture I->II (ARCH 210 -> 211) and Construction I->II (ARCH 223 -> 224). However, only 2 of the plan's requirement groups parsed (both Fourth Year), so term-over-term progression and, critically, the point where the M.B.A. Management-of-Technology graduate courses interleave with the B.S. are not visible; the combined-degree bridge is asserted rather than shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the full five-year plan of study showing exactly which term the graduate MGMT/FIN/ACCT courses begin and how they build on the undergraduate architecture core, so the B.S.-to-M.B.A. transition is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: With a deepest prerequisite chain of 4 the undergraduate graph is sensibly shallow and clean (e.g., ARCH 224 requires ARCH 223; ARCH 395 requires ARCH 224 plus a studio and history course). The high missingCourseCount of 26 is dominated by graduate M.B.A./M.O.T. courses that live outside the undergraduate catalog: missingCoursesSample lists ACCT 615, FIN 516/600/618/624, MGMT 620/625/630, HRM 601, MATH 661, and ARCH 650/651/652, plus GER 200/300 category placeholders. These are expected dangling references for a combined degree, not broken undergraduate chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the graduate course codes (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 620, etc.) to the graduate catalog or mark them as graduate-level so they no longer register as dangling undergraduate references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a partial plan and a course list are present. For a combined B.S./M.B.A. this absence is especially consequential because the whole value proposition is an integrated design-plus-technology-management competency that should be stated explicitly.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes that name the integrated architecture + technology-management capabilities graduates should demonstrate (design fluency, project/technology management, finance for the built environment).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is -93 (summedTermCredits 27 vs. statedTotalCredits 120), but this is a data-extraction caveat rather than a real imbalance: only the two Fourth-Year requirement groups were parsed, so 93 credits of earlier terms and the graduate load are simply absent from the summed figure. The visible portion is entirely ARCH/free electives and a credit-less GER placeholder, giving no view of the gen-ed/core/elective split.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full plan of study so the summed credits reconcile to the stated 120 (plus the graduate credits) and the general-education, architecture-core, and management split becomes transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: As parsed, the catalog page shows only the Fourth-Year first and second semesters, populated largely by generic 'ARCH Elective', 'ARCH Elective (Technology)', and 'Free Elective' rows with one credit-less 'History and Humanities GER 300+ level' item. There is no program description, no first three years, and no view of the graduate portion, so a prospective student cannot read the shape of the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program overview paragraph and the missing First through Third Year terms, and give the placeholder elective and GER rows explicit credit values.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The graduate M.B.A. in Management of Technology is itself a currency-relevant pairing (technology strategy, finance, operations for design professionals). The undergraduate courses shown, however, lean traditional: the computing requirement is the dated CS 104 'Computer Programming and Graphics Problems,' and nothing visible addresses building energy performance, sustainability, or real-estate/development finance that would naturally bridge architecture and the M.O.T. content.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a real-estate-development-and-finance elective and a building-performance/sustainability course as the explicit link between the architecture core and the Management-of-Technology graduate work.

descriptionQuality.justification: Undergraduate course descriptions are generally present and specific (ARCH 223 names wood, steel, masonry and concrete assemblies; ARCH 210 defines a Paleolithic-to-industrialization scope). Quality is uneven, though: several read as thin extraction fragments (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195. ARCH 2**. Architecture Elective...'; ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and none of the graduate M.B.A./M.O.T. courses that define this degree are described at all.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Undergraduate prerequisites are mostly stated clearly with sensible logic (ARCH 224 requires ARCH 223; ARCH 295 uses an explicit alternative-group form). Clarity is reduced by 26 dangling references (the graduate ACCT/FIN/MGMT/HRM courses and GER placeholders) and by a couple of ambiguous operator precedence strings such as ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252'.

missingTopics.topic: Building environmental systems and energy performance
missingTopics.rationale: Construction I-II (ARCH 223/224) cover materials and enclosure but no visible course addresses HVAC, lighting, or energy modeling, a core competency for any accredited architecture path.
The plan shows construction assemblies but no statics or structures course, leaving a gap between ARCH 223/224 and the technical judgment a design professional needs.

missingTopics.topic: Real estate development and project finance
missingTopics.rationale: This is the natural bridge between the architecture B.S. and the M.O.T. finance courses (FIN 600/618), yet no development-finance course links the two halves of the degree.
The only computing course is the dated CS 104; contemporary practice expects parametric modeling (Grasshopper/Rhino) and BIM well beyond ARCH 156's introductory digital tools.

missingTopics.topic: Professional practice and construction project management
missingTopics.rationale: For a management-of-technology pairing, a course on AEC project delivery, contracts, and construction management would directly operationalize the M.B.A. content in a built-environment context.

emergingTrends.rationale: Net-zero and embodied-carbon mandates are reshaping practice; the technology-management focus is an ideal frame to teach the business case for low-carbon design, which the current construction courses omit.
Generative tools are entering schematic design and optioneering; upgrading the CS 104 requirement toward design computation would keep graduates current.
Technology-driven real estate (smart buildings, digital asset management) sits squarely at the architecture + management intersection this degree targets.

emergingTrends.trend: Building lifecycle BIM and digital twins
emergingTrends.rationale: Owners increasingly demand model-based lifecycle management; pairing BIM with the M.O.T. operations content would differentiate graduates.
The M.B.A. Management-of-Technology track positions graduates to launch AEC-tech ventures, a growing niche the curriculum could name explicitly.

topSuggestions: Publish the complete five-year plan of study (First through Third Year and the graduate terms) so the page reconciles to its stated 120 undergraduate credits and shows where the M.B.A. courses begin.
Add explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated architecture-plus-technology-management competency (none are currently present).
