But this minor is explicitly for non-YWCC majors, and the deepestPrereqChain is 7: the most advanced electives (IS 465 at depth 7 behind a statistics GUR, IS 421 at depth 5 behind IS 219 and IS 331) sit behind ladders a non-computing student cannot reach inside 15 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate a low-prerequisite path through the minor (starting from depth-0 courses such as IS 265, IS 117, IS 247) so non-majors can complete 15 credits without hitting depth-5-to-7 gatekept electives.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data. Non-majors adding this minor cannot see what business-and-information-systems competencies they will gain.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 learning outcomes framed for non-computing students (e.g., build a business web application, design a database, analyze systems requirements, manage IS-enabled business processes).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit transparency is partial: statedTotalCredits is 15.0 (a standard, clearly stated minor total, better than the null-credit minors in this college), but with requirementGroups=0 and a null creditReconcileDelta the split between any required core and electives is not shown, so balance within the 15 credits cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Break the 15 credits into a stated required core and elective allowance (e.g., 9 credits required + 6 elective) so students see how the minor is composed, not just its total.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The stated 15-credit total and clean references (missingCourseCount=0) aid clarity, and course descriptions/prerequisites are present, but the minor still lacks an overview and any requirement structure (requirementGroups=0), so it reads as a course list rather than a designed minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short minor overview and an explicit requirement block (required courses + 'choose N from' electives) to make the 15-credit structure legible.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The applied-IS content is current and employable for non-majors (IS 218 web apps, IS 322 mobile, IS 331 databases, IS 247/375 UX, IT 310 e-commerce, IT 120 networking, IT 340 system administration, IS 455 IS management). The gaps are the modern business-technology staples: data analytics/BI, cybersecurity fundamentals, cloud, and project management.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a business-analytics or cybersecurity-fundamentals elective to the menu so non-majors gain a currently in-demand skill alongside the web/database core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific across the sampled courses (e.g., IS 333 social network analysis, IT 265 game architecture and design), with informative content rather than placeholders. Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters and a few are shorter (IT 265 ~149), but the pool is well described overall.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and boolean structure (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and missingCourseCount is 0 with no dangling references, so the graph is internally consistent. The one caveat is that several attractive electives carry deep chains a non-major must plan around.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool covers building and managing systems (IS 218/331/455) but no analytics or business-intelligence course; data-driven decision making is now central to information-systems roles and is the clearest missing competency for non-majors.
Only IT 120 networking is present; a non-computing student building web, mobile, and database systems (IS 218/322/331) should have a security-fundamentals option, which the menu lacks.

IS 390 covers requirements and IS 455 covers IS management, but there is no project-management course (Agile/Scrum, scope, budgeting) that non-majors need to lead technology work in a business setting.

missingTopics.topic: Enterprise systems / ERP and cloud SaaS
missingTopics.rationale: E-commerce (IT 310) and system administration (IT 340) appear, but there is no exposure to enterprise/ERP or cloud SaaS platforms that dominate real business information systems.
With no analytics or visualization course, non-majors leave without the ability to turn business data into clear decisions, a skill employers increasingly expect from an IS minor.

emergingTrends.rationale: Much business IS work now happens on low-code platforms; a minor for non-majors is an ideal place to add low-code app building alongside the traditional IS 117/218 web sequence.
Generative-AI features and copilots are entering everyday business software; the minor should expose non-majors to using and evaluating AI-enabled business systems.
Business systems are overwhelmingly cloud/SaaS today, yet the minor's build and admin courses (IS 218/421, IT 340) are traditional; a cloud thread would modernize it.
Security and privacy responsibilities now reach every business function; a security-fundamentals option would make the minor markedly more relevant to non-computing majors.
IS 455 IS management and business processes can grow toward automation/RPA and digital-transformation practice, a fast-growing area for business professionals adding an IS credential.

topSuggestions: Publish an explicit requirement structure for the 15 credits (required IS core + curated electives); requirementGroups=0 means the minor currently shows only a course pool.
Designate a low-prerequisite path so non-computing majors can complete the minor without the depth-5-to-7 gatekept electives (IS 421, IS 465).
Add a business-analytics/BI course and a cybersecurity-fundamentals option, the most in-demand competencies missing from the menu.
Publish learning outcomes framed for non-majors, none of which appear in the data.
Separate the game/media electives (IT 265/266/286/366/380) from the default menu so the applied-business-IS focus is clear.

business-minor-711.name: Business Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so there is no captured required core or elective rule, despite a stated 15-credit total. The 59 referenced courses form a broad, undifferentiated pool spanning every business function (ACCT, ECON, FIN, HRM, MGMT, MRKT, MIS) plus computing and fintech courses (CS 113/114, FIN 306 blockchain, FIN 410 ML), so the path a minor student would follow cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Define and publish a coherent minor structure: a small required foundation (e.g., accounting plus economics plus one of marketing or management) and a curated elective list that maps to the stated 15 credits.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains on the undergraduate courses are well-formed with grade conditions (deepestPrereqChain 6), but the program has the highest dangling-reference count in this set: nine missing courses, six of which are graduate-level (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) that do not belong on an undergraduate minor page, plus GER 200/300 and MGMT 399. The graduate references suggest content pulled from an accelerated BS/MS pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the six 600-level graduate references from the undergraduate minor pool so the prerequisite graph reflects only courses a minor student can actually take.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. A minor's purpose is to certify a defined competency to non-majors, and no statement of that business competency is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the business minor certifies (e.g., financial literacy, understanding of core management and marketing functions, and basic business analytics).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: A total of 15 credits is stated (better than the fully null minors in this set), but summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null and no course-by-course distribution is captured, so how the 15 credits split between required and elective courses cannot be evaluated. Per the rubric the null summed/delta is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor.
dimensions.suggestion: List exactly which courses count toward the 15 credits and how many are required versus elective, so the credit distribution is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete: no plan of study, no distribution behind the 15-credit total, no description, a very broad course pool, and a thin placeholder course (BME 498, title 'ST:' with an empty description) plus six graduate-course references. A reader cannot tell what the business minor consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a description, the required/elective course list mapped to 15 credits, and remove the BME 498 placeholder and graduate-course references.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The pool contains current, marketable topics (FIN 306 Blockchain, FIN 410 Data Mining and Machine Learning, MGMT 416 AI for Business Decisions, FIN 320 financial analytics, MGMT 480 Managing Technology and Innovation), so a well-chosen minor is relevant. The score is held to adequate because, with no defined structure, there is no guarantee a minor student reaches these modern courses rather than stopping at introductory ones.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern elective track (analytics/AI plus a fintech option) so non-majors can deliberately acquire in-demand business-technology skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled descriptions are specific and substantive (e.g., FIN 306 on blockchain, MGMT 416 on AI/NLP in enterprises, OM 375 on decision modeling). However, the pool includes BME 498 with the placeholder title 'ST:' and an empty description, a genuine description-quality defect, and several titles are abbreviated, which pulls the score down from strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear with grade conditions and sensible alternatives (e.g., FIN 320's multi-part prerequisite). Internal consistency is reduced by nine dangling references - notably six graduate courses absent from the undergraduate catalog - which a minor page should not carry.

missingTopics.rationale: A business minor should guarantee a coherent base (e.g., accounting plus economics plus one of marketing/management), but no required core is captured in the data - the most important 'missing' element is a stated foundation every minor completes.
MGMT 290 Business Law appears in the pool, but there is no ethics course; a minor certifying business competence to non-majors should include baseline ethics/legal literacy.
FIN 315 is present, but there is no clearly scoped intro-finance-for-non-majors pathway that a non-business student could follow with minimal prerequisites.
MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics is in the pool but not surfaced as a recommended modern elective, which is exactly what non-majors most want from a business minor.
ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, and MRKT 620 are graduate courses mis-scoped into an undergraduate minor and should be removed from the listing.

emergingTrends.rationale: MGMT 416 is already in the pool and would make an attractive, accessible AI elective for minors from technical majors.
FIN 306 Blockchain Technology for Business offers non-majors exposure to a high-demand emerging area within a business minor.
Building a recommended path through MGMT 216 and OM 375 would give the minor a modern analytics emphasis valued across employers.
MRKT 338 New Product Design is present; adding a digital-marketing option would modernize the marketing side of the minor.
MGMT 480 Managing Technology and Innovation can anchor a contemporary technology-business track well suited to NJIT's engineering-heavy student body.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure (requirementGroups empty) mapped to the stated 15-credit total.
Remove the six graduate-level dangling references (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) that do not belong on an undergraduate minor page.
Fix the BME 498 placeholder (title 'ST:' with an empty description) appearing in the pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes for what the business minor certifies to non-majors.
Define a coherent core (accounting plus economics plus one of marketing/management) with a curated modern elective track (analytics/AI/fintech).

chemistry-minor-for-chemical-engineering.name: Chemistry Minor (for Chemical Engineering majors)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so there is no structured plan, required core or elective rule. The chemistry content within the pool is nonetheless coherent for a chemistry minor: it spans the classical subfields with CHEM 222 (Analytical), CHEM 243 (Organic), CHEM 336/340 (Physical), CHEM 360/361 (Inorganic), CHEM 473/474 (Biochemistry) and CHEM 412/480/491. A significant data caveat applies: this minor's captured course pool is byte-for-byte identical to the Materials Engineering Minor (both scraped from page 580) and both report the same metrics (83 referencedCourses, missingCourseCount 27, deepestPrereqChain 8), so the extraction appears to have attached one shared department course listing to two different minors rather than each minor's own requirements. That shared, undifferentiated pool means the actual minor progression cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the chemistry minor's own required/elective structure (e.g., a two-semester organic or physical-chemistry sequence plus curated electives) distinct from the materials minor, and remove non-chemistry courses that belong to the shared department listing.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The referenced chemistry chains are sensible (deepestPrereqChain 8, e.g., CHEM 125 to 126 to 243 with grade conditions), but prerequisite health is materially hurt by missingCourseCount 27 — an unusually high number of dangling references. The missingCoursesSample (ACCT 200, BME 300, CE 300, CHE 300, CHEM 300, CPT 300, ECE 200/300, EM 600, ENE 200, ENGR 200/211, ENTR 400, FIN 200) is dominated by generic 200-/300-level department placeholders and a graduate EM 600, confirming the pool is a broad scrape rather than a chemistry-minor graph.
dimensions.suggestion: Scope the pool to genuine chemistry-minor courses so the 27 dangling references (most of which are non-chemistry placeholders) fall away, then verify the remaining CHEM chains resolve cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a minor aimed specifically at chemical-engineering majors, the absence is meaningful because it should certify a defined depth of chemistry beyond what the ChemE degree already requires, and no such statement is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes describing the added chemistry depth the minor certifies (e.g., advanced organic/physical/analytical competency) and how it goes beyond the ChemE core.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than an imbalance finding, but as presented a ChemE major cannot see how many additional chemistry credits the minor requires beyond courses already in their degree.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and, importantly for a minor aimed at ChemE majors, specify which credits are incremental to the ChemE degree versus double-counted.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured page is incomplete: no plan of study, no credit total, no description, and a course pool identical to the Materials Engineering Minor, so a reader cannot tell the two minors apart or determine what the chemistry minor requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct chemistry-minor page with its own description, required/elective list and credit total, and fix the parsing so it no longer shares the materials minor's course listing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The chemistry courses in the pool cover the enduring, employable subfields (analytical, organic, physical, inorganic, biochemistry), which are relevant and add legitimate depth for a ChemE major. The score is held to adequate because the pool shows no computational-chemistry, cheminformatics or modern-instrumentation content, and its undifferentiated form gives no assurance a student reaches a modern, coherent subset.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a computational-chemistry or cheminformatics elective to the minor's curated list so it reflects current chemical-science practice rather than only classical wet-lab subfields.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled course descriptions are present and specific (26 of 26, none thin), with substantive chemistry text (e.g., CHEM 473 Biochemistry, CHEM 336/340 Physical Chemistry, CHEM 243 Organic); only excerpt length is truncated. The score is held below 5 because the pool includes many non-chemistry courses whose presence here is a scoping artifact rather than a description-quality issue.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: While individual CHEM prerequisites are stated with grade conditions, prerequisite clarity is undermined by 27 dangling references — largely non-chemistry 200-/300-level placeholders (BME 300, CE 300, ECE 300, EM 600, ENTR 400, FIN 200) — that make the minor's actual entry requirements ambiguous.

missingTopics.topic: A defined incremental chemistry core for ChemE majors
missingTopics.rationale: Because the minor targets ChemE students who already take CHEM 125/126/243, the key missing element is a stated set of additional courses (e.g., CHEM 336/340 Physical, CHEM 360 Inorganic, CHEM 473 Biochemistry) that add genuine new depth.
The pool's physical-chemistry courses (CHEM 336/340) are the natural anchor for a computational/quantum-chemistry elective, which is absent yet central to modern chemical research.
CHEM 222 Analytical appears, but there is no advanced instrumental-analysis/spectroscopy course to give the minor contemporary characterization skills.
No course connects chemistry to data analysis; a cheminformatics elective would modernize a minor otherwise built on classical subfields.

missingTopics.topic: Descoping non-chemistry courses from the pool
missingTopics.rationale: The listing carries BME, CE, ENGR, CS, ACCT/FIN and management placeholders shared with the materials minor; removing them is prerequisite to a coherent chemistry minor.

emergingTrends.rationale: Machine-learning-driven molecular property prediction is reshaping chemistry; building on CHEM 336/340 physical chemistry would position the minor for it.
A green-chemistry elective linked to CHEM 243 organic would align the minor with sustainability priorities relevant to ChemE careers.

emergingTrends.trend: Chemical data science and high-throughput experimentation
emergingTrends.rationale: Coupling analytical chemistry (CHEM 222) with data methods reflects the automation and data-rich direction of modern chemical laboratories.

emergingTrends.trend: Biochemistry and chemical biology at the interface
emergingTrends.rationale: CHEM 473/474 Biochemistry is a strong anchor to emphasize the chemistry-biology interface increasingly central to pharma and materials work.
An electrochemistry elective would connect the minor to battery/energy-storage chemistry, a fast-growing area adjacent to chemical engineering.

topSuggestions: Fix the data-extraction conflation: this minor's course pool is identical to the Materials Engineering Minor (both from page 580) and both report the same 27 dangling references — give each minor its own scraped requirements.
Publish the chemistry minor's actual required/elective structure and total credits (all currently null), specifying which credits are incremental to the ChemE degree.
Scope out the non-chemistry placeholder courses (BME/CE/ECE/ENGR/ACCT/FIN/EM 600) so the 27 dangling references resolve and the CHEM chains stand alone.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the added chemistry depth the minor certifies beyond the ChemE core.
Add a computational-chemistry or cheminformatics elective so the curated minor reflects current practice, not only classical subfields.

chemistry-minor-not-for-chemical-enginee.name: Chemistry Minor (not for Chemical Engineering majors)

The coherence caveat is a data/presentation gap rather than proof of a broken curriculum, but the pool is also diluted by clearly non-chemistry entries (CS 100/113, EPS 202, EVSC and FRSC courses) that blur what the minor actually requires.

The 6 missing references (missingCoursesSample: CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) are lab co-requisites and GER placeholders, not chemistry-course breaks.

For a minor a null delta is an expected data caveat rather than a defect, but here there is also no visible credit count or required-vs-elective split at all, so balance cannot be assessed by a prospective student.

CHEM 336's excerpt also ends in a stray '282 Jordan' page artifact, signaling scrape noise in the source.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the minor as a structured requirement block (core + electives with credits) instead of an undifferentiated eligible-course list, and clean the scrape artifacts.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The chemistry pool is current and employable: CHEM 437 Applications of Computational Chemistry and Molecular Modeling, CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials (semiconductors, nanoscale), CHEM 360/361 Environmental Chemistry and climate, CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis, and CHEM 473/474 Biochemistry together cover materials, computational, analytical, and life-science chemistry that industry and graduate programs value.
dimensions.suggestion: Foreground the computational (CHEM 437) and materials (CHEM 340) offerings as a recommended modern elective path so minors can signal in-demand skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are generally present and substantive across the pool (CHEM 231/235 Physical, 412 Inorganic, 473/474 Biochemistry, 480 Instrumental Analysis); the only weaknesses are minor scrape artifacts such as CHEM 336's truncated '282 Jordan' text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and coherent course logic; the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=6) are lab co-requisites and GER placeholders, so the chemistry chains themselves are unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: CHEM 360/361 cover environmental chemistry, but there is no dedicated green-chemistry course teaching atom economy, benign solvents, and sustainable synthesis that now shape industrial chemistry.

missingTopics.topic: Cheminformatics and machine learning for molecules

CHEM 340 Chemistry of Materials addresses crystalline solids and semiconductors but not polymers, which are among the largest sectors of the chemical industry.
CHEM 480 Instrumental Analysis is a methods lab, but there is no dedicated spectroscopic structure-determination course combining multinuclear NMR and mass-spectrometry reasoning.

missingTopics.topic: Chemical safety and laboratory quality practice
missingTopics.rationale: The pool has research (CHEM 491) but no formal treatment of chemical hygiene, risk assessment, or QA/QC that employers expect.

emergingTrends.trend: Electrochemistry and chemistry for energy (batteries, catalysis, CO2)
emergingTrends.rationale: CHEM 235 Physical Chemistry and CHEM 340 Materials could anchor content on energy storage and electrocatalysis central to the energy transition.
Building green-chemistry principles onto CHEM 360/361 positions completers for a decarbonizing chemical sector.
Self-driving labs and robotic synthesis are reshaping practice; even an elective module would signal currency.
Extending CHEM 473/474 Biochemistry toward biologics/bioprocess chemistry tracks a fast-growing pharma segment.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's actual required-vs-elective structure with a stated credit total instead of an undifferentiated eligible-course pool.
Remove the non-chemistry courses (CS, EPS, EVSC, FRSC) that appear to have leaked in from adjacent department pools so the page reads as a chemistry minor.
Add a program-level learning-outcomes statement describing the competencies the minor certifies.
Modernize the computational (CHEM 437) and materials (CHEM 340) offerings toward data-driven/ML methods and highlight them as a recommended path.
Introduce green/sustainable chemistry and a polymers option to close the clearest gaps against contemporary industrial chemistry.

communication-minor-446.name: Communication Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a minor the entry publishes no plan of study (requirementGroups=0), so the 55 referenced courses read as a menu rather than a sequence. The menu itself is strong and coherent: a foundational intro (COM 201 Introduction to Communication and Media) feeds a genuine ladder into COM 240 New Media Technologies and then COM 241 Sound Communication and COM 336 Multimedia Journalism, alongside broad strands in production (COM 303, COM 350 Digital Video, COM 353/354/355), journalism (COM 337 Photojournalism, COM 338 The Newsroom), analytics (COM 343 Social Media Analytics, COM 371 Social Network Analysis), and theory (COM 318). What is missing is a published required core or grouping to give the menu a defined shape.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a required foundation (e.g. COM 201 plus one theory course) and organize the remaining courses into labeled tracks (production, journalism, analytics) so the minor reads as a structured credential.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is genuinely laddered for a minor: deepestPrereqChain is 6, built on the COM 201 -> COM 240 -> COM 241 / COM 336 progression, so unlike the Communication majors' flat 300-level clusters the minor encodes real sequencing. missingCourseCount is 2 and both are requirement-category placeholders (GER 200, GER 300) rather than genuine dangling course references.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the COM 201 -> COM 240 ladder explicitly on the page so students see the intended order rather than a flat list.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data. A minor should state the specific competency it certifies (e.g. media production, media analysis, or professional communication), and none is published.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 learning outcomes and a short statement of what the Communication Minor is designed to add to a student's major.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, there are no requirementGroups, and creditReconcileDelta is null -- expected for a minor and noted as a data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but the page therefore shows no total credit requirement and no required-vs-elective split, so students cannot see the size or shape of the minor.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and how many credits are required core versus electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are rich and specific, but the entry is a flat inventory of 55 courses with no required core marked and no term/group structure, so the strong content is hard to navigate. The only unresolved rows are the GER 200/300 placeholders.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the courses into labeled tracks with a marked required core so the page communicates a path rather than a long list.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The minor is notably current: COM 343 Social Media Analytics and Management, COM 240 New Media Technologies (social platforms, AR/VR, algorithmic systems), COM 324 Podcast Practicum, COM 350 Digital Video Production, and COM 371 Social Network Analysis give students modern, marketable media skills well beyond a traditional communication survey. The main gap is a guaranteed media-law/ethics course, since law/ethics coverage is only implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate a media-law/ethics course within the minor's core so every student gets the professional-ethics grounding that complements the strong production and analytics offerings.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions across the COM inventory are consistently rich and specific -- COM 240 on emerging media technologies, COM 343 on social-media analytics and management, COM 324 on podcast production, COM 371 on network science in communication. None reads as thin or placeholder; only the GER rows lack course text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade gates and real course dependencies (COM 240 requires COM 201; COM 241 and COM 336 require COM 240; COM 371 requires STS 304/307), and the ladder is internally consistent. The only unresolved items are the GER 200/300 placeholders, and missingCourseCount is a low 2.

missingTopics.rationale: A minor this rich in production and analytics has no clearly required law/ethics course, yet legal and ethical literacy is a baseline expectation for communication credentials.
COM 370 Strategic Communication exists in the department but the sampled minor pool centers on journalism and production; PR/organizational messaging should be an explicit option.
The minor offers analytics courses (COM 343, COM 371) but no foundational methods course, leaving students without formal grounding in survey/content-analysis technique.
COM 346 Race, Gender, and Media addresses representation, but cross-cultural/global communication for diverse audiences is not distinctly represented.
A practical, high-demand competency (crisis messaging, risk framing) is absent from an otherwise applied, career-oriented menu.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI in media creation and workflow
emergingTrends.rationale: COM 240 New Media Technologies and COM 354 Designing Digital Media are the natural hosts for AI content-creation, editing, and disclosure practices now reshaping media work.

emergingTrends.trend: Social-media analytics and the creator economy
emergingTrends.rationale: COM 343 and COM 371 already give the minor a data-and-network spine to position students for platform-native, metrics-driven communication careers.
COM 336 Multimedia Journalism and COM 338 The Newsroom can incorporate source-verification and misinformation-detection skills central to contemporary media.
COM 324 Podcast Practicum and COM 241 Sound Communication give the minor a strong, modern audio strand to build on.
COM 240 already introduces AR/VR and algorithmic systems, an on-ramp to immersive storytelling as those formats mature.

topSuggestions: Publish a required core, a total-credit requirement, and learning outcomes so the minor reads as a defined credential rather than a 55-course list.
Organize the courses into labeled tracks (production, journalism, analytics) that surface the COM 201 -> COM 240 -> COM 241/336 ladder.
Designate a required media-law/ethics course to guarantee professional-ethics coverage.
Add strategic-communication/PR and a research-methods course as explicit options to round out the applied menu.
Introduce a generative-AI-in-media unit leveraging COM 240 and COM 354 to keep the minor current.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page provides no parsed plan of study or credit total, and because this variant and the CS-major and EE-major variants present identical course data and metrics in the source, a reader cannot determine the required courses, credits, or how this variant differs from the others.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the minor as an explicit required/elective list with credits, and state precisely which courses are waived or substituted for this audience so the variant is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course pool reflects classical computer-engineering practice (digital design, microprocessors, computer organization/architecture, operating systems) and reaches some current electives (advanced computer architecture ECE 451/452, communications ECE 422/425/481/489). For a general minor it usefully packages the ECE hardware core for students from other disciplines. The same modern gaps as the parent degree apply: no HDL/FPGA, embedded/RTOS, ML-hardware, or hardware-security content.
dimensions.suggestion: Offer at least one modern elective (HDL/FPGA design or an embedded/edge-AI course) within the minor so it certifies contemporary hardware skills, not only classical circuits and microprocessors.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and technically specific (ECE 353 on register-transfer logic and CPU design, ECE 251 on combinational/sequential logic, ECE 489 on Matlab/Simulink and Software-Defined-Radio labs, CS 332 on process management and concurrency). Only minor truncations and the ECE 429 'Pre or' fragment detract.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with grade and compound conditions (e.g., ECE 431 'ECE 333, or ECE 232 and MATH 337', ECE 495 '(ECE 294 and ECE 353) or (ECE 394 and ECE 353)'). Clarity is reduced by the deep (depth-8) chains that are heavy for a minor and by dangling lab-alternate references ECE 291/ECE 394 and project ECE 414.

missingTopics.rationale: The minor exposes a 36-course pool with an 8-deep prerequisite chain but no parsed required list, so a student cannot see the minimal path; a short, shallow required set (e.g., ECE 251, ECE 252, ECE 353) is the first gap to fill.

missingTopics.topic: Hardware description languages and FPGA design
missingTopics.rationale: The pool teaches gate/register-level design (ECE 251/353) but no Verilog/VHDL or FPGA flow; an HDL/FPGA elective would let minors design on programmable hardware.
ECE 252 microprocessors and ECE 395 lab cover bare-metal work, but no embedded/RTOS course is offered in the pool, a core modern CmpE skill.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning and hardware for AI
missingTopics.rationale: With computer-architecture electives (ECE 451/452) available, adding an ML or ML-accelerator elective would give the minor a hardware-for-AI dimension it currently lacks.
No security content accompanies the architecture and microprocessor courses; a hardware-security elective would round out the minor for connected-device work.

emergingTrends.rationale: Running ML on microcontrollers is a defining CmpE direction; an edge-AI elective built on ECE 252/353 would modernize the minor.
Standardization on RISC-V makes an open-core lab (building on ECE 353) a high-value, current addition.
Connected embedded devices dominate hiring; pairing microprocessors with a networking elective targets this trend even within a minor.
Side-channel and secure-boot concerns are now mainstream hardware topics the minor could introduce via a single elective.
Modern systems fuse accelerators on one chip; the advanced-architecture electives (ECE 451/452) are the natural vehicle to reflect this.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's explicit required-course list and electives rules; requirementGroups is 0 so no structure is available in the data.
Publish the minor's total credit requirement and per-course credits (currently no credit total appears in the data).
Define a compact required subset with a shallower prerequisite depth than the current depth-8 chains, which are heavy for a minor.
Resolve the dangling ECE 291/ECE 394 lab-alternate and ECE 414 references so electives point at real catalog entries.
Add at least one modern elective (HDL/FPGA or embedded/edge-AI) so the minor certifies contemporary hardware skills.

computer-science-minor-235.name: Computer Science Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed at all (requirementGroups 0), so the minor has no structured plan of study, no declared required core, and no elective rules in the data; the 41 referenced courses read as an undifferentiated pool that mixes the CS spine (CS 113/114/241/280/435) with advanced computational-mathematics courses (MATH 440/448/450/451/453) that look pulled in from an adjacent computational-math program. Without a stated structure the progression a minor student would follow cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Define and publish an explicit minor structure: a small required foundation (e.g., CS 113 + CS 114 + one of CS 241/CS 280) plus a curated elective list, and remove the unrelated advanced-MATH courses from the CS-minor pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Among the referenced courses the prerequisite chains are well-formed with a deepest chain of 7 and consistent grade conditions (e.g., CS 113 -> 114 -> 241 -> 435), and only 5 dangling references (GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A), all placeholders or labs. However, with no requirement groups the graph cannot be tied to an actual minor pathway, so structural health can only be judged on the loose course pool.
dimensions.suggestion: Once the required/elective structure is defined, verify that a minor-only student can satisfy the prerequisites of the chosen electives without hidden major-only chains (e.g., CS 435 requires CS 241 and CS 288).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a minor this is a particularly meaningful gap, because a minor's whole purpose is to certify a defined, coherent competency to non-majors, and there is no statement of what that competency is.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what a CS minor certifies (e.g., programming proficiency, data-structures fluency, ability to apply computing in the student's major field).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information at all: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null, and creditReconcileDelta is null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data program rather than evidence of a curriculum imbalance, but as presented a prospective minor cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they are distributed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement for the minor (typically 15-18 credits) and list exactly which courses count, so the credit load is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog entry as captured is essentially incomplete: no plan of study (requirementGroups empty), no credit total, no program description, and a course pool that appears to blend CS-minor courses with an unrelated computational-mathematics course set. A reader cannot tell what the minor consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description, the required/elective course list, and total credits, and fix the parsing so only genuine CS-minor courses appear.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The referenced course pool includes current, employable topics (CS 301 Data Science, CS 351 Cybersecurity, CS 388 Android Application Development, CS 331 Databases, CS 435 Algorithms), so a well-chosen minor could be quite marketable. The score is held to adequate because, absent a defined structure, there is no guarantee a minor student actually reaches these modern courses rather than stopping at intro programming.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern elective track for the minor (e.g., a data/AI option and a security option) so non-majors can deliberately pick up in-demand skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled course descriptions are present, specific, and substantive (CS 388 on the Android SDK and deployment, CS 301 on data-science models and hands-on skills, MATH 453 on state-of-the-art large-scale numerical algorithms), with none reading as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the referenced courses are stated clearly with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better', CS 388 'CS 288 with a grade C or better'). The main caveat is that some chains (CS 435 via CS 241/CS 288) may be deep for a minor, which the undefined structure does not clarify.

missingTopics.rationale: A CS minor should guarantee programming plus data structures (CS 113/CS 114 and one of CS 241/CS 280), but the data shows no required core, so the most important 'missing topic' is a stated foundation every minor completes.

missingTopics.topic: Applied AI / data literacy for non-majors
missingTopics.rationale: The pool contains CS 301 but no minor-friendly, low-prerequisite applied-AI or data-analytics option, which is exactly what students minoring from other disciplines most want; add an accessible data/AI elective.
CS 388 Android is present but there is no clearly surfaced web or full-stack option scoped for minors, a common motivation for taking a CS minor; curate a practical-development elective.
CS 351 appears in the pool but has deep prerequisites; a minor-accessible security-literacy course would let non-majors gain baseline secure-computing knowledge.

Advanced numerical/applied-math courses (MATH 440/448/450/451/453) appear in the CS-minor pool and are almost certainly mis-scoped from a computational-math program; removing them is as important as adding topics for a coherent minor.

emergingTrends.rationale: A CS minor is the ideal vehicle to give students from other majors practical fluency in building with and reasoning about AI tools; a minor-scoped applied-AI course would be highly attractive.
Minors increasingly serve students combining computing with a home discipline; defining domain-oriented elective tracks (computing for business, science, or design) would modernize the minor.
Data analytics is now a cross-cutting workplace expectation; surfacing an accessible analytics elective positions the minor for demand from non-majors.
As development shifts toward AI-assisted tooling, a minor emphasizing practical, tool-supported building lowers the barrier for non-majors to become productive.
With IS 350 (Computers, Society and Ethics) already in the pool, the minor could foreground responsible-computing literacy as a distinctive, broadly relevant outcome.

topSuggestions: Publish the actual minor requirements and total credits, which are entirely absent from the data (requirementGroups empty; statedTotalCredits, summed, and delta all null).
Define a coherent structure: a small required foundation (CS 113 + CS 114 + one of CS 241/CS 280) plus a curated, minor-accessible elective list.
Fix the course-pool scoping so unrelated advanced computational-mathematics courses (MATH 440/448/450/451/453) are removed from the CS-minor listing.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the CS minor certifies to non-majors.
Curate a modern, low-prerequisite elective track (applied AI/data and security literacy) so minor students can deliberately reach in-demand topics rather than stopping at intro programming.

data-analytics-minor-263.name: Data Analytics Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A real analytics spine exists in the referenced set: statistics (MATH 333, MATH 341, IE 331), data mining (CS 482), databases (CS 331, IS 331), web mining (IS 392), and social network analysis (IS 333). But that spine is surrounded by many courses with no analytics rationale (AD 112/150 art and design, COM 303/354/355 digital media, DD 284/321, ID 203, HRM 301, ENTR 210, IE 447 legal), and with requirementGroups=0 there is no visible required-core-then-elective ladder to guide selection.
dimensions.suggestion: Define an explicit required analytics core (a statistics course, a databases course, and CS 482 data mining) plus a curated elective list, and drop or clearly separate the art/design/media courses that do not serve a data-analytics outcome.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is well-formed with readable, grade-conditioned prerequisites, but the analytics capstone-level courses sit deep: CS 482 Data Mining is at depth 7 (it requires CS 301, which itself chains back through the CS 113/114 sequence), and the deepestPrereqChain is 7. missingCourseCount is 2 (YWCC 207, YWCC 307), both peripheral seminars. For a minor aimed partly at non-CS students, reaching the most valuable analytics courses demands a long prerequisite ladder.
dimensions.suggestion: Offer at least one applied-analytics course with a short prerequisite path (e.g., statistics -> a single programming/data course) so non-CS minors can reach a genuine analytics capstone without completing the depth-7 CS 301 chain, and catalog YWCC 207/307.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; the minor is represented only by a referenced course pool. Prospective students cannot see what an analytics-minor graduate should be able to do (e.g., clean data, model, visualize, interpret).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 learning outcomes for the minor covering data preparation, statistical modeling, mining/prediction, and communicating results with visualizations.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information to evaluate: statedTotalCredits is null, requirementGroups is 0, and creditReconcileDelta is null, so the required credit total and the required-versus-elective split are entirely absent. This is a credit-transparency gap rather than an internal inconsistency, but it leaves students unable to plan the minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the minor's total credit requirement and how it divides between a required analytics core and electives (a typical 15-18 credit structure) directly on the page.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: With requirementGroups=0 the page exposes no structure (no 'choose N from' lists, no core-versus-elective grouping) and no minor overview, and 2 course references dangle (YWCC 207/307). Course-level descriptions and prerequisites are present, but the minor as a unit is not legibly presented.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short minor overview and an explicit requirement block listing the required courses and the elective menu so the analytics focus is visible at a glance.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The classic analytics toolkit is represented (MATH 333/341 statistical inference, CS 482 data mining, IS 392 web mining, IS 333 social network analysis, database courses), which is solid but somewhat dated. Absent are the tools that now define the field: a Python/R data-science course, applied machine learning, data visualization/BI, and any data-engineering or big-data/cloud component.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an applied machine-learning course and a hands-on data-visualization/BI course (Python or R based) so the minor reflects how analytics is practiced today rather than stopping at classical statistics and mining.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific across the pool (e.g., CS 482 'covers the concepts and principles of advanced data mining systems design ... association and dependency analysis, classification, prediction, and clustering'; MATH 333 lists 'discrete and continuous distributions ... statistical inference for the mean and variance'). A few excerpts are shorter (IT 265 at ~149 characters), and all are truncated at ~220, but content is informative rather than placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and grade-conditioned (e.g., 'CS 301 with a grade C or better' for CS 482, 'MATH 244 ... or MATH 333' for MATH 341), and only 2 peripheral references (YWCC 207/307) dangle. The depth is high but the statements themselves are clear and internally consistent.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool relies on CS 482 and stats courses but shows no dedicated data-programming course; modern analytics is built on Python/R data-wrangling, and without it the minor teaches concepts students cannot readily implement.

CS 482 Data Mining is the only predictive-modeling course and it sits at depth 7; a lighter applied-ML course (supervised/unsupervised methods, model evaluation) is now core to any analytics credential and is absent.
No visualization, dashboarding, or BI course appears, yet communicating findings visually (Tableau/Power BI/matplotlib) is the analytics skill employers most consistently demand.

missingTopics.topic: Data engineering and SQL at scale / ETL
missingTopics.rationale: CS 331 and IS 331 cover database design, but there is no ETL, data-warehousing, or big-data (Spark/cloud) content, leaving the pipeline that feeds analytics unaddressed.
For a data-analytics credential there is no course on responsible data use, bias, or privacy; given IS 333 harvests social-media data, an ethics/privacy component is a notable and topical gap.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and LLMs for analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: LLM-assisted data analysis and natural-language querying are rapidly entering the analyst workflow; the minor should add exposure so graduates use current tooling rather than classical mining alone.
Analytics work is increasingly productionized; even at minor scope, awareness of versioned, reproducible pipelines would modernize the CS 482 / database thread.
Analytics now runs on Snowflake/BigQuery-style cloud warehouses; the database courses (CS 331/IS 331) could be extended toward cloud data platforms to match hiring demand.
Regulation and public scrutiny of data use are rising; a minor that already includes social-network and web mining (IS 333/IS 392) should position explicitly around ethical, privacy-aware analytics.
Batch statistics is giving way to streaming/real-time analytics in industry; positioning the mining and database courses toward streaming data would keep the minor current.

topSuggestions: Define an explicit required analytics core (statistics + databases + CS 482 data mining) and a curated elective list; the current referenced pool mixes analytics with unrelated art/design/media courses and shows no structure (requirementGroups=0).
Add a hands-on data-programming course (Python/R) and an applied machine-learning course so students can implement, not just study, analytics.
Add a data-visualization/BI course to cover the field's most-demanded communication skill, currently absent.
Publish the minor's total credits and required-versus-elective split (statedTotalCredits is null) and add a short overview so the minor is legible and plannable.
Provide a low-prerequisite route to a capstone analytics course so non-CS students can reach it without the depth-7 CS 301 chain.

design-of-the-user-experience-minor-264.name: Design of the User Experience Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A genuine UX core is present and well-chosen: IS 247 Designing the User Experience, IS 375 Discovering User Needs for UX, IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX, and IE 355 Human Factors, supported by design foundations (AD 112, AD 150, DD 284/321, ID 203). However the referenced pool is diluted by a large block of game-programming and technical courses (IT 265/266/286/360/366/382/383/466/487, IT 230 security, IT 330 forensics, CS 482 data mining) that are not UX, and with requirementGroups=0 there is no required-core-then-elective progression shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Anchor the minor on a required UX core (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448 plus IE 355) and curate the elective list toward interaction and visual design, moving the game-programming and forensics/security courses out of the default UX menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This program has the cleanest prerequisite data of the set: missingCourseCount is 0 with an empty missingCoursesSample, and prerequisites are readable and grade-conditioned. The deepestPrereqChain is 7, driven by advanced electives (IS 448 and IS 465 gated behind a statistics GUR, CS 482 behind CS 301), which is deep for a design-oriented audience even though the core UX courses (IS 247, IS 375 at depth 0) are accessible.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the accessible UX core at the front and clearly flag the depth-7 electives (IS 448, IS 465, CS 482) as advanced/optional so design-focused students are not blocked by statistics or CS-algorithms prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data. A UX minor in particular benefits from stated outcomes (research, design, evaluation) because employers read UX competencies literally, and none are published here.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 UX learning outcomes covering user research, interaction/visual design, prototyping, and usability evaluation.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: No credit information is available to assess balance: statedTotalCredits is null, requirementGroups is 0, and creditReconcileDelta is null. This is a credit-transparency gap for the minor rather than an internal inconsistency, but students cannot see the required total or how the UX core and electives divide.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the minor's credit total and the split between the required UX core and electives (a typical 15-18 credit structure).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The underlying data is clean (missingCourseCount=0, no dangling references, clear course descriptions and prerequisites), which aids readability, but at the minor level there is no overview and no requirement structure (requirementGroups=0), so the page presents a course pool rather than a designed minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief UX-minor overview and an explicit requirement block (required core + 'choose N from' electives) so the strong UX spine is visible rather than buried in a long alphabetical pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The UX content is current and marketable: IS 375 user-needs research, IS 448 usability and UX measurement, IE 355 human factors, and forward-looking interactive-media electives including IT 382/383 Game Design for XR and DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments. The visible gaps are the practitioner staples of accessibility, prototyping tools, and design systems rather than any dated emphasis.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an accessibility/inclusive-design course and a hands-on prototyping course (industry tools such as Figma) to cover the two competencies most requested of UX hires.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., IS 448 explains 'user experience research is the process of understanding why and how people use products and services ... usability refers to the ease of use and learnability'; IT 265 introduces 'core concepts and design methodologies integral to designing and developing games'). Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters and a couple are shorter, but none read as placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite clarity is the strongest in this set: missingCourseCount is 0 with no dangling references, and prerequisites are stated unambiguously with grade conditions (e.g., 'CS 113 or CS 115', 'IT 266 and either IT 114 or CS 114'). The graph is internally consistent end to end.

missingTopics.rationale: With IS 247/375/448 covering research and usability, the absence of any WCAG/accessibility course is the most conspicuous gap for a modern UX credential where accessibility is now a legal and hiring requirement.
The pool teaches research and evaluation but shows no course centered on hands-on interaction design and industry prototyping (Figma/Sketch), the concrete deliverable UX employers expect.
No IA or content-design course appears; structuring navigation, taxonomy, and content is a core UX competency distinct from the visual-design foundations (AD 112/150) present here.
Modern UX work is delivered through component-based design systems; the minor has visual foundations but nothing on systematized UI patterns bridging design and front-end.

IS 448 measures usability, but there is no dedicated A/B testing / UX analytics course, leaving the data-driven side of contemporary UX practice underweighted despite the available statistics courses.

emergingTrends.rationale: Generative UI and AI-assisted design are reshaping the discipline; a minor centered on IS 247/375/448 should introduce designing for and with AI to stay current.
IT 382/383 Game Design for XR and DD 321 already give the minor an XR foothold; extending these toward spatial/AR-VR UX positions graduates for a fast-growing interface frontier.
Voice and chat-based UX are now mainstream product surfaces not represented in the pool; adding conversational-design content would broaden the minor's relevance.
Regulation and platform policy are pushing accessibility and value-sensitive design to the center of UX; the minor should make inclusive, ethical design an explicit thread.
UX increasingly runs as an operational discipline embedded with engineering and product; exposure to design ops and handoff practice would make graduates immediately useful on teams.

topSuggestions: Anchor the minor on a required UX core (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448 plus IE 355) and curate electives toward design, moving the large game-programming and forensics/security block out of the default UX menu.
Add an accessibility/inclusive-design course and a hands-on prototyping course (Figma), the two most-requested UX competencies currently missing.
Publish the minor's credit total and required-versus-elective structure (statedTotalCredits is null; requirementGroups=0) plus a short UX overview.
Publish UX learning outcomes (research, design, prototyping, evaluation), none of which appear in the data.
Flag the depth-7 electives (IS 448, IS 465, CS 482) as advanced so design-focused students are not blocked by statistics/CS-algorithm prerequisites.

economics-minor-712.name: Economics Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), and, more tellingly, the captured pool contains only three genuine economics courses - ECON 201 Economics, ECON 265 Microeconomics, and ECON 266 Macroeconomics - amid a large set of finance, management, marketing, MIS, accounting, and computing courses that appear drawn from a shared MTSM pool. The economics content that should define the minor is thin and there is no structure to sequence it.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an economics-specific structure that names required economics courses and economics electives, and remove the non-economics courses so the minor reads as an economics program rather than a general business pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains on the referenced business/finance courses are well-formed (deepestPrereqChain 6), but the economics-specific graph is shallow - only ECON 265 carries a prerequisite (a calculus course), while ECON 201 and ECON 266 have none - reflecting how few economics courses are present. Eight references are dangling, six of them graduate courses (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) absent from the undergraduate catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the six graduate-level references and, as intermediate and field economics courses are added, ensure they form a sensible prerequisite ladder (principles -> intermediate theory -> econometrics/field courses).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For an economics minor the absence is compounded by the thin course offering: there is no statement of what economic-reasoning competency the minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating the economics competency the minor certifies (e.g., apply microeconomic and macroeconomic reasoning and interpret empirical economic evidence).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor, but a prospective student cannot see the credit requirement or how it is distributed, and only three economics courses are available to fill it.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credits and list which economics courses are required versus elective, expanding the economics offerings so the credit total can be met with genuine economics content.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete and misleading for an economics minor: no plan of study, no credits, no description, and a pool dominated by non-economics courses with only three actual ECON courses, plus a thin placeholder (BME 498, title 'ST:' with an empty description). A reader cannot tell what the economics minor consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean economics-minor page listing the required/elective economics courses and credits, and remove the placeholder and unrelated business/finance courses from the pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Judged as an economics credential, the offering is weak: it is limited to principles-level micro and macro with no econometrics, empirical/data economics, or applied field courses (labor, international, public, environmental), which are the core of contemporary economics practice. The broader pool has modern analytics courses (FIN 320, FIN 410, MGMT 416), but these are finance/management, not economics, and do not remedy the economics gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an econometrics/empirical-economics course and at least one applied field course so the minor reflects how economics is actually practiced today.

descriptionQuality.justification: The economics descriptions that are present are specific and standard (ECON 265 on price determination and market structure, ECON 266 on national-income determination and monetary/fiscal policy). The score is held down by the BME 498 placeholder ('ST:' with an empty description) in the pool and by the small number of genuine economics descriptions available to assess.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clear where stated (ECON 265 requires a calculus course with alternatives), but internal consistency is weakened by eight dangling references, six of them graduate courses absent from the undergraduate catalog, and by an economics prerequisite structure so thin it offers almost no laddering.

missingTopics.rationale: The single most important gap: a modern economics minor must include econometrics, but the pool has no econometrics course, and the nearest analytics course (FIN 320) is finance-scoped, not economics.
The pool offers only principles-level ECON 265/266 with no intermediate theory, so students cannot progress beyond introductory economics within the minor.
There is no labor, international, public, environmental, or health economics course, leaving the minor without any field breadth.
Central pillars of modern microeconomics are absent from the captured pool, limiting analytical depth.
No economics-scoped data-analysis course exists; although CS 114 and finance-analytics courses are in the pool, none teach empirical economic analysis.

emergingTrends.rationale: Combining econometrics with programming (leveraging CS 114 and analytics tooling) reflects the field's shift toward large-dataset empirical work and would modernize a currently principles-only minor.
The 'credibility revolution' has made causal inference central to economics; a course on natural experiments and causal methods would align the minor with current research practice.
A major growth area bridging economics and psychology is entirely absent from the pool and would be an attractive, accessible addition.

emergingTrends.trend: Economics of technology, AI, and digital platforms
emergingTrends.rationale: Fitting for NJIT's technology focus, an economics-of-innovation course (MGMT 480 could inspire one) would connect economics to the digital economy.
Growing policy and employer demand for sustainability analysis makes an environmental/energy economics course a timely addition.

topSuggestions: Add the defining economics courses the minor lacks - especially econometrics and intermediate micro/macro - since the captured pool contains only three principles-level ECON courses.
Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).
Remove the six graduate-level dangling references (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) and the BME 498 empty placeholder from the pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating the economics competency the minor certifies.
Broaden field coverage (behavioral, international/public, environmental economics) and add a computational-economics/econometrics-with-R course to modernize the minor.

electrical-engineering-minor-616.name: Electrical Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 20-course pool is a coherent electrical-engineering subset, the circuits/electronics core (ECE 231/232 circuits, ECE 251 digital design, ECE 252 microprocessors, ECE 271 electronics, ECE 294 lab) with EM fields (ECE 361), signals (ECE 333), and upper electives, plus CS 116/CS 332. But requirementGroups is 0 and no plan of study parsed, so which of these 20 courses make up the stated 17 credits, and in what order, cannot be verified from the data. As the general-audience variant it should define a self-contained 17-credit EE path for students from other disciplines.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the specific required and elective courses that constitute the 17-credit minor, with their intended sequence, rather than a flat course pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is explicit and consistent (e.g., ECE 232 'ECE 231', ECE 361 'ECE 231, MATH 213 and MATH 222', ECE 431 'ECE 333, or ECE 232 and MATH 337'), but its deepestPrereqChain of 8 is very deep for a 17-credit minor and reflects electives that carry long ladders from the major. The 2 missing references (missingCoursesSample: ECE 291, ECE 394) are lab-alternate placeholders.
dimensions.suggestion: Choose electives whose prerequisite depth fits a 17-credit minor, and resolve the ECE 291/ECE 394 lab-alternate references so no listed course points at an undefined prerequisite.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the provided data; only a course pool and descriptions are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a brief statement of the competencies the Electrical Engineering minor certifies (e.g., circuit analysis, electronics, and signals/systems) as explicit outcomes.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: A total is stated (statedTotalCredits 17.0), which is better than the sibling CmpE minors, but requirementGroups is 0 and creditReconcileDelta is null, so the 17 credits cannot be reconciled against a parsed set of required courses. This is a data-consistency caveat rather than a proven imbalance.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the course-by-course credit breakdown that sums to the stated 17 credits so the total is verifiable.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page states 17 credits but provides no parsed plan of study or required-course list, and because this variant and the for-Computer-Engineering-majors variant present identical course data and metrics, a reader cannot see the required courses or how this variant differs from the other.
dimensions.suggestion: Render the minor as an explicit 17-credit required/elective list and state precisely which courses are waived for this audience so the variant is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The pool is a current EE subset that reaches modern electives (renewable energy ECE 443, wireless/SDR ECE 489, data-network protocols ECE 429) on top of the classical circuits/electronics/fields core. For a general minor it usefully packages circuit analysis, electronics, and signals for non-EE majors. Because the required subset is not published, contemporary coverage cannot be guaranteed for every student who takes the minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Guarantee at least one modern elective slot (e.g., renewable energy ECE 443 or SDR/communications ECE 489) in the required structure so the minor reliably reflects current EE practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific (ECE 231 on circuit theory and system analysis, ECE 361 on electrostatics and Coulomb's/Gauss's laws, ECE 333 on signal models and system representations, ECE 489 on Matlab/Simulink and SDR labs). Only minor truncation and the ECE 429 'Pre or' fragment detract.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with compound and grade conditions (e.g., CS 116 'CS 115 with a grade C or better', ECE 375 '(ECE 271 and ECE 291) or (ECE 271 and ECE 294)', ECE 489 '(ECE 294 and ECE 421) or (ECE 394 and ECE 421)'). Clarity is reduced by depth-8 chains that are heavy for a 17-credit minor and by the dangling ECE 291/ECE 394 lab-alternate references.

missingTopics.rationale: The minor states 17 credits but no parsed required list, so a student cannot see the minimal path through the 20-course pool; specifying the required subset (e.g., ECE 231/232/271 plus one elective) is the first gap.
The pool has signals (ECE 333) and communications electives (ECE 481/489) but no ML; a machine-learning-for-signals elective would modernize the minor's analytical content.
Renewable energy (ECE 443) is available but no power-electronics/converter course; adding one would connect the minor to electrification and EV/grid work.
Microprocessors (ECE 252) sit in the pool without an embedded/IoT elective that ties low-power sensing and connectivity together for modern EE devices.
Device-level courses (ECE 374/375-adjacent) stop short of IC/VLSI design; a VLSI elective would align the minor with semiconductor-sector demand.

emergingTrends.rationale: ECE 443 already gives the minor a renewables entry; emphasizing it would make sustainability a visible strength.
ECE 489 (SDR) and ECE 429 (data-network protocols) position the minor for modern wireless; refreshing them toward 5G/6G keeps it current.
Chip reshoring is driving demand; a VLSI/device elective would let the minor tap this trend.
Data-driven signal processing is reshaping EE; the signals course (ECE 333) is a natural host for an ML module.
EV and grid electrification depend on converters absent from the pool, motivating a power-electronics elective.

topSuggestions: Publish the explicit 17-credit required and elective course list; requirementGroups is 0 so no structure is available in the data.
Publish the course-by-course breakdown that sums to the stated 17 credits (requirementGroups is 0 and the delta is null).
Select electives whose prerequisite depth suits a 17-credit minor rather than the current depth-8 chains.
Resolve the dangling ECE 291/ECE 394 lab-alternate references so listed courses have defined prerequisites.
Guarantee a modern elective slot (renewable energy ECE 443 or SDR ECE 489) so the minor reliably reflects current EE practice.

electronic-creative-writing-minor-447.name: Electronic Creative Writing Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a minor the entry publishes no plan of study (requirementGroups=0), so its 51 referenced courses read as a menu. The data quality is the best in this group -- missingCourseCount is 0, so every referenced course resolves to the catalog. The coherence weakness is identity: for an 'Electronic Creative Writing' minor, only a few courses are clearly creative-writing craft (COM 390 Electronic Writing Workshop, COM 369 Digital Poetry, COM 317 Advanced Composition, with LIT 230 Introduction to Literature as reading support), while the bulk of the pool is general communication, journalism, and media (COM 336, COM 337, COM 338, COM 343, COM 350, plus a large HIST elective bench), so the creative-writing core is thin relative to the media/journalism content.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a required creative-writing core (COM 390 and COM 369 plus COM 317) and separate the general communication/journalism and HIST courses into a clearly optional context list so the minor's writing identity is unmistakable.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well formed and, notably, clean: deepestPrereqChain is 6 (via the COM 201 -> COM 240 -> COM 241/COM 336 ladder) and missingCourseCount is 0, so there are no dangling references at all -- the strongest prerequisite-data health in this batch. The creative-writing anchors (COM 390, COM 369, COM 317) use standard ENGL 102 + GER gates that are consistent and sensible.
dimensions.suggestion: Because the graph is clean, simply mark which of the laddered courses count toward the creative-writing core versus context so students can navigate the sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data. For a specialized writing minor, an outcomes statement is needed to distinguish 'electronic creative writing' (screen-based, interactive, multimedia composition) from general composition or communication.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 learning outcomes emphasizing creative composition for electronic/interactive formats, and a short statement of the minor's distinctive focus.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, there are no requirementGroups, and creditReconcileDelta is null -- expected for a minor and noted as a data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but the page shows no total credit requirement and no required-vs-elective split, so the minor's size and shape are not visible.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the required-core-versus-elective breakdown for the minor.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are rich and, uniquely in this group, there are no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), so the inventory is clean. But the entry is a flat 51-course list with no marked creative-writing core, so a prospective student cannot tell which courses actually constitute the electronic-creative-writing focus.
dimensions.suggestion: Restructure the page to foreground the creative-writing core (COM 390, COM 369, COM 317, LIT 230) and demote the general media/journalism/HIST courses to a labeled elective/context section.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The digital-writing content is reasonably current: COM 390 Electronic Writing Workshop (creative expression for screen and performance), COM 369 Digital Poetry (algorithmic and computer-integrated poetics), and COM 240 New Media Technologies give students exposure to screen-based and computational composition. The gaps are the fast-moving craft forms -- interactive fiction, game/narrative writing, and generative-AI writing -- which the minor does not yet cover.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an interactive-fiction / narrative-design course and a generative-AI-writing unit so the 'electronic' side reflects current digital-writing practice rather than mainly digital poetry and media production.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific throughout, and the pool has no missing/dangling courses (missingCourseCount 0). The creative-writing anchors are well described -- COM 390 on creative expression in electronic formats for screen and performance, COM 369 on poets integrating computer technology and algorithmic programming, COM 317 on multi-tool persuasive composition. No description reads as thin.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit, consistent, and fully resolvable (missingCourseCount 0): the COM 201 -> COM 240 -> COM 241/COM 336 ladder is coherent and the creative-writing anchors use standard ENGL 102 + GER gates. The only ambiguity is that the page does not distinguish which prerequisites gate the creative-writing core versus the broader media electives.

missingTopics.topic: Foundational fiction and poetry writing workshops
missingTopics.rationale: The minor jumps to electronic/digital forms (COM 390, COM 369) without a traditional prose-fiction or poetry craft workshop to ground students in narrative and poetic technique.
For an 'electronic creative writing' minor, dedicated interactive-fiction/hypertext authoring (Twine, Ink) is conspicuously absent, though it is the defining genre of the field.
COM 390 gestures at writing for screen and performance, but there is no dedicated screenwriting/scriptwriting course to develop dramatic writing craft.
Interactive narrative for games is a major professional outlet for digital creative writers and is not represented in the pool.

missingTopics.topic: Editing, publishing, and the digital literary marketplace
missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses digital publishing, self-publishing platforms, or editing workflow -- practical competencies that turn creative writing into a career.

emergingTrends.rationale: COM 369 Digital Poetry already engages algorithmic text; extending to LLM-assisted composition, prompt-craft, and human-AI co-writing is the fastest-moving area of electronic creative writing.
The minor's 'electronic' promise is best fulfilled by interactive-fiction authoring, an accessible and expanding form for digital storytellers.
COM 240 New Media Technologies and COM 343 Social Media Analytics give context for narratives that span platforms and audiences.
COM 324 Podcast Practicum and COM 241 Sound Communication support a strong audio-fiction/narrative strand within electronic writing.
As immersive media matures, COM 240's AR/VR grounding positions the minor to add spatial and immersive storytelling.
