topSuggestions: Define and publish an explicit creative-writing core (COM 390, COM 369, COM 317, LIT 230) so the minor's writing identity stands out from the general communication/journalism pool it currently shares.
Publish learning outcomes and a total-credit requirement that distinguish electronic creative writing from general composition.
Add an interactive-fiction / narrative-design course -- the defining electronic-writing genre currently missing.
Introduce a generative-AI creative-writing unit building on COM 369's algorithmic-poetry foundation.
Add a foundational fiction/poetry workshop so students have traditional craft grounding before the electronic forms.

engineering-innovation-minor-686.name: Engineering Innovation Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan of study or requirement structure is published (requirementGroups is empty), and the 60-course pool is undifferentiated and identical to the Minor in Drones and Robotics -- both share the 686 id and the same course list. The innovation-relevant courses -- ENGR 350 (IP for Engineers), ENTR 210 (Entrepreneurship), ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture), ENTR 440 (Lean Startup), ENGR 320 (Prototyping), and ENGR 430 (Quality & Reliability) -- are present but embedded among unrelated courses (CET 435, BME 472, CHE 312), so no coherent innovation pathway is shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a defined innovation sequence (e.g. required ENGR 350 + ENTR 210 + ENTR 440, with electives such as ENTR 320, ENGR 320, IE 463) and remove the unrelated construction/biomedical/chemical-process courses from the minor's pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Only four codes are dangling (ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 231A), and the innovation courses appropriately carry light or no prerequisites (ENTR 210/440 none; ENTR 320 requires ENTR 410 or 210; ENGR 350 requires FED 101 or MET 103), which suits an accessible minor. The reported deepestPrereqChain of 8 is driven entirely by unrelated technical courses in the shared pool, not by the innovation courses, so it misrepresents this minor's depth.
dimensions.suggestion: Scope the pool to the innovation/entrepreneurship courses and document the (short) prerequisite expectations so students -- including non-engineering-adjacent majors -- can see the low barrier to entry.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes and no requirement structure are provided; the page offers neither objectives nor a plan of study. This is a substantial omission for a minor whose value is a defined innovation/commercialization skill set.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit minor-level outcomes (e.g. 'assess patentability and IP strategy' and 'develop and validate a technology business model') along with the required-credit structure.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit balance cannot be assessed: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null because no plan exists (a data-consistency caveat from how the minor was captured). A minor should specify its fixed credit total and required/elective split, none of which is present.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and its required-vs-elective breakdown (typical engineering minors are 15-18 credits).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: As captured, the page has no plan of study, no stated credits, and a course pool that is polluted with unrelated subjects and byte-for-byte identical to the Drones and Robotics minor -- a reader cannot determine what the Engineering Innovation Minor uniquely requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the minor its own curated course list distinct from the Drones and Robotics minor, with a required-credit summary at the top of the page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The innovation content that is present is current and coherent as a set: ENGR 350 (IP for Engineers), ENTR 210 (Entrepreneurship), ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture), ENTR 440 (Lean Startup Accelerator), ENGR 320 (Prototyping), and ENGR 301 (Data Science) map well to how engineers commercialize technology today. The relevance is real even though it must be extracted from a cluttered, shared pool.
dimensions.suggestion: Feature the ideation-to-venture pipeline (ENGR 320 prototyping -> ENGR 350 IP -> ENTR 210/440 venture building -> ENTR 320 financing) as the minor's spine and add a design-thinking front end.

descriptionQuality.justification: The innovation course descriptions are specific and informative -- ENGR 350 (patents/IP realization), ENTR 210 (opportunity recognition, business-model validation), ENTR 320 (estimating and securing funding, financial metrics), ENTR 440 (lean-startup customer discovery) -- with no thin or placeholder text; the pool's other descriptions are also complete.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites for the innovation courses are clear and consistent (ENTR 320/330 require ENTR 410 or 210; ENGR 350 requires FED 101 or MET 103), but because no plan defines which courses count toward the minor, students cannot see which prerequisite chains actually apply to them.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool jumps from prototyping (ENGR 320) and IP (ENGR 350) to venture building (ENTR 210/440) without a structured front-end ideation/design-thinking course.
ENGR 350 covers IP fundamentals, but the mechanics of licensing and moving technology IP to market are not represented as a defined competency.
IE 463 (Invention & Entrepreneurship) in the pool touches product creation, but ongoing product-lifecycle management is absent from any defined requirement.
ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) addresses venture funding, but broader engineering-economics/cost-analysis competency (e.g. IE 335) is not surfaced as part of the minor.

emergingTrends.trend: AI-assisted product design and generative design
emergingTrends.rationale: As the minor teaches prototyping (ENGR 320) and invention (IE 463), generative-AI design tools are rapidly changing how engineers create and iterate products.
ENGR 350 (IP for Engineers) would be modernized by prior-art search and patent analytics tools now central to IP practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Lean/agile venture building and alternative financing
emergingTrends.rationale: ENTR 440 (Lean Startup) and ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) position the minor for today's metrics-driven, crowdfunding/SAFE-era startup landscape.
ESG and mission-driven venture models are a growing expectation that pairs naturally with the ENTR entrepreneurship sequence.

topSuggestions: Publish a curated, dedicated innovation course list and sequence (required ENGR 350/ENTR 210/ENTR 440 plus scoped electives) and remove unrelated courses from the pool.
Separate this minor's course pool from the Drones and Robotics minor, which currently shares the identical 686 course list.
State the minor's total credit requirement and required-vs-elective split (currently null).
Publish explicit minor-level learning outcomes (patentability/IP strategy, business-model validation).
Add a design-thinking front end and surface ENTR 320 (Financing New Venture) to complete the ideation-to-funding pipeline.

environmental-engineering-minor-593.name: Environmental Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so there is no structured plan or required/elective rule. The pool does contain a coherent environmental spine — ENE 262 Introduction to Environmental Engineering, ENE 360/361/362/465, plus water-relevant CE courses (CE 320/321/322 fluids and water resources) and CE 360 Sustainable Materials — which fits an environmental minor. A data caveat applies: this minor's captured course pool is identical to the Geosystems Minor (both scraped from page 593) with the same metrics (49 referencedCourses, missingCourseCount 7, deepestPrereqChain 8), so the extraction appears to share one civil/environmental department listing across two distinct minors. Because that pool also carries structural, geotechnical and transportation CE courses shared with the Geosystems minor, the environmental-specific progression cannot be cleanly assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit environmental minor structure built around the ENE sequence (ENE 262 plus ENE 360/361/362) and water-resources CE courses, and remove the structural/geotechnical/transportation courses that belong to the shared listing.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains among the referenced courses are reasonable (deepestPrereqChain 8) with grade conditions, but missingCourseCount is 7 and the missingCoursesSample (ARCH 622, CE 341A, CE 431, CE 506, CE 602, CE 644, GER 300) includes several graduate-level (500-/600-) references and a lab section, indicating the pool reaches beyond an undergraduate environmental minor. With no requirement structure, the graph cannot be tied to an actual minor pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Restrict the pool to undergraduate environmental courses so the graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) drop out, then confirm the ENE chains resolve for non-CE-major students.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For an environmental minor this matters because it should certify a defined competency (e.g., water/wastewater or environmental-systems analysis) to students from other majors, and no such statement is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing what the environmental minor certifies (e.g., ability to analyze water/wastewater treatment, assess environmental impact, apply mass-balance methods to environmental systems).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are all null — a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than a proven imbalance. As presented a prospective student cannot see the minor's credit requirement or how required/elective credits distribute.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credit requirement and list exactly which environmental courses count as required versus elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured page is incomplete: no plan of study, no credit total, no description, and a course pool identical to the Geosystems Minor. A reader cannot distinguish the two minors or determine what the environmental minor requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct environmental-minor page with its own description, a curated environmental course list and a credit total, and fix the parser so it no longer shares the geosystems listing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The environmental content in the pool is relevant and employable (ENE 262 environmental fundamentals, the ENE 360-series, water-resources CE 321, sustainable materials CE 360), covering water and environmental-systems topics that remain in demand. The score is held to adequate because the pool shows no environmental-data-science/GIS, climate/air-quality, or emerging-contaminants content, and its shared, unstructured form gives no assurance a student reaches a coherent modern subset.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an environmental-data/GIS or emerging-contaminants elective to the curated minor so it reflects current environmental-engineering practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled courses have present, specific descriptions (32 of 34; 2 lack a description excerpt), with substantive text on environmental and civil topics (ENE 262 Introduction to Environmental Engineering, CE 321 Water Resources); only excerpt length is truncated. The score is held below 5 by the two missing descriptions and by the presence of non-environmental courses from the shared listing.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites carry grade conditions where present, but the pool mixes undergraduate and graduate (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) references among its 7 dangling links, and with no requirement structure the environmental minor's actual entry requirements are not clearly delimited.

missingTopics.topic: A defined environmental core scoped to the minor
missingTopics.rationale: The pool mixes ENE environmental courses with structural/geotechnical/transportation CE courses shared with Geosystems; the key missing element is a stated required core (ENE 262 plus ENE 360/361/362).
The water/environmental courses (ENE 360-series, CE 321) generate spatial and monitoring data, yet no GIS or environmental-data course appears to teach its analysis.
The pool is water- and land-focused (ENE water courses, CE 320/321); a minor covering environmental engineering should also address air-quality assessment and control.
No course addresses PFAS/micropollutants and advanced treatment, now central to environmental practice; an elective here would modernize the minor.
Graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) and structural/transportation courses shared with Geosystems should be removed so the minor reads as an undergraduate environmental pathway.

emergingTrends.trend: Climate adaptation and resilient water systems
emergingTrends.rationale: Extending ENE water courses and CE 321 toward flood/drought resilience aligns the minor with the dominant near-term direction of environmental practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Environmental data science and remote sensing
emergingTrends.rationale: Sensor networks and satellite data are transforming environmental monitoring; a data/GIS elective would position the minor for it.
Regulatory attention on micropollutants is driving demand for advanced-treatment expertise the minor could add to its ENE base.
Linking ENE 262 and CE 321, green-stormwater and nature-based design reflect the shift toward distributed, sustainable water management.
Recovering energy and nutrients from waste streams is a growing environmental-engineering theme a modern elective could introduce.

topSuggestions: Fix the data-extraction conflation: this minor's pool is identical to the Geosystems Minor (both from page 593) — give each minor its own scraped, curated course list.
Publish the environmental minor's required/elective structure and total credits (all currently null), built around the ENE 262 / ENE 360-series spine.
Restrict the pool to undergraduate environmental courses so the graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) and off-topic structural/transportation courses drop out.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the environmental competency the minor certifies to non-CE majors.
Add a modern elective (environmental data/GIS or emerging contaminants) so the minor reflects current practice.

environmental-science-and-policy-minor-3.name: Environmental Science and Policy Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups=0), so the minor appears as an eligible-course pool. The environmental-science half of that pool is coherent (EVSC 375 Environmental Biology, 385 Microbiology, 416 Toxicology, 484 Environmental Analysis; CHEM 360/361 environmental chemistry; BIOL 375 Conservation), but the 'and Policy' half is barely represented -- EPS 202 Society, Technology, and the Environment is essentially the only policy/society course visible, so the two named halves are unbalanced.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate the pool into an explicit science core plus a required policy/society component (e.g., add environmental law, economics, or governance courses) so the 'and Policy' in the title is actually delivered.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This minor has the cleanest prerequisite graph of the group: missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references) and a sensible deepestPrereqChain=6 (e.g., CHEM 126 -> 360 -> 361, and the BIOL 201/205 -> conservation/microbiology chains) with explicit grade conditions.
dimensions.suggestion: Preserve the clean graph while adding the policy courses; ensure any new policy electives carry only realistic humanities/writing prerequisites so accessibility is maintained.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only a course pool with descriptions is provided. For an interdisciplinary science-and-policy credential, stated outcomes are especially important to show the integration the title promises.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes describing what a completer can do across both halves (analyze environmental data/samples and reason about environmental policy and regulation).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: No credit requirement is stated (statedTotalCredits=null, summedTermCredits=null, creditReconcileDelta=null); for a minor the null delta is an expected data caveat, but the page also shows no credit count or science-vs-policy distribution, so a student cannot see the commitment or how the two halves are weighted.
dimensions.suggestion: State a total credit requirement and specify a minimum number of policy/society credits so the interdisciplinary balance is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: With no parsed requirement groups and a pool dominated by chemistry and environmental-science courses (plus stray FRSC entries), the page neither reads as a structured minor nor visibly matches its 'Science and Policy' name, since the policy dimension is nearly invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a structured requirement block and surface the policy component prominently so the page communicates the interdisciplinary design at a glance.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The science content is current and applied -- EVSC 416 Principles of Toxicology, EVSC 484 Environmental Analysis, CHEM 360 (air pollution and climate change) and CHEM 361 (water and soil pollution), and BIOL 375 Conservation Biology map well to environmental-consulting and monitoring careers. Currency is weaker on the policy/careers side (no environmental law, GIS/geospatial, or policy-analysis courses visible), which limits the interdisciplinary employability the title implies.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a GIS/geospatial-analysis option and an environmental-policy/law course so graduates can pair defensible science with the regulatory and spatial skills employers request.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and substantive across the environmental-science pool (EVSC 385 Microbiology, EVSC 416 Toxicology, EVSC 484 Environmental Analysis, CHEM 360/361), giving a clear picture of each course's scope.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous with explicit grade conditions, and missingCourseCount=0 means there are no dangling references anywhere in the pool -- the healthiest prerequisite signal among these programs.

missingTopics.rationale: The title promises 'Policy' but only EPS 202 represents it; there is no environmental law (e.g., EVSC 335), regulation, or policy-analysis course, leaving half the minor's premise unmet.
Environmental science now runs on spatial data, yet no GIS or remote-sensing course appears in the pool.
EVSC 484 Environmental Analysis covers sample handling, but there is no quantitative environmental data-analysis course (R/Python, time series) for interpreting monitoring data.
CHEM 360 addresses atmospheric chemistry and climate change chemically, but there is no dedicated climate-systems or modeling course for a program foregrounding the environment.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability, energy systems, and life-cycle assessment
missingTopics.rationale: The pool lacks any sustainability/LCA or energy-systems course that connects environmental science to decarbonization decision-making.

emergingTrends.rationale: Environmental-justice and climate courses already exist in the department (HIST 338, HIST 375); pulling them in would finally deliver the policy half and track a fast-growing field.

emergingTrends.trend: Geospatial big data and remote sensing
emergingTrends.rationale: Satellite and sensor data are central to modern environmental work; a GIS/remote-sensing elective would sharply raise employability.
BIOL 375 Conservation Biology could anchor content on restoration and ecosystem-service valuation that now drive conservation funding.
Corporate ESG and environmental-compliance roles are expanding; a policy/governance course would position completers for them.
Linking environmental analysis to circular-economy and clean-energy systems reflects where the sector's jobs are moving.

topSuggestions: Deliver the 'and Policy' half by requiring a policy/law/governance course (e.g., EVSC 335 Environmental Law, HIST 338/375) rather than leaving EPS 202 as the lone representative.
Add GIS/geospatial and quantitative environmental data-analysis options to match modern environmental-science practice.
Publish a structured requirement block with a stated credit total and a minimum policy-credit requirement so the interdisciplinary balance is transparent.
State program-level learning outcomes spanning both science and policy.
Remove stray non-environmental courses (e.g., FRSC entries) from the eligible pool so the page reads coherently.

environmental-studies-and-sustainability.name: Environmental Studies and Sustainability Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: There is no requirement structure (requirementGroups=0), and the referenced course pool is dominated by law and history courses (HIST 3xx law-and-society, IT 331/332/400 technology law, MGMT 290 Business Law, IE 447 Legal Aspects of Engineering, STS 300 Legal Reasoning) and a block of physics courses (PHYS 418/433/442/446/450, OPSE 402), with only a few genuinely environmental entries (EVSC 335 Environmental Law, HIST 338 Environmental Justice and Climate Change, HIST 375 Legal Issues in Environmental History). As presented the minor does not cohere around environmental studies and sustainability -- the pool appears shared with a Law/Technology/Culture program and a physics program.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a curated, structured plan built around environmental-science and sustainability courses rather than the borrowed law and physics pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0, so there are no dangling references; deepestPrereqChain=7 reflects the catalog depth of the source courses (e.g., PHYS 433/442 at depth 7) rather than an internal requirement of an interdisciplinary minor. The prerequisite picture is clean.
dimensions.suggestion: State a low, broadly accessible entry prerequisite so this interdisciplinary minor is open to students across colleges.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data for this minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes describing the environmental-literacy, policy, and sustainability competencies the minor certifies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and there is no term plan or summed credit figure (creditReconcileDelta null), so the minor's size and its core/elective balance cannot be verified.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and a required-versus-elective split.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The empty requirement groups, absent stated total, and an off-topic law/physics-heavy course pool make the page unclear as an environmental-studies plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a legible plan-of-study table listing clearly environmental and sustainability courses.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The environmental-law, policy, and justice angle that is present (EVSC 335 Environmental Law, HIST 338 Environmental Justice and Climate Change, HIST 375 Legal Issues in Environmental History) is genuinely relevant to sustainability careers, but the minor lacks the natural-science and technical sustainability content -- climate science, renewable energy, environmental science, and geospatial analysis -- that employers and graduate programs expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Add environmental-science, climate-science, and renewable-energy/sustainability-technology courses to complement the strong environmental-law and justice offerings.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and substantive across the pool (EVSC 335 Environmental Law, HIST 338 Environmental Justice and Climate Change in America, HIST 375 Legal Issues in Environmental History), with no thin or placeholder excerpts among sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites carry clear conditions (typically ENGL 102 plus a 200-level GER) and missingCourseCount=0, so there are no dangling references and the statements are internally consistent.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool has environmental law and history but no ecology or environmental-science course, the natural-science foundation of the field.
HIST 338 covers climate change socially and through environmental justice, but no physical climate-science course is present.
Despite 'Sustainability' in the title, there is no energy/renewables/green-technology course (the physics courses in the pool, e.g., PHYS 446 solid state, are not energy-focused).

missingTopics.topic: GIS, remote sensing, and environmental data
missingTopics.rationale: No geospatial or environmental-data-analysis course is present, though this is a core applied skill in the field.
MGMT 290 is general business law and EVSC 335 is environmental law, but no environmental-economics or policy-analysis course is present.

emergingTrends.rationale: Connecting HIST 338 environmental justice to technical adaptation content matches a fast-growing area of practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Sustainability analytics, ESG, and carbon accounting
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven sustainability is a rapidly growing employer demand absent from the current pool.
Pairing the physics offerings with an explicit energy-systems course would give the 'sustainability' half real content.
HIST 338 already anchors this rapidly growing area, which the minor could build out into a strength.
Quantitative sustainability methods such as LCA are increasingly expected and are absent from the pool.

topSuggestions: Curate the course pool to the discipline -- as presented it is dominated by law/history and physics courses with only a few environmental entries (EVSC 335, HIST 338, HIST 375); rebuild it around environmental-science and sustainability courses.

Add the field's core science and technical content -- environmental science/ecology, climate science, renewable energy, and GIS/environmental data -- to complement the strong environmental-law and justice offerings.
Publish an explicit requirement structure and a stated total credit count (both currently absent).
Add an environmental-economics/policy-analysis course and sustainability-analytics/ESG content.
Publish program-level learning outcomes for the minor.

forensic-science-minor-376.name: Forensic Science Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups=0), so the minor is presented as an eligible-course pool rather than an ordered sequence. Within it the forensic offerings are coherent and recognizable (FRSC 201 Intro -> 307 Crime Scene -> 359 Physical Methods, plus 475 Forensic Chemistry, 479 Forensic Biology, 480 Forensic Microscopy, and HIST 320 Law and Evidence), but the pool is also padded with chemistry, environmental, and IT courses that obscure the intended forensic pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit forensic-minor structure (FRSC 201 core plus a stated number of forensic electives) and trim the unrelated chemistry/environmental entries so the pathway is clear.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0 means no dangling references, and deepestPrereqChain=6 is sensible; the forensic chains are dependency-honest (FRSC 201 -> 307 -> 359; FRSC 479 behind BIOL 352; FRSC 475/480 behind CHEM 221) with explicit grade conditions.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the graph clean while curating the pool; verify that any forensic electives requiring deep chemistry/biology chains are flagged so minors can plan the prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes are provided -- only a course pool with descriptions. A forensic credential should state the competencies (evidence integrity, analytical exposure, legal context) it certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an outcomes statement describing what the minor certifies (crime-scene/evidence fundamentals, one hands-on forensic analysis area, and legal-context literacy).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: No credit requirement is stated (statedTotalCredits=null, summedTermCredits=null, creditReconcileDelta=null). The null delta is an expected minor-data caveat, but with no visible credit count or core-vs-elective split a student cannot gauge the commitment.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credits and how many must be FRSC-designated so the requirement is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: With no parsed requirement groups and a pool that mixes FRSC courses with chemistry, environmental, and IT offerings, the page does not read as a self-contained forensic minor; the FRSC 498 excerpt also bleeds into an unrelated 'HIST 2**' catalog fragment, indicating scrape noise.
dimensions.suggestion: Present a structured requirement block distinct from adjacent department pools and clean the scrape artifacts (e.g., the FRSC 498 description).

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The forensic pool is broad and current: classical wet-lab forensics (FRSC 307 Crime Scene, 359 Physical Methods, 475 Forensic Chemistry, 479 Forensic Biology, 480 Forensic Microscopy) sit alongside digital forensics (IT 330 Computer Forensic, IT 332 Digital Crime) and legal context (HIST 320 Law and Evidence) -- notably it exposes minors to digital evidence that the B.S. required plan does not.
dimensions.suggestion: Formalize a two-track option (physical/biological forensics and digital forensics) so minors can deliberately pair wet-lab and digital-evidence skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and substantive across the forensic offerings (FRSC 307, 359, 475, 479, 480) and supporting courses; the only issue is scrape noise in the FRSC 498 excerpt.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and missingCourseCount=0 (no dangling references), so the forensic chains are unambiguous; deeper electives like FRSC 479 correctly surface their biology prerequisites.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool includes analytical and pattern courses (FRSC 359, 480) but no treatment of likelihood ratios or error rates, which post-NAS/PCAST practice makes essential to defensible conclusions.

missingTopics.topic: Quality assurance, accreditation, and expert testimony
missingTopics.rationale: FRSC 495 Senior Seminar aside, there is no dedicated coverage of ISO 17025/FEPAC quality systems, proficiency testing, or courtroom testimony that crime labs require.
FRSC 475 Forensic Chemistry and EVSC 416 Toxicology exist in the catalog but are not framed as a toxicology pathway, despite toxicology's central role in casework.
The pool is strong on chemistry/biology/microscopy but omits skeletal, entomological, and scene-reconstruction specialties that round out the field.
IT 330/332 cover computer and cyber crime, but modern mobile-device and cloud-evidence acquisition is not explicitly represented.

emergingTrends.trend: Forensic genetic genealogy and massively parallel sequencing
emergingTrends.rationale: FRSC 479 Forensic Biology teaches serology/STR DNA; investigative genetic genealogy and next-generation sequencing are reshaping identification and should modernize its content.
Building on IT 330/332, coverage of mobile/cloud extraction and AI-assisted triage matches where investigative workloads are growing.
Point-of-collection DNA and handheld spectrometers are moving analysis to the scene; FRSC 307/359 could expose students to them.

emergingTrends.trend: Validation and error-rate reform of pattern evidence
emergingTrends.rationale: PCAST-driven scrutiny of fingerprint/firearm/impression evidence calls for empirical validation content in FRSC 359/480.
As synthetic media spreads, authentication of images/audio/video is an emerging forensic specialty the minor could position for.

topSuggestions: Publish an explicit forensic-minor structure (FRSC core plus a stated number of forensic electives with a credit total) instead of an undifferentiated eligible-course pool.
Trim unrelated chemistry/environmental courses and formalize a physical/biological vs. digital forensics track choice.
Add coverage of forensic statistics/evidence interpretation and laboratory QA/expert testimony to align with post-PCAST practice.
State program-level learning outcomes for the credential.
Modernize FRSC 479's DNA content toward next-generation sequencing and forensic genetic genealogy, and clean the FRSC 498 scrape artifact.

game-development-minor-265.name: Game Development Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The minor is presented as an undifferentiated 40-course pool with no plan-of-study grid (requirementGroups=0), so the 15 credits carry no prescribed core-to-advanced progression; a student could satisfy it with five loosely related picks. The interdisciplinary breadth (AD 112/150, DD 284/321, COM 303/354, IE 355 Human Factors, plus IT 265/266/270/286/360/366/380) fits game development's cross-disciplinary nature, but the flagship advanced game courses (IT 466 3D Game Programming, IT 487 Advanced Game Production, IT 382/383 Game Design for XR) that appear in sibling YWCC minor pools are absent here.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a required spine (e.g., IT 265 Game Architecture -> IT 366 2D Game Programming -> IT 466 3D Game Programming) plus a curated art/UX elective set so the 15 credits form a deliberate progression rather than a menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite references are clean (missingCourseCount=0, no dangling codes), a genuine strength, but the deepest chain is 7 and several pool courses sit very deep: IS 333 Social Network Analysis, IS 448 Usability, and IS 465 are at depth 7, and IS 421 at depth 5. In a 15-credit minor those courses are effectively unreachable without the parent IS/IT major sequence, so a meaningful part of the advertised pool is inaccessible to typical minor students.
dimensions.suggestion: Mark which pool courses are realistically reachable inside a 15-credit minor and publish a low-prerequisite entry path (e.g., IT 265/266/270/286 at depths 1-2) so students are not steered toward depth-7 courses they cannot enroll in.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a course pool with per-course descriptions is present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, and it is especially costly for a skills-based credential like game development where employers look for demonstrable competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 explicit minor outcomes (e.g., 'build and ship a playable 2D/3D game', 'apply game AI and real-time graphics techniques', 'conduct playtesting/UX evaluation') so the minor's value is legible.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no plan-of-study grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a credit-transparency point rather than a reconciliation defect: statedTotalCredits=15 (five 3-credit courses) is stated but the page gives no split between a required core and electives. Every pool course is a uniform 3 credits, which keeps arithmetic simple but hides how much of the 15 is prescribed versus free choice.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure explicitly (e.g., 9 credits required game core + 6 credits curated electives) so students can see the required-versus-elective balance at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: No plan grid is expected for a minor, and course descriptions are legible and substantive. Clarity is undercut, however, by a pool that omits the very advanced game courses (IT 466, IT 487, IT 382/383) present in sibling minors while mixing in many non-game courses (HIST 387, ID 203, IE 355) with no required/optional labeling, so the reader cannot tell what the minor actually centers on.
dimensions.suggestion: Present a clearly labeled required-core / elective structure and include the advanced game-production and XR courses so the page reflects the minor's stated focus.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Game-specific coverage is respectable (IT 265 Game Architecture, IT 366 2D Game Programming, IT 270 3D Modeling, IT 286 Foundations of Game Production, IT 360 Computer Graphics for VFX) and UX is well served (IS 247/375/448). But there is no dedicated modern game-engine course (Unity/Unreal, C#/C++ pipelines), no game AI (pathfinding, behavior trees, PCG), no multiplayer/networking, and no game audio, and the XR courses are absent from this pool despite existing in the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or modernize an engine-centric build course and a game-AI course, and surface the existing XR (IT 382/383) offerings so the minor tracks current studio practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific across the pool (e.g., DD 284 Video and Animation, IS 322 Mobile Applications, IT 366 2D Game Programming), with no thin/placeholder excerpts observed; restriction notes are included where relevant.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with grade conditions (e.g., COM 303/354 require 'ENGL 102 with a grade of C or higher'), and missingCourseCount=0 means no dangling references; depth-0 intro courses correctly carry none.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool leans on IT 366 2D Game Programming and IT 270 3D Modeling but has no course anchored in a contemporary engine with scripting (C#/C++), scene/ECS architecture, and asset pipelines; the catalog's IT 466 3D Game Programming should be surfaced and modernized as engine-based.

missingTopics.topic: Gameplay AI and procedural content generation
missingTopics.rationale: No course covers pathfinding, finite-state machines, behavior trees, or PCG, which are core to shipping games; a dedicated Game AI course would fill the largest technical gap in the pool.

IT 120 (intro networking) and IT 490 (systems integration) are generic; there is no netcode, client-server, or real-time synchronization content despite multiplayer being standard in commercial titles.
The pool covers art, animation, and graphics (AD/DD/IT 360) but has no audio component, leaving a full production discipline unrepresented.
IT 286 Foundations of Game Production is present but the advanced production course (IT 487) is missing from the pool, so there is no shipped-game capstone to anchor a student portfolio.

emergingTrends.rationale: The catalog already offers IT 382/383 Game Design for XR but they are absent from this minor's pool; surfacing them would position students for the fastest-growing interactive platform.
Procedural asset generation, LLM-driven NPC dialogue, and AI-assisted art are reshaping production pipelines, yet nothing in the pool touches AI-assisted content creation.

emergingTrends.trend: Real-time 3D beyond entertainment (virtual production, serious games, digital twins)
emergingTrends.rationale: IT 360 Computer Graphics for VFX and IT 380 Educational Software Design hint at adjacent uses; game-engine skills increasingly transfer to film, training, and simulation markets.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-driven / live-service design and player analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 Social Network Analysis and IS 392 Web Mining exist in the parent pool; games-as-a-service depend on telemetry and analytics that this minor could leverage.
IS 322 Mobile Applications is in scope; mobile remains the largest games platform, so a mobile game-build path would sharpen employability.

topSuggestions: Convert the flat 40-course pool into a required game core plus curated electives with an explicit progression (architecture -> 2D -> 3D -> production capstone).
Add the missing modern competencies: a game-engine build course, a game-AI course, and game audio; surface the existing XR courses (IT 382/383) that are absent from the pool.
Publish program-level learning outcomes tied to demonstrable deliverables (ship a playable game, build a portfolio).
Restore the advanced game courses (IT 466 3D Game Programming, IT 487 Advanced Game Production) to the displayed pool so the minor reflects its own focus.
State the 15-credit required-vs-elective split and flag which deep-prerequisite pool courses are realistically reachable within the minor.

geosystems-minor-593.name: Geosystems Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), and the captured course pool is identical to the Environmental Engineering Minor (both from page 593, same metrics: 49 referenced, 7 missing, depth 8), so it is a shared department listing rather than a geosystems-specific plan. A geosystems (geotechnical / geo-environmental) minor would be expected to center on soil mechanics, foundations, geology and groundwater; the pool does include CE 341/342 (Soil Mechanics) and CE 443 (Foundation Design), but these are outnumbered by structural, transportation and environmental CE/ENE courses, so the geosystems focus is under-represented and its progression cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a geosystems-specific structure built around the geotechnical spine (CE 341/342 Soil Mechanics, CE 443 Foundation Design) plus geology/groundwater electives, and remove the structural/transportation/environmental courses inherited from the shared listing.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The referenced chains are reasonable (deepestPrereqChain 8) with grade conditions, but missingCourseCount is 7 and the missingCoursesSample (ARCH 622, CE 341A, CE 431, CE 506, CE 602, CE 644, GER 300) includes graduate-level references and a soil-mechanics lab section, again indicating a pool broader than an undergraduate geosystems minor. With no requirement structure, the geotechnical pathway cannot be tied to the graph.
dimensions.suggestion: Scope the pool to undergraduate geosystems courses so the graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) drop out, and ensure the CE 341/342 to CE 443 geotechnical chain (including the CE 341A lab) resolves cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a geosystems minor this is a real gap because it should certify a defined geotechnical / geo-environmental competency to students from other majors, and no such statement is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes describing the geosystems competency the minor certifies (e.g., ability to characterize soils, analyze slope/foundation behavior, assess subsurface and groundwater conditions).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are all null — a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than a proven imbalance. As presented a student cannot see the minor's credit requirement or distribution, and the mismatch between the geosystems label and the broad shared pool adds uncertainty.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and list the specific geotechnical/geo-environmental courses that count, so the load matches the minor's title.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured page is incomplete and effectively indistinguishable from the Environmental Engineering Minor: no plan of study, no credit total, no description, and an identical course pool. A reader cannot tell what the geosystems minor requires or how it differs from the environmental minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct geosystems-minor page with its own description, a geotechnically focused course list and a credit total, and fix the parser so it no longer shares the environmental listing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The geotechnical content that is present (CE 341/342 Soil Mechanics, CE 443 Foundation Design, CE 360 Sustainable Materials) is relevant and employable, and geosystems skills are in steady demand for infrastructure and geo-environmental work. The score is held to adequate because the pool under-represents the discipline (no explicit geology, hydrogeology/groundwater, geosynthetics or in-situ testing) and its shared, unstructured form gives no assurance a student reaches a coherent geosystems subset.
dimensions.suggestion: Add geology/hydrogeology and modern geotechnical electives (geosynthetics, in-situ testing, ground improvement) so the minor genuinely represents geosystems practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most sampled courses have present, specific descriptions (32 of 34; 2 lack a description excerpt), including the geotechnical courses (CE 341 Soil Mechanics, CE 443 Foundation Design) and supporting CE/ENE courses; only excerpt length is truncated. The score is held below 5 by the two missing descriptions and by the many non-geosystems courses carried in from the shared listing.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites carry grade conditions where present, but the pool mixes undergraduate and graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) among its 7 dangling links and is not scoped to geosystems, so the minor's actual entry requirements are not clearly delimited.

missingTopics.topic: A defined geotechnical core scoped to the minor
missingTopics.rationale: CE 341/342 Soil Mechanics and CE 443 Foundation Design are present but buried in a broad shared pool; the key missing element is a stated geotechnical required core distinct from the environmental minor.
A geosystems minor should cover geology and subsurface water, yet no explicit geology or groundwater course appears in the pool.
Modern geotechnical practice relies heavily on geosynthetics and ground-improvement methods, which the current soil-mechanics/foundations pair does not address.
The CE 341A lab is a dangling reference; a site-characterization/in-situ-testing course would give the minor the field-investigation skills geosystems work requires.

Structural, transportation and environmental courses shared with the Environmental minor, plus graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622), should be removed so the minor reads as a geotechnical pathway.

emergingTrends.rationale: Seismic and hazard-resilient geotechnical design is a growing priority that a modern elective building on CE 341/443 could introduce.

emergingTrends.trend: Geospatial analysis and remote sensing of ground conditions
emergingTrends.rationale: GIS, InSAR and remote sensing are transforming subsurface and slope monitoring; a geospatial elective would modernize the minor.
Linking geotechnics (CE 341/342) with environmental content positions the minor for brownfield/remediation work at the geo-environmental interface.

emergingTrends.trend: Sustainable geotechnics and low-carbon ground improvement
emergingTrends.rationale: Bio-cementation and low-carbon ground-improvement methods reflect the sustainability shift reaching geotechnical practice.
Instrumented slopes, embankments and foundations are becoming standard; a monitoring/instrumentation component would give the minor current relevance.

topSuggestions: Fix the data-extraction conflation and label mismatch: the pool is identical to the Environmental Engineering Minor (both from page 593) — give the geosystems minor its own geotechnically focused course list.
Publish the geosystems minor's required/elective structure and total credits (all currently null), built around CE 341/342 Soil Mechanics and CE 443 Foundation Design.
Add the missing geosystems core (engineering geology, hydrogeology/groundwater, geosynthetics, in-situ testing) so the content matches the minor's title.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the geotechnical/geo-environmental competency the minor certifies.
Scope out the graduate references (CE 506/602/644, ARCH 622) and non-geosystems courses so the minor reads as a coherent undergraduate geotechnical pathway.

geriatric-engineering-technology-minor-6.name: Geriatric Engineering Technology Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups 0), which is normal for a minor, but the 36-course sample pool does not reflect the minor's stated theme: it is a generic mechanical/manufacturing-technology selection (MET 236/237/301/302/303/304/307/308/314/403/404/407/409/415, ME 215, ENGR/ENTR/IE courses) with no gerontology, assistive-technology, rehabilitation or aging-health content. Notably, the department's MIT 360 Introduction to Gerontology and MIT 362 Geriatric Engineering I (which appear in the sibling 650-suffixed minors) are absent here.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the course pool around the aging/assistive theme, starting with MIT 360 Gerontology and MIT 362 Geriatric Engineering I plus human factors (IE 355).

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 7 with sound chains (MET 404 depth 5 on MET 303/304, MET 302 depth 7). Of the 4 dangling references, two are genuine broken links (CPT 325, ENGR 290) and two are GER placeholders (GER 200, GER 300).
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the genuine dangling references CPT 325 and ENGR 290, and the GER 200/300 placeholders.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes appear in the provided data; only a course pool is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes describing the assistive/aging-technology competencies a completer should gain.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is null with no parsed terms, so neither the required credit total nor its distribution can be determined from the data; the missing total is a data gap that should be filled for a catalog minor.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credits and publish a required-vs-elective credit breakdown.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page lacks a credit total and a plan of study, and the displayed course pool does not match the minor's name, so a prospective student cannot tell what a Geriatric Engineering Technology minor entails.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the credit total, a structured course list, and a short description tying the minor to gerontechnology and assistive engineering.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The minor's premise, engineering for an aging population (assistive devices, rehabilitation, aging-in-place, gerontechnology), is highly relevant and growing, but the sampled courses contain none of that content, so as published the minor does not reflect the field it names.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the pool with aging-focused courses (MIT 360/362), assistive-robotics/wearables and health-informatics options so it reflects current gerontechnology practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled descriptions themselves are present and specific (MET 404 on conduction/convection/radiation heat transfer, MET 307/308 on plastics conversion and processing, ENGR 301 on engineering applications of data science); the weakness is topical fit to the minor, not description quality.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most prerequisites are explicit and compound (MET 404 '(MATH 309 or MATH 211 or MATH 213), and MET 303, and MET 304'), but two genuine dangling references (CPT 325, ENGR 290) plus several 'None'/junior-standing-only entries reduce overall clarity.

missingTopics.topic: Gerontology and the physiology of aging
missingTopics.rationale: The minor names geriatric engineering but includes no aging-science course; MIT 360 Introduction to Gerontology exists in the department and appears in the sibling minors but is absent here.
There is no course on designing assistive/rehabilitation devices; MIT 362 Geriatric Engineering I (assistive-technology system design) exists in the department yet is missing from this pool.

missingTopics.topic: Human factors and ergonomics for older adults
missingTopics.rationale: Designing for reduced mobility, vision and dexterity requires human-factors grounding; IE 355 Human Factors is available in the department but not included.
Mobility aids, exoskeletons and fall-prevention systems are core geriatric-engineering topics with no representation in the sampled courses.
Telehealth and remote patient monitoring for aging-in-place are absent; the department's MIT 326 Electronic Medical Record Design and MIT 231 medical-device security could support this.

emergingTrends.trend: Wearables and remote monitoring for aging-in-place
emergingTrends.rationale: Continuous health sensing lets older adults live independently; a wearables/remote-monitoring course would give the minor a genuine geriatric focus.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML for fall detection and health prediction
emergingTrends.rationale: Machine learning on sensor data drives modern elder-care technology; ENGR 301 Engineering Applications of Data Science is a ready anchor.

emergingTrends.trend: Assistive robotics and smart-home/IoT for elder care
emergingTrends.rationale: Service robots and connected homes are rapidly entering geriatric care and would align the minor with its stated purpose.
Additive manufacturing enables personalized aids; CHE 415 Introduction to 3D Printing is already in the pool and could be aimed at assistive design.
Connected geriatric devices raise safety and privacy stakes; MIT 231 Introduction to Computer Security for Medical Devices is available in the department.

topSuggestions: Align the course pool with the minor's theme: it currently contains no aging/assistive content; add MIT 360 Gerontology and MIT 362 Geriatric Engineering I (offered in the department, absent here) and human factors (IE 355).
State the minor's total credits (statedTotalCredits is null) and publish a required-vs-elective structure.
Publish explicit learning outcomes, which are absent from the data.
Add assistive-robotics/wearables, health-informatics (MIT 326) and medical-device-security (MIT 231) options, and aim CHE 415 3D printing at custom assistive devices.
Resolve the genuine dangling references CPT 325 and ENGR 290.

global-studies-minor-447.name: Global Studies Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The visible course pool (40 of 51 referenced) is dominated by general Communication/Media courses (COM 201, 240, 303, 310, 315-390) that are not intrinsically global; genuinely global content is confined to a thin history spine (HIST 213 The Twentieth-Century World, HIST 363 The United States as a World Power, HIST 365 Science and Technology in the Global South, HIST 367 International Law and Diplomacy, HIST 372 Contemporary Europe). With no requirementGroups (empty plan of study) and no defined global core, the menu reads as a broad HSS elective list rather than a curated global-studies pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a curated global core (a required internationally focused sequence plus a world-region or language component) and separate it from general electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. The deepest chain is 6, driven mainly by the ENGL 101 -> ENGL 102 -> 200-level GER -> 300-level gateway pattern rather than deep discipline-internal sequencing, which is reasonable for a minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep gateway chains shallow for a minor by allowing more 200-level global entry points that do not stack the full ENGL/GER ladder.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, so the number of credits the minor requires is not published, and creditReconcileDelta is null (a data-consistency caveat expected for a menu-style minor with no term breakdown, not a defect in itself). Courses are uniformly 3 credits, so within-menu balance is fine, but the total requirement and any core/elective split are opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement (e.g., 15-18) and how credits split between a required global core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The program page (as parsed) has no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits; course-level descriptions are present and substantive, but the program itself is thinly presented and the course menu's global focus is not legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short program description and a legibly grouped plan of study (core vs. regional/thematic electives).

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The world-history courses (HIST 365 on the Global South, HIST 367 International Law and Diplomacy, HIST 372 Contemporary Europe) plus digital-society electives (COM 343 Social Media Analytics, COM 371 Social Network Theory) give some current relevance to careers in international NGOs, global business, and policy; however the pool lacks international relations, global political economy, and any language/area-studies depth expected of the field.
dimensions.suggestion: Add contemporary global-affairs and political-economy coursework so the minor connects to global governance and development careers.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled course descriptions are present and substantive (full-sentence overviews such as COM 201's history/theory/practice framing); none are flagged thin or missing.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present for essentially every course with consistent, explicit grade conditions ('C or higher') and 0 dangling references (missingCourseCount=0). The main ambiguity is that many prereqs cite a GER category ('one History and Humanities GER 200-level course') rather than a specific course.

missingTopics.rationale: A Global Studies minor typically requires language competency or a region specialization; the visible pool has no language or single-region deep-dive course.
There are no political-science/IR courses on international institutions or diplomacy beyond the single historical HIST 367.
No economics or development courses appear; the Global South is represented only by HIST 365.
A core of contemporary global studies, entirely absent from the referenced courses.
Transnational public health and climate governance are missing; STS 308 Globalization / STS 363 Sustainability could be cross-listed.

emergingTrends.rationale: Leverage COM 343 Social Media Analytics and COM 371 Social Network Theory to study cross-border information and influence.
Connect the history spine to climate-driven displacement, an increasingly central global issue.
Build HIST 365 Science and Technology in the Global South into a fuller decolonial/area-studies track.
Post-pandemic reshoring and trade fragmentation warrant a global political-economy offering the minor currently lacks.
Quantitative and mapping methods for global problems would modernize an otherwise humanities-only menu.

topSuggestions: Define and publish a curated global core (required internationally focused sequence plus a language or world-region component) instead of a broad HSS elective menu.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes.
State the total credit requirement and the core-vs-elective structure.
Add international relations / global political economy and migration/human-rights courses.
Reduce reliance on US-centric media electives and foreground world-history and globalization courses.

grand-challenges-of-engineering-minor-65.name: Grand Challenges of Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups 0), normal for a minor, but the 16-credit minor's identity is weak in the data: its on-theme spine (ENGR 290 intro [dangling], ENGR 491/492 research, ENGR 493 service learning, ENGR 301 data science, ENGR 423 drone science) is embedded in a large manufacturing/mechanical pool (MNET 215/300/303/318/405/416/420, MET 307/308/403/404/409) that is identical to the sibling Manufacturing Engineering Technology minor, so the Grand-Challenges focus is diluted.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a Grand-Challenges-aligned pool (energy/sustainability, water/environment, health, security) around the ENGR research/service-learning spine rather than sharing the manufacturing course list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 7 with reasonable chains (MNET 405 depth 4, MET 404 depth 5). Both dangling references (missingCourseCount 2: CPT 325, ENGR 290) are genuine broken links, and ENGR 290 is especially notable because it is the program's own intro/gateway course and a prerequisite for ENGR 493.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the dangling references, prioritizing ENGR 290, the minor's own gateway course.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes appear in the provided data; only a course pool is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes describing the interdisciplinary, challenge-driven competencies a completer should gain.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The stated total is 16 credits, but with no parsed terms creditReconcileDelta is null, so the required-vs-elective split cannot be verified; the null delta is a data-consistency caveat for a minor rather than a defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the required-vs-elective credit breakdown so the 16 credits reconcile transparently.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page shows a 16-credit total but no plan of study, and its sample course list is identical to the Manufacturing Engineering Technology minor (both id-650), a data-consistency concern that makes the two minors indistinguishable in the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Differentiate the Grand-Challenges course list from the manufacturing minor and add a description tying it to the NAE Grand Challenges themes.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The NAE Grand Challenges framing is highly relevant, and several pool courses support it (ENGR 301 data science, ENGR 423 drones, ENGR 491/492 research, ENGR 493 service learning). However, the dominant MNET/MET manufacturing courses map only weakly to challenge themes such as clean energy, water, health, security and infrastructure.
dimensions.suggestion: Add courses that map directly to NAE challenge themes (clean energy/sustainability, environmental/water, health informatics, cybersecurity) and foreground the ENGR spine as the required core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific (ENGR 301 on engineering applications of data science, ENGR 493 on service-learning and multicultural competency, MNET 405 on NC mill/lathe programming, MET 404 on heat-transfer modes); only a few instructor-permission entries are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Many prerequisites are explicit (ENGR 492 'ENGR 491', MNET 405 'ME 215 and MNET 303', MNET 420 'MNET 315'), but two genuine dangling references (ENGR 290, CPT 325) plus several instructor-permission/'None' gateways reduce clarity, and ENGR 493's requirement on the missing ENGR 290 is a real break.

missingTopics.rationale: NAE challenges lead with economical solar, fusion and carbon management, yet the pool has no energy or sustainability course, only manufacturing and mechanical electives.
Access to clean water is a headline Grand Challenge with no representation in the sampled courses.
Advancing health informatics and engineering better medicines are core challenges; the department's health courses (MIT 326, MIT 360/362) are not foregrounded here.
Cybersecurity is an explicit Grand Challenge missing from the pool; MIT 231 Introduction to Computer Security for Medical Devices exists in the department and could seed it.
Restoring and improving urban infrastructure is a named challenge with no course coverage in the sampled list.

emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven methods cut across every NAE challenge; ENGR 301 Engineering Applications of Data Science is a natural required anchor.
Decarbonization and renewable energy are the defining engineering frontier and should headline a Grand-Challenges minor.
Engineering better medicines and health informatics is a fast-growing challenge area the minor could emphasize.

emergingTrends.trend: Autonomous systems and drones for humanitarian/infrastructure use
emergingTrends.rationale: ENGR 423 Drone Science Fundamentals positions the minor for disaster-response and infrastructure-monitoring applications.
Interdisciplinary, community-engaged engineering (ENGR 493 service learning) reflects how grand-challenge work is actually organized.

topSuggestions: Differentiate the course pool from the Manufacturing Engineering Technology minor: the two id-650 minors carry an identical sample course list, making them indistinguishable in the data.
Resolve the dangling references ENGR 290 (the minor's own gateway/intro course) and CPT 325.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and a required-vs-elective plan, both absent from the data.
Add courses mapping to NAE challenge themes: clean energy/sustainability, environmental/water, health informatics, and cybersecurity (e.g., MIT 231).
Foreground the ENGR research/service-learning spine (ENGR 290/491/492/493) and AI/data (ENGR 301) as the required core.

history-minor-418.name: History Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups=0), so the minor is an eligible-course pool rather than an ordered sequence, and the listed home department ('forensic-science-law-technology-and-culture-b-s') is itself a data-consistency quirk for a history minor. The history offerings in the pool are thematically rich and coherent around law-and-society and technology history (HIST 342 Civil Rights, 361 Founding, 364 American Law in the World, 369 Law and Society, 370 Media Law, 375 Environmental Legal Issues, 378 Health Law, 384 Invention and Regulation), but the pool is padded with many non-history IT/forensic/chemistry courses that blur the minor's identity.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit history-minor structure (a stated number of HIST credits with a distribution rule) and separate the genuine history requirement from the cross-listed IT/forensic electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain=6 is reasonable and the HIST courses share a clean, uniform prerequisite (ENGL 102 plus a 200-level History/Humanities GER). missingCourseCount=2 involves only a GER 200 placeholder and HIST 312, so the history offerings themselves have essentially no dangling references.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the GER 200 placeholder and the HIST 312 reference so the graph is fully clean.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only a course pool with descriptions is provided. A history minor should still state the analytical and writing competencies it certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an outcomes statement (historical analysis, primary-source interpretation, argumentative writing) appropriate to a minor's scope.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: No credit requirement is stated (statedTotalCredits=null, summedTermCredits=null, creditReconcileDelta=null). For a minor the null delta is an expected data caveat, but the page shows no credit count or distribution rule, so the commitment and how many credits must be history (versus cross-listed IT/forensic courses) are invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credits and require a minimum number of HIST-designated credits so the credential is genuinely a history minor.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: With no parsed requirement groups, a home-department tag that points at a forensic/law B.S., and a pool mixing HIST courses with a large set of IT, forensic, and chemistry courses, the page does not read as a self-contained history minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the department attribution and render a structured HIST requirement block distinct from the cross-listed technical electives.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: For a technological university, the pool is unusually current and career-relevant: it foregrounds law-and-technology history (HIST 384 Invention and Regulation, HIST 370 Legal Issues in the History of Media, HIST 378 Medicine and Health Law, HIST 338 Environmental Justice and Climate Change) and even cross-lists contemporary technology-law/security courses (IT 331 Privacy, IT 400 IT and the Law, IT 332 Digital Crime), giving the minor a distinctive modern profile.
dimensions.suggestion: Formalize the law-and-technology history strength as a named theme so students can intentionally build a contemporary, employable history minor.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions across the history pool are substantive and specific (HIST 369 Law and Society, HIST 384 Invention and Regulation, HIST 378 Medicine and Health Law, HIST 370 Media Law), clearly conveying scope.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and uniform for the HIST courses (ENGL 102 plus a 200-level GER) with grade conditions, and only 2 references dangle (GER 200 placeholder and HIST 312), so the history offerings are clear.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool is almost entirely modern US law-and-society and technology history; pre-modern, European, and non-Western coverage is thin for a general 'History Minor.'
HIST 395 Research Methods in Law and Society is topical; a general historical-methods/historiography course would better serve minors sampling the discipline.
Despite many IT crossovers in the pool, there is no digital-history or computational text-analysis course connecting technical tools to historical inquiry.
No applied public-history/archives course appears, a common bridge from a history minor to careers.
The offerings concentrate on legal history; broader economic/social-history topics are underrepresented.

emergingTrends.trend: Law-and-technology history as a signature strength
emergingTrends.rationale: The pool (HIST 384 Invention and Regulation, HIST 370 Media Law, plus IT 400 IT and the Law) already positions this; naming it as a formal theme would differentiate the minor.
The cross-listed IT courses make this minor a natural home for text-mining/AI-assisted historical analysis.
HIST 338 Environmental Justice and HIST 375 Environmental Legal Issues track a fast-growing subfield with strong student interest.

emergingTrends.trend: History of medicine, health, and bioethics
emergingTrends.rationale: HIST 378 Medicine and Health Law gives the minor post-pandemic salience the department could build on.

emergingTrends.trend: Public and digital public history careers
emergingTrends.rationale: Applied and digital public-history work is expanding; an offering would connect the minor to concrete outcomes.

topSuggestions: Publish an explicit history-minor structure with a stated credit total and a minimum number of required HIST credits, distinct from the cross-listed IT/forensic electives.
Correct the minor's department attribution (currently tagged to a forensic/law B.S.) so the page is accurate.
Ensure chronological/geographic breadth and a historiography/methods option so the minor represents the discipline, not only modern legal history.
Formalize the law-and-technology history strength (and a digital-humanities option) as a named, contemporary theme.
State program-level learning outcomes and resolve the GER 200 and HIST 312 dangling references.

industrial-engineering-minor-633.name: Industrial Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups 0), which is normal for a minor, so term-over-term progression cannot be assessed; the 15-credit minor instead presents a course pool. The core industrial-engineering options are coherent (IE 331 statistics, IE 335 cost analysis, IE 339 work measurement, IE 355 human factors, IE 439 deterministic OR, IE 461 quality, IE 466 facilities), but the pool also mixes in far-afield entries (MATH 371 Physiology, MATH 372 Population Biology, ME 432 Air Conditioning) that blur the minor's identity.
dimensions.suggestion: Declare a required IE core and a curated elective list (with a term/credit structure) so students see an intentional path rather than an undifferentiated menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This is the cleanest prerequisite graph of the group: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references) with good depth, deepestPrereqChain 8 and well-formed chains such as IE 461 (depth 6, on IE 331), IE 466 (depth 5, on IE 439) and ECET 205 (depth 6).
dimensions.suggestion: Maintain the zero-dangling status as courses are revised, and document any co-requisites explicitly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes appear in the provided data; only the course pool and a 15-credit total are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes for the minor (what an IE-minor completer should be able to analyze, model and optimize).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The stated total is 15 credits, but with no parsed terms creditReconcileDelta is null, so the split between required and elective credit cannot be verified from the data; the null delta is a data-consistency caveat rather than a defect for a minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the required-vs-elective credit breakdown so the 15 credits reconcile transparently.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The course pool is legible and the 15-credit total is stated, but the page shows no required/elective structure and includes probable parsing artifacts (MATH 371 Physiology, MATH 372 Population Biology, ME 432 HVAC) that read as noise against an IE minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Present the minor as a short structured list (core + electives) and remove off-topic pool entries.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The pool reflects solid, current industrial-engineering practice: operations research (IE 439), applied statistics (IE 331), quality (IE 461), human factors (IE 355), facilities/material handling (IE 466), industrial robotics (IE 449), CIM (IE 453) and PLCs (IE 455). The main currency gap is the absence of a data-analytics/ML-for-operations option.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a data-analytics/optimization option (e.g., building on ENGR 301 Engineering Applications of Data Science) to the elective pool.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific (IE 439 on linear/nonlinear/integer/dynamic programming and network flows, IE 461 on process capability and inspection data, IE 466 on facility location/layout and material handling); only a handful of cross-listed math/economics entries are terse topic lists.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit and internally consistent (IE 461 'IE 331', IE 466 'IE 439', IE 339 'IE 203, IE 224') and missingCourseCount is 0, though several courses list 'None' while their text imposes a 'junior standing' restriction that is not captured as a formal prerequisite.

missingTopics.topic: Data analytics and statistical learning for operations
missingTopics.rationale: The pool's quantitative content stops at IE 331 Applied Statistical Methods; a modern IE minor should expose students to regression/ML-based analytics for process and demand data.
Quality is represented only by IE 461 Product Quality Assurance; explicit lean/Six Sigma and DMAIC content is a standard IE expectation and is absent.
IE 466 covers facilities and material handling, but there is no supply-chain/production-planning option (IE 459 is not in the pool), a core IE competency.
Systems modeling leans analytical (IE 439 deterministic OR); IE 445 Industrial Simulation is not in this pool, leaving simulation underrepresented.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability and safety in systems design
missingTopics.rationale: IE 473 Safety Engineering exists in the department but is not in this pool; sustainability/ergonomic-risk topics are also absent.

emergingTrends.rationale: IIoT-instrumented factories are redefining IE work; the minor's CIM (IE 453) and PLC (IE 455) courses are a foundation to extend toward connected, data-rich operations.

emergingTrends.trend: Data science and ML for operations
emergingTrends.rationale: Prescriptive analytics and ML forecasting are now central to operations research; ENGR 301 could anchor a data-driven IE elective.
Post-pandemic supply-chain resilience and digital logistics are high-demand skills that the facilities/OR core (IE 466/IE 439) could grow into.
IE 449 Industrial Robotics positions the minor to address collaborative-robot ergonomics and safety, an active industrial-engineering frontier.
