Optimization is moving from batch OR (IE 439) toward real-time, AI-assisted decision support, a trend the minor should signal.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit learning outcomes for the minor, which are absent from the data.
Declare a required IE core plus a curated elective list with a credit/term structure so the 15 credits reconcile transparently (creditReconcileDelta is null).
Add a data-analytics/ML-for-operations elective (e.g., leveraging ENGR 301) to modernize the pool.
Curate the elective pool by removing off-topic artifacts (MATH 371 Physiology, MATH 372 Population Biology, ME 432 HVAC) that dilute the IE identity.
Preserve the clean prerequisite graph (missingCourseCount 0) as courses are revised.

information-technology-minor-for-ywcc-ma.name: Information Technology Minor (for YWCC majors)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The pool does contain a legitimate IT infrastructure spine (IT 120 Network Technology -> IT 202 Internet Applications -> IT 340 System Administration / IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks -> IT 490 Systems Integration, plus IS 331 Databases), which gives the minor real coherence potential. That spine is diluted, however, by nine game-design courses (IT 265/266/270/286/366/382/383/466/487) in a 32-course pool with no plan-of-study grid, so the IT identity is buried in a menu.
dimensions.suggestion: Define an explicit IT core progression (networking -> systems administration -> systems integration, plus databases and web) and move the game courses out of the required IT set.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: References are clean (missingCourseCount=0, no dangling codes), a real strength. The deepest chain is 7 with IS 465 at depth 7 and IT 340/420/IS 421 at depth 5; since this minor is for YWCC (computing) majors, those students generally do hold the underlying prerequisites, so accessibility is less of a problem here than for the non-YWCC version.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a recommended prerequisite-aware ordering so YWCC majors slot the IT courses efficiently around their major sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; only course descriptions are present. For a catalog credential this is a real finding, and it also makes it impossible to see how this minor complements a student's YWCC major.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes describing the IT breadth a YWCC major gains (e.g., 'administer networked systems', 'integrate enterprise IT components', 'manage relational data stores').

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no term grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a transparency point rather than a defect: statedTotalCredits=15 is stated but with no required-versus-elective breakdown. All courses are uniform 3 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure and, importantly, list which of the student's own YWCC-major courses may not be double-counted toward the minor.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The displayed course pool is byte-identical to the 'not for YWCC majors' IT minor and to the Mobile and Web minor, so the catalog gives no way to distinguish this specific credential. The entire policy rationale for a separate 'for YWCC majors' entry (different eligible/countable courses) is invisible in the presented data, which is a genuine clarity defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Differentiate this entry from the non-YWCC version by showing the eligibility rules and the course set that YWCC majors may actually count.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The IT infrastructure coverage is solid and job-relevant (networking IT 120/420, system administration IT 340, systems integration IT 490, databases IS 331, web/internet IT 202/IS 218), but the pool has no cybersecurity and no cloud/virtualization content, which are the two defining competencies of contemporary IT practice and are expected under ACM IT2017 guidance.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an information-security course and a cloud/virtualization course to the IT core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific across the pool (IT 120 Network Technology, IT 340 System Administration, IS 331 Database Design), with no thin or placeholder excerpts observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear course logic and grade conditions where relevant, and missingCourseCount=0 confirms no dangling references across the 32-course pool.

missingTopics.rationale: A 32-course IT pool with networking and system administration but no security course is a serious gap; ACM IT2017 treats information assurance and security as a pervasive pillar, so a Network/Information Security course is the highest-value addition.

IT 340 System Administration and IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks are traditional on-premises courses; there is no AWS/Azure/GCP, containers, or infrastructure-as-code, which is where IT operations now run.
IT 490 Systems Integration is the closest fit, but there is no dedicated scripting-for-operations, configuration-management, or CI/CD content that modern IT roles require.
IS 331 Databases is present but there is no data-warehouse, business-intelligence, or analytics course, even though IT increasingly centers on data pipelines and reporting.

missingTopics.topic: A curated IT identity over game bloat
missingTopics.rationale: Nine game-design courses dominate the pool while security and cloud are absent, so the pool does not read as a coherent contemporary IT skill set.

emergingTrends.rationale: IT is shifting to cloud, containers, and declarative provisioning; IT 340/420 should gain cloud modules so YWCC majors leave with current operations skills.
An escalating threat landscape makes security literacy mandatory for every IT professional, and this pool currently offers none.
AI-driven monitoring, incident triage, and copilots are reshaping IT operations and support, an area no current course touches.
IT 120/420 networking should extend to IoT device management and edge architectures that are expanding the IT footprint.
Regulatory pressure (GDPR/CCPA) makes governance and compliance a core IT competency that complements the minor's data and systems courses.

topSuggestions: Add cybersecurity and cloud/virtualization courses, the two most conspicuous gaps in a modern IT pool.
Differentiate this minor from the identical-looking 'not for YWCC majors' and 'Mobile and Web' pools by showing the eligibility rules and countable course set specific to YWCC majors.
Define a coherent IT core progression and separate the nine game courses that currently dilute the pool.
Publish program-level learning outcomes describing the IT breadth a YWCC major gains.
State the 15-credit required-vs-elective structure, including which courses a student cannot double-count from their YWCC major.

information-technology-minor-not-for-ywc.name: Information Technology Minor (not for YWCC majors

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The pool carries a real IT infrastructure spine (IT 120 -> IT 202 -> IT 340/IT 420 -> IT 490, plus IS 331 Databases), but as with the sibling IT minor it is diluted by nine game-design courses in a 32-course pool with no plan-of-study grid. For non-computing students this incoherence is more damaging, because they have no major context to help them assemble a sensible five-course path from the menu.
dimensions.suggestion: For a non-YWCC audience, prescribe a fixed IT core sequence rather than a pool so students without a computing background follow a coherent introduction-to-IT path.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: References are clean (missingCourseCount=0), but the prerequisite depth is the central problem for THIS audience: the deepest chain is 7 (IS 465 depth 7; IT 340/420 and IS 421 at depth 5) and those chains run back through CS/IS courses that non-YWCC students will not have taken. A large share of the advertised pool is therefore effectively unreachable for the exact students this minor targets.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a low-prerequisite pathway (IT 120, IS 117, IT 202 at depths 0-2 with clearly stated non-computing entry points) and drop or gate the depth-5-to-7 courses that non-majors cannot realistically enter.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present; only course descriptions exist. This is a real finding for any catalog page and is especially important here, since non-computing students rely on stated outcomes to judge whether the minor is appropriate for them.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes aimed at non-computing students (e.g., 'apply IT tools to a home discipline', 'administer basic networked systems', 'manage and query data').

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no term grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a transparency point: statedTotalCredits=15 is given without a required-versus-elective breakdown. Uniform 3-credit courses keep arithmetic simple.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure and clearly mark any prerequisite courses a non-major must take first, since those could push the real cost above 15 credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The displayed course pool is byte-identical to the 'for YWCC majors' IT minor and to the Mobile and Web minor, so the catalog provides no way to see what makes this non-major credential different, and the parsed name is even truncated ('...not for YWCC majors' with no closing parenthesis). Both issues are genuine presentation defects.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the truncated program name and present a distinct, non-major-appropriate course list rather than reusing the shared pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The IT infrastructure content (networking IT 120/420, system administration IT 340, systems integration IT 490, databases IS 331, web IT 202/IS 218) is relevant, but for a non-major audience the more current draw would be applied IT literacy, and the pool lacks cybersecurity and cloud entirely while over-indexing on game courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an accessible security-and-privacy course and a cloud-literacy course pitched at non-computing students.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific throughout the pool (IT 120 Network Technology, IT 202 Internet Applications, IS 331 Database Design), with no thin or placeholder text observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite statements themselves are clear and non-dangling (missingCourseCount=0), but for a non-computing audience the clarity gap is that many prerequisites presuppose CS/IS courses these students lack, and the page does not flag which courses are open to non-majors.

missingTopics.rationale: There is no security course in a pool that includes networking and system administration; security is the highest-value addition and is especially relevant framing for non-computing students.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud computing and IT service literacy
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340/IT 420 are traditional on-premises courses with no cloud, virtualization, or SaaS content, yet cloud services are how most non-computing professionals actually encounter IT.

missingTopics.topic: Applied data management and analytics for non-majors
missingTopics.rationale: IS 331 Databases is technical; a non-major audience would benefit from an applied data/analytics course connecting IT to their home discipline.
The pool is dominated by depth-4-to-7 IS/IT courses and nine game courses, but offers little that a student with no computing prerequisites can start with, so the pool is mismatched to its audience.
No course teaches basic scripting/automation, which is arguably the most transferable IT skill for students from other majors.

emergingTrends.trend: Cloud and SaaS-based IT for non-specialists
emergingTrends.rationale: Non-computing professionals increasingly manage cloud tools; the minor should teach cloud literacy rather than only on-premises system administration.

emergingTrends.trend: Cybersecurity awareness and privacy for all disciplines
emergingTrends.rationale: Every field now faces security and privacy obligations, making a non-major security course both timely and broadly marketable.
AI copilots are transforming how non-technical professionals use IT; an AI-literacy component would strongly differentiate this non-major minor.
Non-computing students can deliver real value through no-code platforms, a fast-growing area the current IS/IT-heavy pool does not address.

emergingTrends.trend: Data governance and compliance across professions
emergingTrends.rationale: GDPR/CCPA-style obligations reach every discipline, so governance literacy would make this minor relevant to non-computing majors' careers.

topSuggestions: Replace the shared 32-course pool with a curated low-prerequisite IT pathway that non-computing students can actually complete without unstated CS/IS prerequisites.
Fix the truncated program name and differentiate this entry from the identical 'for YWCC majors' and 'Mobile and Web' course lists.
Add accessible cybersecurity and cloud-literacy courses aimed at non-majors, the competencies most missing and most marketable.
Publish learning outcomes framed for non-computing students so they can judge fit.
State the 15-credit structure and flag prerequisite courses that could raise the true credit cost for a non-major.

innovation-and-entrepreneurship-minor-71.name: Innovation and Entrepreneurship Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), and the captured course pool and metrics are identical to those of the Economics Minor (both carry id suffix 712), so the entrepreneurship course list is not individually curated. A genuine entrepreneurship spine is present within the shared pool (ENTR 210 -> ENTR 320 Financing New Venture / ENTR 330 Entrepreneurial Strategy -> ENTR 440 Lean Startup Accelerator, plus MGMT 480 Managing Technology and Innovation and MRKT 338 New Product Design), but it is embedded in a much larger unrelated set with no defined structure.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an I&E-specific structure centered on ENTR 210/320/330/440, MGMT 480, and MRKT 338, and de-duplicate it from the Economics Minor so the two distinct minors no longer share the same course pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The entrepreneurship prerequisite chain is sensible and appropriately shallow (ENTR 320 and ENTR 330 require ENTR 410 or ENTR 210; ENTR 440 has none), which suits a minor, and grade conditions elsewhere are clear (deepestPrereqChain 6 comes from finance courses in the shared pool). Internal consistency is weakened by eight dangling references, six of them graduate courses (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) absent from the undergraduate catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the six graduate-level references and confirm the entrepreneurship electives form the intended ladder (ENTR 210 as gateway into financing, strategy, and the lean-startup accelerator).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. A minor should certify a defined competency, and there is no statement of the innovation/entrepreneurship competency this minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes (e.g., identify and validate a venture opportunity, build a business model and financing plan, and apply lean-startup customer-discovery methods).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than an imbalance, but a prospective student cannot see the credit requirement or how required and elective credits are split.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credits and list which ENTR and related courses are required versus elective, with credits for each.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete: no plan of study, no credits, no description, and a pool that is identical to the Economics Minor's (both id-712) and includes a thin placeholder (BME 498, title 'ST:' with an empty description) and graduate-course references. A reader cannot tell what distinguishes this minor from the co-listed one.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct I&E minor page with a description and the curated ENTR-centered course list and credits, and fix the shared-pool duplication with the Economics Minor.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuine entrepreneurship courses are current and practice-oriented: ENTR 440 Lean Startup Accelerator uses lean-startup customer-discovery methodology, ENTR 320 Financing New Venture covers venture funding, ENTR 330 covers entrepreneurial strategy, MGMT 480 covers technology and innovation management, and MRKT 338 covers new product development. This is a strong, contemporary vocational core for an I&E minor. It is not exemplary because, with no defined structure and a pool shared with another minor, there is no guarantee a student assembles the intended sequence.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended I&E track (ENTR 210 -> ENTR 320/330 -> ENTR 440 capstone) so students deliberately move from opportunity recognition through financing to a launch-focused accelerator.

descriptionQuality.justification: The entrepreneurship and innovation descriptions are specific and practice-oriented (ENTR 440 on the lean-startup methodology and acquiring real customers, ENTR 320 on estimating and securing venture funding, MGMT 480 on strategic technology management). The score is held down by the BME 498 placeholder ('ST:' with an empty description) present in the shared pool.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the entrepreneurship courses are clear and sensible (ENTR 320/330 require ENTR 410 or ENTR 210). Internal consistency is reduced by eight dangling references, six of them graduate courses absent from the undergraduate catalog, carried over from the shared pool.

missingTopics.topic: Entrepreneurial finance depth (VC, valuation, cap tables)
missingTopics.rationale: ENTR 320 introduces venture financing, but there is no deeper course on venture-capital structures, startup valuation, or cap-table mechanics that founders increasingly need.
NJIT offers ENGR 350 Intellectual Property for Engineers, but no IP/commercialization course appears in this minor's pool, a notable gap for a technology-oriented I&E credential.
MRKT 338 New Product Design is present, but a dedicated design-thinking/customer-discovery methods course would strengthen the front end of the innovation process.
There is no course on social ventures or impact/mission-driven entrepreneurship, a rapidly growing area of entrepreneurship education.
The pool covers launch (ENTR 440 lean startup) but not the operations of scaling a growing venture or structured go-to-market strategy.

emergingTrends.rationale: Building on MGMT 480 and the lean-startup approach (ENTR 440), an AI/no-code venture-building elective would reflect how new firms are increasingly launched with AI tooling.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital platforms and marketplace business models
emergingTrends.rationale: Modern venture models built on platforms and marketplaces are not explicitly covered and are central to today's high-growth startups.
Green ventures and ESG-driven startups are a high-growth space that the minor could position for with a dedicated elective.
MGMT 480 anchors technology-and-innovation management; extending it to intrapreneurship would serve students who will innovate inside established firms.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-driven customer discovery and growth analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: Combining ENTR 440's lean-startup customer discovery with analytics (MGMT 216) would give students evidence-based venture-growth skills aligned with current practice.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).

De-duplicate the shared course pool: this minor and the Economics Minor resolve to an identical course list and metrics (both id-712), so curate an I&E-specific list centered on ENTR 210/320/330/440, MGMT 480, and MRKT 338.
Remove the six graduate-level dangling references (ACCT 615, FIN 600, MGMT 630, MIS 645, MIS 680, MRKT 620) and the BME 498 empty placeholder from the pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes for the entrepreneurship competency the minor certifies.
Add IP/technology-commercialization and a growth/scaling course, and modernize toward AI-enabled and sustainable entrepreneurship.

journalism-minor-448.name: Journalism Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A recognizable journalism craft spine is present (COM 338 The Newsroom, COM 336 Multimedia Journalism, COM 337 Photojournalism, COM 317 Advanced Composition, COM 324 Podcast Practicum, COM 390 Electronic Writing Workshop), and COM 336 sensibly builds on COM 240. However the 15-credit menu is diluted by many non-journalism COM/HIST/LIT electives, and with an empty requirementGroups there is no declared intro-to-advanced required path.
dimensions.suggestion: Designate a required reporting-craft core (e.g., COM 201 -> COM 338 The Newsroom -> COM 336 Multimedia Journalism) and mark the rest as electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. COM 336 Multimedia Journalism -> COM 240 New Media Technologies is a sensible internal build; the deepest chain (6) is otherwise driven by the ENGL/GER gateway pattern.
dimensions.suggestion: Where a course genuinely needs production skills (e.g., COM 336), state the media prerequisite explicitly rather than relying on the GER ladder.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The minor states a 15-credit total and every course is a uniform 3 credits, so the requirement is transparent and evenly weighted; creditReconcileDelta is null only because no term breakdown is parsed (a data caveat for a menu-style minor, not a defect).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish how the 15 credits divide between a required reporting core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course-level descriptions are present and substantive and the total credits are stated, but the program page has no description and no requirementGroups, so the plan of study is not legibly grouped.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief program description and group the menu into a required core and elective tiers.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The menu is notably current for a small program: multimedia and mobile storytelling (COM 336, COM 350 Digital Video Production), audio (COM 324 Podcast Practicum), visual reporting (COM 337 Photojournalism), and platform practice (COM 343 Social Media Analytics) all map to today's newsroom; gaps are data journalism, verification, and a dedicated ethics/law course.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a data-journalism and verification component so graduates can report with and about data and detect manipulated media.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and informative across the COM/HIST/LIT courses; none are flagged thin or missing.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references. Some clauses are verbose or redundant (COM 343 restates that 'COM 201 or PSY 201 provides necessary background'), and many cite a GER category rather than a specific course.

missingTopics.rationale: No practice-oriented ethics/law course is required; HIST 370 Legal Issues in the History of Media is historical, not professional standards.
There is no data/CAR course; a natural build on COM 343 Social Media Analytics.
COM 338 The Newsroom and COM 336 Multimedia Journalism cover production but not sourcing, verification, or public-records methods.
Specialized beat reporting - valuable at a STEM university - is absent from the menu.
Media economics and entrepreneurial/creator-economy journalism are not represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: LLM-assisted drafting, summarization, and synthetic-media detection should modernize COM 336 Multimedia Journalism and COM 355 Digital Media Futures.
Extend COM 343 Social Media Analytics into provenance and manipulated-media detection, now core to digital reporting.
COM 324 Podcast Practicum is a strong seed for newsletters, independent audio, and subscription models.
Pair COM 353 Applied Visual Communication with data visualization for explanatory journalism.
Extend COM 350 Digital Video Production toward platform-native vertical formats and social distribution.

topSuggestions: Add a required journalism ethics & media-law course.
Add data/computational journalism and verification/fact-checking skills.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and a required-core vs. elective structure.
Integrate AI-assisted reporting and synthetic-media detection into existing digital courses.
Trim non-journalism electives so the 15 credits center on reporting craft.

leadership-and-aerospace-studies-minor-3.name: Leadership and Aerospace Studies Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study or requirement groups are parsed at all (requirementGroups=0), so the minor presents no ordered sequence. The visible course list is a grab-bag that mixes the genuine AFROTC leadership ladder (AS 333 Leading People & Effective Communication -> AS 334 -> AS 443 National Security Affairs -> AS 444 Preparation for Active Duty) with unrelated courses that clearly bled in from adjacent catalog pages (BIOL 115, BIOL 150, MATH 107/108/110/111/138, THTR 480/483/484), leaving no coherent progression a student could follow.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit plan of study listing the required Aerospace Studies course sequence (AS 100 through AS 444) and remove the unrelated BIOL/MATH/THTR courses that do not belong to the minor.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The AS ladder that is present has a sensible depth (deepestPrereqChain=4: AS 333 -> AS 334 -> AS 443 -> AS 444) with clear 'or approval of the Professor of Aerospace Studies' escape clauses, but the entire foundational spine dangles: all 9 missing references (missingCoursesSample: AS 100, AS 111, AS 112, AS 200, AS 221, AS 222, AS 300, AS 400, GER 200) are the core AFROTC courses the upper AS courses build on, so the prerequisite graph has no valid base in the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries for the AS 100/111/112/200/221/222/300/400 core so the prerequisite chain resolves rather than dangling at its foundation.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data. This is especially notable for a leadership minor, whose entire purpose is developing named leadership and officership competencies that should be straightforward to articulate.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes (e.g., the leadership, communication, and national-security competencies a completer demonstrates) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit accounting whatsoever: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null, and creditReconcileDelta is null with zero requirement groups. Null reconciliation is an expected data-consistency caveat for a minor, but here a prospective student cannot see how many credits or courses the minor requires at all, and the labeled leadership-lab entries (AS 335/336) carry 0 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and the specific credit-bearing courses that count toward it.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page has no term structure, several core AS entries have empty descriptions (AS 301, AS 335, AS 336, AS 401) and truncated titles ('Leading People & Effective Com', 'Aeropsace Independent Study' [sic]), and unrelated courses appear alongside the AS content, making the entry read as a raw extract rather than a curated minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the page with labeled requirements, complete AS course descriptions, and corrected titles/spelling, and drop the non-AS courses.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As an Air Force ROTC commissioning-track minor, the present content is appropriate to its professional purpose: leadership and communication (AS 333/334), national security affairs (AS 443), and preparation for active duty (AS 444) are standard Professional Officer Course material, so 'industry currency' maps to current military-officer development rather than a technical field. What is dated is the framing: the visible national-security content does not yet reflect the newer operational domains (space, cyber) that now define Air and Space Force careers.
dimensions.suggestion: Refresh AS 443 National Security Affairs to foreground space-domain and cyber operations and joint all-domain command themes so the minor mirrors current Air and Space Force doctrine.

descriptionQuality.justification: Description quality is uneven: several core AS courses have empty descriptionExcerpt fields (AS 301, AS 335, AS 336, AS 401) and titles are truncated or misspelled, while only AS 333/334/443/444 carry substantive text, so a reader cannot tell what much of the minor covers.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: For the courses that are present the prerequisites are stated clearly and consistently (each upper AS course lists the prior AS course 'or approval of the Professor of Aerospace Studies'), but because the entire AS 100-400 base is missing from the catalog (missingCourseCount=9), the stated prerequisites point at courses a reader cannot look up.

missingTopics.rationale: AS 443 National Security Affairs covers traditional military-society issues but no visible course addresses the space or cyber domains that now anchor Air and Space Force officer careers.
The leadership sequence (AS 333/334) emphasizes management and communication but no dedicated ethics/officership-values course appears, a core commissioning competency.
AS 444 Preparation for Active Duty prepares officers for service, yet there is no explicit joint/allied operations content reflecting how modern missions are executed.
The management/communication focus of AS 333/334 would be strengthened by explicit content on leading diverse teams, a stated priority across the modern services.

emergingTrends.trend: Space Force and the space domain
emergingTrends.rationale: With the Space Force now a distinct service, the aerospace-studies content built around AS 443/444 should position cadets for space-domain careers, not just air.
National-security content in AS 443 should incorporate cyber and information-operations threats that dominate current defense planning.
Drone and autonomous-platform operations are reshaping air power and warrant coverage in the leadership/national-security sequence.
The leadership courses (AS 333/334) can modernize toward data-driven decision-making and decision-support tools now standard in military command.

topSuggestions: Publish an actual plan of study with the required AS course sequence and a stated credit total; the entry currently shows no requirement groups and no credits.
Add catalog entries for the missing AS 100/111/112/200/221/222/300/400 core so the prerequisite chain no longer dangles at its foundation.
Remove the unrelated BIOL/MATH/THTR courses and fill the empty AS course descriptions (AS 301/335/336/401) so the page reads as a curated minor.
Publish explicit leadership and officership learning outcomes for the minor.
Modernize the national-security and leadership content (AS 443/444) toward space, cyber, and joint all-domain operations.

legal-studies-minor-418.name: Legal Studies Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a minor the entry publishes no parsed plan of study (requirementGroups=0), so the 41 referenced courses read as an undifferentiated menu rather than a curated path. There is a genuine and distinctive law-and-technology spine -- IT 331 Privacy and Information Technology, IT 332 Digital Crime, IT 400 Information Technology and the Law, MGMT 290 Business Law I, IE 447 Legal Aspects of Engineering, and a deep HIST law bench (HIST 320 Law and Evidence, HIST 342 Civil Rights and Law, HIST 369 Law and Society, HIST 384 Invention and Regulation). Coherence is undercut, however, by heterogeneous science courses in the same pool (CHEM 222 Analytical Chemistry, FRSC 480 Forensic Microscopy, FRSC 359) whose fit to a legal-studies minor is unclear, suggesting the list pools the whole department rather than a legal-studies track.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a short required core (e.g. one foundational law survey plus a choice of law-in-context electives) and separate the law-relevant courses from the forensic/chemistry courses that appear to be included only because they share the department.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 6, driven by the IT security ladder (IT 120 -> IT 340 -> IT 430 Ethical Hacking) and the forensic science chain (CHEM -> FRSC 480), which over-constrain a minor whose law courses themselves need only ENGL 102 plus a 200-level History/Humanities GER. missingCourseCount is 2 -- GER 200 is a requirement-category placeholder rather than a real course, and HIST 312 is a genuine dangling reference. The law courses form a shallow, sensible gate; the deep chains come from science electives that a non-major minor is unlikely to reach.
dimensions.suggestion: Flag or trim the deep IT-security and forensic prerequisite chains for minor students, and resolve the dangling HIST 312 reference (or remove it if it is no longer offered).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview statement appear in the data. For an interdisciplinary minor drawing on history, IT, business, and engineering law, the absence of an outcomes statement leaves students no way to see what competency the minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 explicit learning outcomes (e.g. reading legal sources, reasoning about law-technology conflict, understanding regulatory/privacy frameworks) and a one-paragraph overview of the minor's purpose.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, there are no requirementGroups, and creditReconcileDelta is null -- expected for a minor and noted here as a data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but it does mean the catalog page shows no total credits, no required/elective split, and no cap, so a student cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they are distributed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and the required-vs-elective breakdown so students can plan it against their major.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and informative, but the entry is a flat course inventory with no term structure, no required core marked, and two placeholder/dangling references (GER 200, HIST 312). The law-technology strength is real but buried in a long undifferentiated list that also contains chemistry and forensic-lab courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Restructure the page into labeled sections (foundations, law-and-technology electives, law-in-history electives) and remove the science-lab courses that do not belong to a legal-studies path.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The technology-law emphasis is a genuine, current differentiator for a legal-studies minor at a technological university: IT 331 Privacy and Information Technology, IT 332 Digital Crime, IT 400 IT and the Law, IT 330 Computer Forensics, plus HIST 384 Invention and Regulation and HIST 370 Legal Issues in the History of Media give students exposure to the fastest-moving areas of law. The gap is the near-total absence of explicit intellectual-property/patent law and any AI-governance content, which a tech-facing legal minor should own.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an intellectual-property / patent-law course and an emerging AI-and-algorithmic-accountability offering to complete the technology-law profile.

descriptionQuality.justification: Across the sampled courses descriptions are consistently present and substantive -- HIST law courses, IT 331/332/400, and MGMT 290 each give a clear scope of topics, and none read as thin or placeholder. A few science electives (CHEM 222, FRSC 480) are well described but out of scope for the minor's stated subject.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit with grade gates (e.g. IT 430 'IT 340 or equivalent', CHEM 222 chained C-or-better conditions), but clarity is weakened by the GER 200 placeholder used as if it were a course, the dangling HIST 312, and the many law courses sharing the identical 'ENGL 102 + 200-level History/Humanities GER' string, which does not distinguish their real readiness levels.

missingTopics.topic: Intellectual property, patent, and copyright law
missingTopics.rationale: A technological university's legal minor should foreground IP; only Business Law I (MGMT 290) and IE 447 touch commercial/engineering law, with no dedicated IP or patent course among the 41 references.

missingTopics.topic: Constitutional law and civil liberties fundamentals
missingTopics.rationale: The pool is rich in applied and historical law (HIST 342, HIST 364) but lacks a foundational constitutional-law course to anchor students' understanding of rights and government power.

missingTopics.topic: Contracts, torts, and legal procedure basics
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond MGMT 290 Business Law I there is no systematic exposure to the core private-law doctrines (contracts, torts, procedure) that a legal-studies credential is expected to introduce.

missingTopics.topic: Legal research and writing as a skills course
missingTopics.rationale: HIST 395 Research Methods in Law and Society is close, but a dedicated legal-research/writing course (comparable to STS 300 Legal Reasoning offered elsewhere in the college) would give the minor a transferable skills anchor.
No mediation/arbitration/negotiation content appears, though it is now standard in undergraduate legal-studies curricula and pairs naturally with the program's business-law courses.

emergingTrends.rationale: IT 331 Privacy and IT 332 Digital Crime position the minor to add a course on AI regulation, liability, and algorithmic decision-making, now central to legal practice.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-privacy regulation (GDPR/CCPA) and cross-border data law
emergingTrends.rationale: IT 331 already teaches privacy foundations; a modern legal minor should extend it to the fast-evolving statutory privacy regimes that dominate technology-law careers.
With IT 330 Computer Forensics in the pool, the minor is well placed to add legal-tech/e-discovery content reflecting how litigation is actually conducted today.
The IT security ladder (IT 230, IT 330) provides the technical base for a law-facing course on breach liability, compliance, and cybercrime prosecution.
EVSC 335 Environmental Law and HIST 375 Legal Issues in Environmental History already exist; climate litigation and regulation are a growth area the minor could brand around.

topSuggestions: Publish an explicit required core, a total-credit requirement, and learning outcomes so the minor reads as a defined credential rather than a departmental course dump.
Add a dedicated intellectual-property/patent-law course -- the most conspicuous gap for a legal minor at a technological university.
Separate the genuine legal-studies electives from the forensic-science and chemistry courses that currently share the pool.
Resolve the dangling HIST 312 reference and convert the GER 200 placeholder into a labeled requirement category.
Introduce an AI-governance / algorithmic-accountability elective to modernize the strong existing technology-law bench.

literature-minor-448.name: Literature Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A workable literature spine is visible (LIT 230 Introduction to Literature, LIT 320 American Literature, LIT 340 Contemporary Literature, LIT 355 Poetry, LIT 378 Literature and Nature, LIT 325 Special Topics), giving a genre/period sampler. But the 15-credit menu is embedded in a large COM/HIST pool, requirementGroups is empty, and there is no period-distribution requirement to guarantee historical breadth.
dimensions.suggestion: Anchor the minor on LIT 230 plus a period/genre distribution requirement, and separate literature courses from the broader COM/HIST electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. Chains are shallow within the discipline (LIT 230 with an ENGL 102 co-requisite) and the deepest chain (6) reflects the institution-wide ENGL/GER gateway rather than literature-internal stacking.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep LIT electives reachable after a single gateway so a minor is not gated behind the full 300-level GER ladder.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: A 15-credit total is stated and all courses are uniform 3-credit offerings, making the requirement transparent and balanced; the null creditReconcileDelta only reflects the absence of a parsed term plan (a data caveat, not a defect).
dimensions.suggestion: State how the 15 credits split between a required introduction/core and distribution electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive and total credits are stated, but the program page has no description and no requirementGroups, so period/genre organization is not legible on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description and group the menu by period and genre so breadth is visible.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The menu shows healthy genre range (LIT 355 Poetry, LIT 340 Contemporary Literature, LIT 378 Literature and Nature) and a modern digital touch (COM 369 Digital Poetry) relevant to writing, publishing, and content careers; it lacks literary theory, world/postcolonial literature, and a defined digital-humanities thread.
dimensions.suggestion: Add literary theory and world literature, and formalize a digital-humanities option that pairs literary study with computational text analysis.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled LIT/COM/HIST descriptions are present and informative; none are flagged thin or missing.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; LIT 230's ENGL 102 co-requisite is clearly noted. Most upper electives cite a GER category rather than a specific literature prerequisite, a mild ambiguity.

missingTopics.rationale: No dedicated theory/criticism course is visible, yet it is foundational for a literature minor's interpretive rigor.
The visible pool is American/contemporary-centric (LIT 320, LIT 340) with no world literature in translation.
Medieval/Renaissance/Romantic coverage is missing; HIST 329 Dante is a history course, not a literature offering.
A creative-writing thread within LIT is absent, even though COM 390 Electronic Writing Workshop hints at demand.
A natural fit at a STEM university and a build on COM 369 Digital Poetry, but not present as a defined option.

emergingTrends.rationale: Text mining and computational analysis pair literature with NJIT's technical strengths; extend LIT courses with a DH option.
LIT 378 Literature and Nature is a strong seed to grow into a fuller ecocriticism sequence.
LIT 386 Science Fiction connects literary study to technology and climate futures relevant to this campus.
COM 369 Digital Poetry points toward born-digital and interactive narrative forms.
Diversifying the canon beyond American/contemporary literature reflects where the discipline is heading.

topSuggestions: Require a literary-theory/criticism course and add world/postcolonial literature for canon breadth.
Guarantee historical-period distribution from pre-1800 through the contemporary.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and a core-vs-distribution structure.
Formalize a digital-humanities thread building on LIT genre courses and COM 369 Digital Poetry.
Expand environmental-humanities offerings from LIT 378 Literature and Nature.

manufacturing-engineering-technology-min.name: Manufacturing Engineering Technology Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan-of-study terms are parsed (requirementGroups 0), normal for a minor, and the 16-credit pool is strongly manufacturing-aligned: MNET 215 materials/metrology, MNET 300 machining, MNET 303 CAD/CAM, MNET 318 manufacturing process design, MNET 405 NC machine tools, MNET 416 production scheduling, MNET 420 quality systems, plus ME 215 materials, MET 307/308 plastics, CHE 415 3D printing and IE 224 production. It reads as a coherent manufacturing menu, though it also carries off-theme entries (ENTR courses, MIT gerontology, ENGR drone/service learning) shared verbatim with the sibling Grand Challenges minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Trim the off-theme entries and declare a required manufacturing core plus a curated elective list.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 7 with sound manufacturing chains (MNET 405 depth 4 on ME 215/MNET 303, MNET 318 depth 2 on MNET 303, MNET 416/420 depth 4 on MNET 315). Both dangling references (missingCourseCount 2: CPT 325, ENGR 290) are genuine broken links and are off-theme for a manufacturing minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the dangling references CPT 325 and ENGR 290 (both off-theme carryovers from the shared pool).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No minor-level learning outcomes appear in the provided data; only a course pool is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit learning outcomes describing the manufacturing competencies a completer should gain.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The stated total is 16 credits, but with no parsed terms creditReconcileDelta is null, so the required-vs-elective split cannot be verified; the null delta is a data-consistency caveat for a minor rather than a defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the required-vs-elective credit breakdown so the 16 credits reconcile transparently.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The 16-credit total is stated and the manufacturing courses are legible, but there is no plan of study and the sample course list is identical to the Grand Challenges minor (both id-650), including off-theme ENTR/MIT/ENGR entries that dilute the manufacturing focus.
dimensions.suggestion: Differentiate the list from the Grand Challenges minor and remove off-theme courses so the manufacturing identity is clear.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The pool reflects current manufacturing-technology practice well: machining (MNET 300), CAD/CAM (MNET 303), manufacturing process design (MNET 318), NC/CNC machine tools (MNET 405), production scheduling (MNET 416), quality systems/SPC/ISO (MNET 420), plastics processing (MET 307/308) and 3D printing (CHE 415). The main gaps are automation/robotics, Industry 4.0/IIoT and deeper additive manufacturing.
dimensions.suggestion: Add automation/robotics and an Industry 4.0/IIoT-and-digital-twin option (IE 449/455 are available; build on MNET 303/318/416).

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific (MNET 300 on speeds/feeds and tooling, MNET 405 on mill/lathe CNC programming and economics, MNET 420 on SPC, TQM, ISO and reliability, MET 308 on extrusion and injection/blow/compression molding); only a few cross-listed entries are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: The manufacturing chains are explicit and consistent (MNET 405 'ME 215 and MNET 303', MNET 318 'MNET 303', MNET 416/420 'MNET 315'), but two genuine dangling references (CPT 325, ENGR 290) carried over from the shared pool and several instructor-permission/'None' entries reduce overall clarity.

missingTopics.rationale: Machining and CAD/CAM are well covered (MNET 300/303/405) but automated cells and robotics are absent; IE 449 Industrial Robotics and IE 455 Robotics and PLCs exist in the department and could join the pool.
Only CHE 415 Introduction to 3D Printing represents additive; production-scale additive and design-for-AM warrant a dedicated follow-on.
Production scheduling (MNET 416) and process design (MNET 318) are classical; connected, sensor-driven smart-factory content is missing.
MNET 215 touches materials/metrology, but a dedicated GD&T/metrology course is a standard manufacturing competency not clearly represented.
Quality is covered by MNET 420, but lean/value-stream and continuous-improvement methods are absent from the pool.

emergingTrends.trend: Smart manufacturing / Industry 4.0 and digital twin
emergingTrends.rationale: Connecting the shop floor with sensors and simulating it digitally is the defining manufacturing trend; MNET 318/416 and MNET 303 CAD/CAM are a foundation to build on.
Metal and polymer additive are moving from prototyping to production; extending CHE 415 toward DfAM would keep the minor current.
Cobots and automated material handling are reshaping production lines; IE 449/455 would position graduates for automation roles.
Machine data now drives SPC and maintenance decisions; MNET 315/420 with ENGR 301 could anchor a predictive-quality elective.
Energy and material efficiency are increasingly required in manufacturing operations and would broaden the minor's process-design core.

topSuggestions: Differentiate the course pool from the Grand Challenges minor: the two id-650 minors carry an identical sample course list, so the manufacturing minor should drop the off-theme ENTR/MIT-gerontology/ENGR-drone entries.
Add automation/robotics (IE 449/455) and an Industry 4.0/IIoT-and-digital-twin option (build on MNET 303/318/416) to modernize.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and a required-vs-elective plan, both absent from the data.
Deepen additive manufacturing beyond the CHE 415 intro and add lean/metrology-GD&T coverage.
Resolve the dangling references CPT 325 and ENGR 290.

materials-engineering-minor-580.name: Materials Engineering Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), and more seriously the captured course pool does not represent materials engineering well: it is chemistry-dominated (CHEM 125/126/222/243/336/340/360/361/473/474/412/480/491) with only BME 304 Material Fundamentals and CHE 444 touching materials directly, and it is byte-for-byte identical to the Chemistry Minor (both from page 580, same metrics: 83 referenced, 27 missing, depth 8). A materials-engineering minor would be expected to center on structure-property relationships, characterization, and material classes (metals/ceramics/polymers/composites), none of which the pool foregrounds. The extraction appears to have attached a shared department listing to both minors.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the shared chemistry pool with a genuine materials-engineering course set built around MTEN 201 Introductory Principles of Materials Engineering, BME 304, and courses on structure-property relationships, characterization and specific material classes.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The referenced chains are sensible where they exist (deepestPrereqChain 8 with grade conditions), but prerequisite health is undermined by missingCourseCount 27 and by the fact that the resolved courses are largely chemistry rather than materials. The missingCoursesSample (ACCT 200, BME 300, CE 300, CHE 300, CHEM 300, CPT 300, ECE 200/300, EM 600, ENE 200, ENGR 200/211, ENTR 400, FIN 200) is dominated by non-materials placeholders, so the graph does not describe a materials-minor pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the prerequisite graph around actual materials courses (e.g., MTEN 201 to advanced materials electives) so the 27 dangling non-materials references disappear.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a materials-engineering minor this is a real gap because the minor should certify a specific competency in materials selection and structure-property analysis, and no such statement is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 outcomes stating what the materials minor certifies (e.g., ability to relate microstructure to properties, select materials for an application, interpret characterization data).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits and creditReconcileDelta are all null, a data-consistency caveat rather than a proven imbalance. As presented, a student cannot see the minor's credit requirement or distribution, and the mismatch between the label (materials) and the chemistry-heavy pool compounds the uncertainty.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement and list the specific materials courses that count, so the minor's load and content are transparent and match its title.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured page is incomplete and, unusually, mislabeled relative to its content: no plan of study, no credit total, no description, and a chemistry-dominated pool identical to the Chemistry Minor. A reader cannot tell what the materials minor actually requires or how it differs from the chemistry minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a distinct materials-minor page with its own description, a materials-centered required/elective list, and a credit total, and fix the parser so it stops sharing the chemistry minor's listing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As captured, the pool does not surface the core materials-engineering topics employers expect — crystallography and phase equilibria, mechanical behavior, characterization (microscopy/diffraction), and functional/electronic materials — relying instead on chemistry courses and a single materials-fundamentals course (BME 304). This makes the minor's currency for materials careers weak as presented, notwithstanding the underlying discipline's strong relevance.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce modern materials content (characterization methods, electronic/energy materials, polymers/composites) so the minor reflects contemporary materials-engineering practice rather than general chemistry.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled descriptions are present and specific (26 of 26, none thin), but they describe chemistry courses far more than materials-engineering courses (only BME 304 Material Fundamentals and CHE 444 are clearly materials-oriented); description quality is fine, but the courses described are largely off-topic for the minor.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Individual prerequisites carry grade conditions, but with 27 dangling references — mostly non-materials placeholders (BME 300, CE 300, ECE 300, EM 600, ENTR 400) — and a pool that does not match the minor's title, the actual entry requirements for a materials minor are effectively unclear.

missingTopics.topic: Structure-property relationships and materials science fundamentals
missingTopics.rationale: Only BME 304 Material Fundamentals touches this; a materials minor must center on the microstructure-to-property core (crystallography, defects, phase diagrams) that the current chemistry-heavy pool omits.
No characterization course appears, yet hands-on characterization is the defining laboratory skill of materials engineering.
The pool lacks any course on deformation, fracture and fatigue — essential for a minor that should let students reason about materials performance.

missingTopics.topic: Material classes: polymers, ceramics, composites and electronic materials
missingTopics.rationale: Beyond BME 304 and CHE 444, the minor has no coverage of specific engineering material families, the practical vocabulary of the field.

missingTopics.topic: A correctly scoped, materials-centered course pool
missingTopics.rationale: Because the listing is identical to the Chemistry Minor and chemistry-dominated, the most important fix is replacing it with genuine materials courses (starting from MTEN 201).

emergingTrends.rationale: Battery, fuel-cell and photovoltaic materials are among the fastest-growing materials-engineering areas; an energy-materials elective would modernize a minor currently built on general chemistry.
Data-driven materials discovery (ML for property prediction) is reshaping the field; building on the pool's physical-chemistry base could support such an elective.
3D-printed metals and polymers are transforming materials processing; a processing/AM component would give the minor contemporary relevance.
Circular-economy pressure is driving demand for recyclable and bio-based materials, a natural modern theme for a materials minor.
Semiconductors and functional materials underpin the electronics and energy sectors; explicit coverage would connect the minor to high-demand roles.

topSuggestions: Fix the data-extraction conflation and mislabeling: the pool is identical to the Chemistry Minor and chemistry-dominated — replace it with a genuine materials-engineering course set centered on MTEN 201 and BME 304.
Publish the materials minor's actual required/elective structure and total credits (all currently null).
Add the missing materials-engineering core (structure-property relationships, characterization, mechanical behavior, material classes) so the content matches the minor's title.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes describing the materials competency the minor certifies.
Introduce a modern elective (energy/electronic materials or materials informatics) to give the minor current-practice relevance.

mathematical-biology-minor-480.name: Mathematical Biology Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No formal requirement groups are provided (requirementGroups=0), but here the shared id-480 course pool is genuinely well-matched to the discipline: a modeling core (MATH 372 Population Biology, MATH 373 Introduction to Mathematical Biology, MATH 374 Stochastic and Discrete Models in Biology, MATH 430/431 Computational Neuroscience) is supported by probability, linear algebra, and numerical methods and a broad biology electives set, so the referenced courses cohere around mathematical biology. The main incoherence is that the pool is shared verbatim with the Applied Statistics and Computational Mathematics minors, so off-topic finance/actuarial courses (MATH 346/347, 432/433, 441) are mixed in.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a curated plan that separates the mathematical-biology modeling core from the biology electives and drops the shared finance/actuarial courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: missingCourseCount=0, so there are no dangling references; deepestPrereqChain=7 reflects the source courses' catalog depth rather than an internal requirement of a short minor. The prerequisite picture is clean.
dimensions.suggestion: State the calculus and introductory-biology entry prerequisites so students see the on-ramp.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data for this minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes describing the biological-modeling and quantitative competencies the minor certifies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits=16.0 is published, but with no term plan or summed credit figure the required-versus-elective split is not shown and creditReconcileDelta is null (a data-consistency caveat expected for an unstructured minor record).
dimensions.suggestion: Show how the stated 16 credits split into a mathematical-biology modeling core versus biology electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity

The gaps are the absence of a bioinformatics/genomics course and of a biostatistics course.

A fast-growing quantitative-biology area currently absent from the pool.

Dynamics on biological networks is an expanding modeling frontier that builds on the existing courses.

A few mathematical-biology courses (MATH 372/373/374, 430/431) sit in the pool as strays.

Market-relevant directions, including emerging digital-asset modeling, that would strengthen graduate employability.
