minor-in-biomedical-engineering-technolo.name: Minor in Biomedical Engineering Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so there is no captured plan of study, required core, or elective rules. A genuine applied-BME spine is visible in the pool (BME 111 Physiology -> BME 210 biosignal processing -> BMET 320 Applied Biomedical Data Acquisition -> BMET 415 Biomedical Mechatronics, plus BME 303/304 biomaterials and BME 472 FDA regulation), which is coherent, but it is buried in a much larger unrelated set (nine ACCT accounting courses, FIN 218/315 corporate finance, MGMT 216) that appears drawn from a shared MTSM pool. Without a defined structure the intended progression cannot be judged.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit minor structure that names the required BMET core (e.g., BME 111, BME 210, BMET 320) and a curated technical elective list, and remove the accounting/finance courses that do not belong in a biomedical-technology minor.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains on the biomedical courses are well-formed with explicit grade conditions (e.g., BME 452 requires BME 302, BME 304, MATH 222, MATH 279/333, and BME 321; BMET 320 lists a multi-course foundation), and only two dangling references (ENGR 211, ENGR 312). The deepest chain of 8 is driven by non-BMET courses (CET 435) rather than the biomedical spine, whose chains are moderate. With no requirement groups, however, the graph cannot be tied to an actual minor pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: After defining the required/elective structure, confirm a non-BME minor student can reach BMET 320/415 without hidden deep BME-major chains, and surface the intended entry point (BME 111, which has no prerequisites).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a technology minor that is meant to certify hands-on device competency to non-majors, the absence of any stated outcome is a real gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the BMET minor certifies (e.g., operate and interpret biomedical instrumentation, understand medical-device materials and the FDA regulatory pathway, and acquire and process physiological signals).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than an imbalance, but a prospective student cannot see the credit requirement or how required and elective credits are split.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credits (typically 15-18) and list which BME/BMET courses are required versus elective, with credits for each.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete: no plan of study (requirementGroups empty), no credit total, no description, and a pool that opens with roughly nine accounting courses before reaching the biomedical content. A reader cannot discern what the BMET minor consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description and the required/elective BMET course list with credits, and fix the parsing so accounting/finance courses do not appear on a biomedical-technology minor.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuine BMET courses are notably current and vocational for a technology credential: BME 472 FDA Regulation of Medical Devices covers design documentation and quality management systems, BMET 320 covers MRI/ultrasound/X-ray imaging and physiological sensors (EKG, motion trackers), BMET 415 covers biomedical mechatronics, and BMET 440 provides experiential learning. This aligns well with medical-device industry roles. The score is not higher only because, absent a defined structure, there is no guarantee a minor student reaches these advanced courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern track (imaging/instrumentation plus regulatory/quality) so minor students deliberately reach BMET 320, BMET 415, and BME 472 rather than stopping at introductory physiology.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled biomedical descriptions are present, specific, and substantive: BMET 320 enumerates imaging modalities and physiological sensors, BME 472 details the FDA regulatory and quality-management process, and BME 452 describes biomaterial properties and failure modes. None of the relevant courses read as thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the biomedical courses are stated clearly with grade conditions and explicit multi-course lists (e.g., BMET 320's foundation set, BME 452's five-course prerequisite). Alternatives are sensible (BMET 415 accepts one of several math courses). The main caveat is that some chains are complex for a minor, which the absent structure does not clarify.

missingTopics.topic: Capstone medical-device design and instrumentation lab
missingTopics.rationale: The pool has BMET 320 data acquisition and BMET 415 mechatronics but no explicit integrative device-design capstone; a hands-on design course would convert the theory spine into demonstrable competency.
A biomedical-technology minor should cover the maintenance, calibration, and management of clinical equipment, which no course in the captured pool addresses.
BME 472 covers FDA regulation, but ISO 13485 quality systems and design controls are not surfaced, and IE 461 Product Quality Assurance in the pool is not linked as a device-quality pathway.
BME 210 introduces biosignal processing, but there is no dedicated course on analyzing specific physiological signals, a core technician competency.
BME 304 and BME 452 are theory-oriented; a laboratory course on materials testing and biocompatibility evaluation is missing.

emergingTrends.rationale: BMET 320 already covers EKG and motion sensors; modernizing toward wearable, continuous, and telehealth monitoring reflects where medical-device practice is heading.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML in medical imaging and diagnostics
emergingTrends.rationale: BMET 320 covers MRI/ultrasound/X-ray; adding machine-learning image analysis (ENGR 301 data science could feed it) matches the rapid adoption of AI diagnostics.
Connected medical devices demand security competency, which is absent from the pool despite the mechatronics/data-acquisition focus.

emergingTrends.trend: Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and AI regulation
emergingTrends.rationale: BME 472 covers FDA hardware regulation; extending to software and AI-based device pathways reflects the fastest-growing area of medical-device regulation.

emergingTrends.trend: Additive manufacturing of implants and prosthetics
emergingTrends.rationale: Biomaterials (BME 304/452) plus prototyping (ENGR 320 in the safety pool) could support a 3D-printing/bioprinting elective aligned with current device manufacturing trends.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits, which are absent (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).
Curate the pool to biomedical courses and remove the roughly nine accounting/finance courses (ACCT 115-435, FIN 218/315) that appear pulled from a shared MTSM course pool.
Add an integrative medical-device design or instrumentation lab so the strong BME/BMET theory spine yields hands-on, demonstrable competency.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the BMET minor certifies.
Modernize toward digital health: wearables/IoMT, AI in medical imaging, and Software-as-a-Medical-Device regulation, building on BMET 320 and BME 472.

minor-in-drones-and-robotics-686.name: Minor in Drones and Robotics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No plan of study or requirement structure is published (requirementGroups is empty), and the 60-course pool is undifferentiated and shared verbatim with the Engineering Innovation Minor. Within that pool the genuinely on-topic courses -- ENGR 423 (Drone Science), ENGR 424 (Robotics Science), ENGR 330 (Microcontrollers/IoT), IE 455 (Robotics & PLCs), CS 440 (Computer Vision), and ECE 431/432 (Feedback Control / Advanced Control & Robotics) -- are buried among clearly unrelated courses (CET 435 temporary structures, BME 472 FDA regulation, CHE 312 chemical process safety), so there is no coherent drone/robotics pathway a student can follow.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a defined drone/robotics course list and sequence (e.g. required ENGR 330 + ENGR 423 + ENGR 424, with electives from CS 440, ECE 431/432, IE 455) and remove the unrelated construction/biomedical/chemical courses from the minor's pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Only four codes are dangling (ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 231A), and the robotics-relevant chain is sound (ECE 231 -> ECE 232 -> ECE 431 -> ECE 432; ENGR 424 gated by a programming course). However the reported deepestPrereqChain of 8 is inflated by off-topic courses (e.g. CET 435 depth 8, CE 414 depth 7) rather than by drone/robotics prerequisites, so the metric overstates the minor's true depth.
dimensions.suggestion: Once the pool is scoped to drone/robotics courses, document the intended entry prerequisites for non-ECE students (e.g. the control courses' need for ECE 232/MATH 337) so the real gating is visible.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes and no requirement structure are provided at all -- the page offers neither objectives nor a plan. For a catalog minor this is a substantial omission.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit minor-level outcomes (e.g. 'model and control a UAV/ground-robot platform' and 'apply computer vision to a robotic task') together with the required-credit structure.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit balance cannot be assessed: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null, and creditReconcileDelta is null because no plan exists (a data-consistency caveat inherent to how this minor was captured). A minor normally specifies a fixed credit count and required/elective split, none of which is present.
dimensions.suggestion: State the minor's total credit requirement and its required-vs-elective breakdown (typical engineering minors are 15-18 credits) so students can plan it.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: As captured, the page has no plan of study, no stated credits, and a course pool that is both polluted with unrelated subjects and identical to a second minor sharing the 686 id -- a reader cannot tell what the Drones and Robotics minor actually requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the minor its own dedicated, curated course list separate from the Engineering Innovation Minor, with a required-credit summary at the top of the page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The on-topic content that is present is genuinely current: ENGR 423 (Drone Science), ENGR 424 (Robotics Science), CS 440 (Computer Vision), ENGR 330 (IoT/microcontrollers), and ECE 431/432 (control/robotics) map directly to modern autonomous-systems practice. The relevance is real even though it must be extracted from a cluttered pool.
dimensions.suggestion: Feature the autonomy stack -- perception (CS 440), control (ECE 431/432), and platforms (ENGR 423/424) -- as the minor's spine and add an autonomy/motion-planning element to match current UAV/robotics roles.

descriptionQuality.justification: The drone/robotics course descriptions are specific and informative -- ENGR 423 (quadrotor kinematics/dynamics, flight control), ENGR 424 (robot sensing, motion control, motion planning), CS 440 (computer-vision methods), ECE 431 (feedback control, Laplace/state-space, stability) -- with no thin text; the pool's non-robotics descriptions are also complete.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are clearly and consistently stated where relevant (ECE 431 requires ECE 333 or ECE 232 + MATH 337; ECE 432 requires ECE 431), but because no plan defines which courses count toward the minor, students cannot see which of these prerequisite chains they must actually satisfy.

missingTopics.rationale: ECE 431 (Introduction to Feedback Control Systems) sits in the pool but is not clearly required; control is indispensable to drones and robotics.

missingTopics.topic: Autonomy: motion planning, state estimation, and SLAM
missingTopics.rationale: ENGR 424 introduces motion planning, but dedicated planning/estimation/SLAM content -- core to autonomous drones/robots -- is not represented as a requirement.
ENGR 330 (microcontrollers/IoT) is a start, but real-time firmware and sensor/actuator integration for flight/ground platforms are not developed.
Robotic perception (CS 440) and drone control (ENGR 423) presume sensor fusion/estimation that no course in the intended set explicitly covers.

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 440 (Computer Vision) and ENGR 424 (Robotics Science) point the minor toward ML-based perception and autonomy, the defining frontier of drones and robotics.

emergingTrends.trend: UAV regulation, BVLOS operations, and airspace integration
emergingTrends.rationale: ENGR 423 (Drone Science) would be strengthened by regulatory/operations content as commercial drone use and BVLOS rules expand rapidly.
Coordinated multi-agent drone/robot operation is an emerging area the ENGR 423/424 base could extend into.
Industry-standard simulation-to-hardware workflows would make the minor's projects reflect current robotics engineering practice.

topSuggestions: Publish a curated, dedicated course list and sequence for the minor (required ENGR 330/423/424 plus scoped electives) and remove unrelated construction/biomedical/chemical courses from the pool.
State the minor's total credit requirement and required-vs-elective split (currently null).
Separate this minor's course pool from the Engineering Innovation Minor, which currently shares the identical 686 course list.
Make a feedback-control course (ECE 431) an explicit requirement and add autonomy/motion-planning content.
Publish explicit minor-level learning outcomes for UAV/robot modeling, control, and perception.

minor-in-safety-engineering-686.name: Minor in Safety Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so the minor has no captured plan of study, required core, or elective rules. The 60 referenced courses read as a broad, shared engineering-technology pool: only a few are genuinely safety-focused (CET 423 Construction Safety, CHE 312 Chemical Process Safety, CE 414 Engineered Construction, ENGR 430 Engineering for Quality and Reliability), while most are unrelated (BME 210 biosignal processing, CS 440 Computer Vision, ECE 231/232 circuits, ENGR 423/424 drones and robotics, ENTR entrepreneurship). Without a defined structure the progression a safety-minor student would follow cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit minor structure with a required safety core (e.g., a hazard-analysis course plus CET 423 and CHE 312) and a curated elective list, removing the non-safety courses that appear pulled from a shared engineering-technology pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains among the referenced courses are well-formed with consistent grade conditions (e.g., ECE 231 -> 232 -> 431 -> 432, CET 331 -> 435), and only four dangling references (ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 231A), which are placeholders or absent labs. However, the deepest chain of 8 is very deep for a minor and is driven by senior major courses (ECE 432 at depth 8, CET 435 at depth 8); with no requirement groups there is no evidence a minor-only student could reach the safety electives without long major-only prerequisite chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Once the required/elective structure is defined, verify that a non-major can satisfy the chosen safety electives without hidden deep chains, and prefer safety courses with shallow prerequisites (e.g., CET 423, which has none).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data. For a minor this is a meaningful gap, because a minor exists to certify a defined competency to non-majors, and there is no statement of what safety-engineering competency this minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the safety-engineering minor certifies (e.g., ability to perform hazard identification and risk assessment, apply OSHA/industry safety codes, and evaluate system reliability).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than proof of imbalance, but as presented a prospective student cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they are distributed between required and elective safety courses.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement (typically 15-18 credits) and list exactly which safety courses count and how many are required versus elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is essentially incomplete: no plan of study (requirementGroups empty), no credit total, no program description, and a course pool that blends a handful of safety courses with many unrelated engineering-technology courses. A reader cannot tell what the safety-engineering minor actually consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description, the required and elective safety course list, and total credits, and fix the parsing so only genuine safety-engineering courses appear.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuinely safety-related courses in the pool are current and vocational (CET 423 addresses OSHA construction safety, CHE 312 covers chemical process safety and quantitative risk, ENGR 430 covers quality and reliability engineering), so a well-chosen selection is marketable. The score is held to adequate because the safety-discipline coverage is narrow (dominated by construction and chemical-process contexts) and there is no explicit modern system-safety, risk-analytics, or fire-protection content, and no structure guaranteeing a student reaches the safety courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern safety track (system safety and quantitative risk assessment, plus an EHS or reliability option) so students deliberately acquire in-demand safety-engineering skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled course descriptions are present, specific, and substantive: CHE 312 details chemical-plant accident impact and societal/individual risk, CET 423 cites OSHA compliance, and ENGR 430 explains quality and reliability tools. None read as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the referenced courses are stated clearly with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., ENGR 424 'BME 210 or CS 101 or CS 106 or CS 113 or CS 115', COM 313 with ENGL 102 and a GER condition). The main caveat is that several chains reach depth 8, which is deep for a minor and unexplained by the absent structure.

missingTopics.topic: System safety engineering and hazard analysis
missingTopics.rationale: The pool has CHE 312 and CET 423 but no dedicated course on systematic hazard analysis (HAZOP, FMEA, fault-tree/event-tree analysis) or probabilistic risk assessment, which is the defining methodology of safety engineering.
Only CET 423 covers OSHA, and only in a construction context; a general industrial/occupational safety and health course is absent from the captured pool.
A core safety-engineering subfield is entirely missing from the pool, leaving no coverage of fire dynamics, suppression, or explosion prevention.

missingTopics.topic: Ergonomics and human factors in safety
missingTopics.rationale: The pool surfaces no human-factors safety course even though NJIT offers IE 355 Human Factors; occupational injury prevention is central to safety engineering.
ENGR 430 touches quality and reliability, but there is no course dedicated to quantitative reliability modeling or risk quantification, a standard component of a modern safety-engineering credential.

emergingTrends.rationale: ENGR 330 (microcontrollers and IoT) and ENGR 301/CS 301 (data science) are already in the pool and could feed a predictive safety-monitoring elective that reflects the shift to instrumented, real-time hazard detection.

emergingTrends.trend: Predictive maintenance and prognostics with machine learning
emergingTrends.rationale: CS 301 Introduction to Data Science and ENGR 301 could anchor a safety-focused predictive-maintenance course, matching industry's move toward ML-driven failure prediction.

emergingTrends.trend: Functional safety of autonomous and robotic systems
emergingTrends.rationale: With ENGR 423 Drone Science, ENGR 424 Robotics, and ECE 431/432 control systems in the pool, safe operation of autonomous machinery (human-robot collaboration safety) is an emerging safety concern the minor could address.

emergingTrends.trend: Environmental, health and safety (EHS) and sustainability
emergingTrends.rationale: Growing regulatory and ESG pressure makes an EHS/sustainability emphasis, connected to CHE 312 process safety, an increasingly expected part of safety education.
Controls courses (ECE 431/432) are present; a standards-based functional-safety module would align the minor with automotive and industrial-controls hiring expectations.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits, which are entirely absent (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).

Curate the course pool to safety-relevant courses and remove the mis-scoped ones (BME signal processing, CS 440 computer vision, ECE circuits, ENGR 423/424 drones and robotics, ENTR entrepreneurship) that appear pulled from a shared engineering-technology pool.
Add a dedicated hazard-analysis/risk-assessment course (HAZOP, FMEA, fault-tree) - the defining competency of safety engineering, currently absent from the pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the safety-engineering minor certifies.
Broaden coverage beyond construction and chemical-process safety to general occupational safety, fire protection, and human-factors/ergonomics.

mobile-and-web-minor-266.name: Mobile and Web Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 32-course pool is not curated to 'mobile and web': it is the full IS/IT inventory and includes nine game courses (IT 265/266/270/286/366/382/383/466/487) and infrastructure courses (IT 340 System Administration, IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks, IT 490 Systems Integration) that have no clear mobile/web rationale. With no plan-of-study grid (requirementGroups=0), the minor reads as an unfiltered menu rather than a coherent web+mobile progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the generic pool with a curated sequence (IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 219/IS 421 for web, IS 322 for mobile, IS 331 for data) and remove the unrelated game and sysadmin courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: References are clean (missingCourseCount=0) with no dangling codes, but the deepest chain is 7 and marquee courses sit deep (IS 465 at depth 7, IS 421/IT 340/IT 420 at depth 5, IS 322/IS 390 at depth 4). For a 15-credit minor many of these are unreachable without the underlying IS/IT major sequence, so the advertised pool overstates what a minor student can actually take.
dimensions.suggestion: Provide a reachable low-prerequisite path (IS 117 and IT 202 at depth 0-2) and label which advanced courses require major-level prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data; only course descriptions exist. For a skills-oriented web/mobile credential this omission is a real finding because employers evaluate this minor on concrete build competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as 'design and deploy a responsive web application', 'build and ship a mobile app', and 'model and query an application database'.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no term grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a transparency point, not a reconciliation defect: statedTotalCredits=15 is given but with no required-versus-elective breakdown. Uniform 3-credit courses keep the arithmetic clean but the page does not show how much of the 15 is prescribed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure (required web+mobile core versus electives) so students see the balance explicitly.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The most visible clarity problem is that the displayed course pool is byte-identical to the two Information Technology minors and contradicts this minor's own title, listing game-design and system-administration courses under 'Mobile and Web'. A reader cannot tell what the minor is about from its course list, which is a genuine presentation defect even though individual descriptions are legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Align the displayed course list with the minor's title so game/sysadmin courses are removed and web/mobile courses are foregrounded.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuinely relevant courses (IS 117/218/219 website development, IS 421 Advanced Web Applications, IS 322 Mobile Applications, IS 331 Databases, IS 373 CMS, IT 202 Internet Applications) cover real ground but skew toward traditional website development: there is no modern JavaScript framework (React/Vue), no explicit REST/GraphQL API design, no web/mobile security, and no cloud deployment or cross-platform mobile (React Native/Flutter).
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize the core toward component-based front-end frameworks, API-first backends, mobile cross-platform frameworks, and cloud deployment/security.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and informative across sampled courses (IS 218 Building Web Applications, IS 219 Advanced Website Development, IS 322 Mobile Applications), with no thin or placeholder text observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated explicitly and consistently (e.g., IS 218/219 require '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and missingCourseCount=0 confirms no dangling references.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool's web courses (IS 117/218/219) are website-development oriented with no explicit React/Vue/Angular, component architecture, or single-page-application content that defines current front-end work.
No course in the pool addresses authentication, OWASP risks, secure APIs, or HTTPS/TLS, despite security being non-negotiable for anything deployed to the web or an app store.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud deployment and DevOps for web
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340 (sysadmin) and IT 420 (networks) are generic infrastructure; there is no cloud-native deployment, containers, CI/CD, serverless, or CDN content that modern web/mobile delivery relies on.
API-first design is implied inside IS 218/421 but never taught explicitly, yet decoupled front-ends and mobile clients depend on well-designed service APIs.

missingTopics.topic: Cross-platform mobile and Progressive Web Apps
missingTopics.rationale: IS 322 is the only mobile course; there is no React Native/Flutter or PWA content, which is exactly the bridge between the 'mobile' and 'web' halves of this minor.

emergingTrends.rationale: Nothing in the pool positions students for integrating LLM APIs, chat UIs, or AI copilots, now a baseline expectation for web/mobile products and for developer productivity.

emergingTrends.trend: Progressive Web Apps, WebAssembly, and edge computing
emergingTrends.rationale: The web platform is moving toward near-native performance and edge delivery; the minor's website-centric courses should incorporate PWA and edge patterns.
IS 373 teaches traditional CMS; the field has shifted to headless CMS, static generation, and serverless functions that this minor could adopt.
WCAG accessibility and GDPR/CCPA privacy are legal and market requirements for shipped web/mobile apps, yet no course covers them.

emergingTrends.trend: Mobile-first, API-first convergence of web and mobile
emergingTrends.rationale: Shared component and API layers increasingly unify web and mobile front-ends, which is the natural organizing theme this minor should teach explicitly.

topSuggestions: Curate the course list to actually match the minor's title, removing the nine game courses and the sysadmin/networks courses that are inherited from a shared pool.
Modernize the web/mobile core toward JavaScript frameworks (React), REST/GraphQL APIs, and cross-platform mobile so it reflects current practice rather than legacy website development.
Add web/mobile security and cloud deployment, the two competencies most conspicuously absent from the pool.
Publish concrete build-oriented learning outcomes for the minor.
State the 15-credit required-vs-elective structure and flag which advanced courses need major-level prerequisites.

philosophy-applied-ethics-minor-449.name: Philosophy Applied Ethics Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: There is a genuinely coherent applied-ethics backbone: PHIL 333 Moral Philosophy, PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics, PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics, PHIL 310 Logic, and PHIL 355 The Philosophy of Science form a strong, well-matched cluster for a technical university. Coherence is limited chiefly by dilution - many COM/PSY/LIT electives sit in the same menu - and by an empty requirementGroups with no declared required core.
dimensions.suggestion: Declare a required ethics core (Moral Philosophy + Logic + one applied ethics) and mark cross-disciplinary courses as supporting electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. Most PHIL courses sit behind the ENGL 102 + 200-level GER gateway (depth 4); the deepest chain (6) comes from the broader pool, not from philosophy-internal stacking, which is appropriate for a minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Ensure at least one gateway ethics course is reachable directly after ENGL 102 so students can enter the minor early.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, so the required credit count is not published, and creditReconcileDelta is null (an expected data caveat for a menu-style minor with no term plan). Courses are uniformly 3 credits, so distribution within the menu is even, but the total is opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement and the split between a required ethics core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive, but the program page carries no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits, so the strong ethics core is not visible as a structured pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description and group the strong PHIL ethics core visibly ahead of the cross-listed electives.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Applied ethics is highly current and this menu is well-aimed for NJIT: PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics and PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics speak directly to engineering and health students, and PHIL 355 Philosophy of Science supports research integrity. The clearest gap is a dedicated AI/data-ethics course as the field's fastest-growing area.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an AI and data ethics course connecting PHIL 334 with STS 375 AI and the Human Mind and STS 350 Computers and Society.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled PHIL/PSY/LIT descriptions are present and substantive; none are flagged thin or missing (PSY 361 carries an abbreviated title, 'Found of Cyberpsychology II').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; internal chains such as PSY 361 -> PSY 359 are sensible. One inconsistency: PHIL 331 lists 'HUM 102' where sibling courses list 'ENGL 102', and most courses cite a GER category rather than a specific course.

missingTopics.rationale: There is no dedicated course on algorithmic bias, privacy, and autonomous systems; PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics is adjacent but general.
STS 360 Ethics and the Environment exists in the catalog but is not surfaced in the visible ethics core; it should be cross-listed.
Organizational and corporate ethics are absent; HRM 310 is management practice, not normative ethics.
PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics is a start, but CRISPR, neurotech, and genome-editing questions are not covered.
Rights, justice, and distributive theory - the backbone of policy ethics - are not represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: The fastest-growing applied-ethics area; bridge PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics with STS 375 AI and the Human Mind.
Connect to PSY 359 Foundations of Cyberpsychology and STS 350 Computers and Society for a technically grounded treatment.
Extend PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics into gene editing, neurotech, and human enhancement.
Link the ethics core to STS 360 Ethics and the Environment and STS 363 Sustainability Studies.

emergingTrends.trend: Ethics of automation & the future of work
emergingTrends.rationale: A professional-responsibility angle increasingly central to engineering and business practice.

topSuggestions: Add a dedicated AI/data-ethics course bridging PHIL 334, STS 375, and STS 350.
Cross-list environmental ethics (STS 360) and add business/professional ethics.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and define a required ethics core vs. electives.
State the total credit requirement.
Modernize PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics to include neuroethics and genome-editing.

psychology-minor-450.name: Psychology Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A reasonable psychology spine is present: PSY 215 Biology of Behavior, PSY 321 Social Psychology, PSY 389 Psychopathology, PSY 304/307 Research Methods I/II, PSY 333 Principles of Psychometrics, and the PSY 359/361 Cyberpsychology sequence. Coherence is limited by missing core subfields (no cognitive or developmental psychology visible) and by an empty requirementGroups with no declared required core.
dimensions.suggestion: Declare a required foundation (a general-psychology gateway plus research methods) and ensure cognitive and developmental breadth in the menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. The discipline-internal chains are sensible (PSY 333 -> PSY 210; PSY 361 -> PSY 359); the deepest chain (5) is modest and healthy for a minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface PSY 210 as an explicit gateway in the menu since it already anchors internal prerequisites like PSY 333.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null so the credit requirement is not published, and creditReconcileDelta is null (an expected caveat for a menu-style minor with no parsed term plan). Courses are uniformly 3 credits, so the menu is even but the total is opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement and the split between a required methods/foundation core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive, but the program page has no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits, so the psychology pathway is not legibly structured.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description and group the menu into a foundation core (methods + breadth) and electives.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The PSY 359/361 Cyberpsychology sequence and PSY 333 Psychometrics give the minor a distinctive, technology-aligned edge suited to NJIT, and PSY 339 Psychology of Diversity is timely. However the absence of cognitive, developmental, and applied-statistics/software depth narrows preparation for graduate study and practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize PSY 307 Research Methods II and PSY 333 Psychometrics with hands-on R/Python and open-science practices, and add cognitive/developmental breadth.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and substantive; none are flagged thin or missing (PSY 361 carries an abbreviated title, 'Found of Cyberpsychology II').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; internal chains PSY 333 -> PSY 210 and PSY 361 -> PSY 359 are unambiguous. Several courses list no prerequisite (PSY 215, PSY 321, PSY 339, PSY 389), which reads as intended open entry rather than an error.

missingTopics.rationale: No cognition/perception/memory course is visible, yet it is a foundational subfield for any psychology credential.
Lifespan development is absent from the menu.
PSY 389 Psychopathology is the only clinical touchpoint; counseling and health psychology are missing.
PSY 307 Research Methods II and PSY 333 Psychometrics should include hands-on R/SPSS/Python data-analysis skills.
Beyond PSY 215 Biology of Behavior, there is little neuroscience content.

emergingTrends.rationale: PSY 359/361 already lead here - a real NJIT strength to expand into social-media effects, VR, and online behavior.
Modernize PSY 307 and PSY 333 with reproducibility, preregistration, and code-based analysis.
Telehealth and app-based interventions extend PSY 389 Psychopathology toward current practice.
Link psychology to STS 375 AI and the Human Mind for emotion-aware and human-centered AI.
Build on PSY 339 Psychology of Diversity as identity and equity become central to the field.

topSuggestions: Add core cognitive and developmental psychology courses for subfield breadth.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and define a required foundation (methods + breadth) vs. electives.
State the total credit requirement.
Modernize research methods and psychometrics with hands-on R/Python and open-science practices.
Leverage the PSY 359/361 Cyberpsychology sequence as a signature applied thread.

science-technology-society-minor-449.name: Science, Technology & Society Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The catalog carries a strong, coherent STS spine (STS 310 Technology and Human Values, STS 350 Computers and Society, STS 351 Minds and Machines, STS 375 AI and the Human Mind, STS 312 Technology and Policy, STS 363 Sustainability, STS 376 Cyborg Society), reinforced by PHIL 355 Philosophy of Science and PHIL 334 Engineering Ethics. The visible 40-of-59 sample is truncated to non-STS courses (COM/HIST/LIT/PSY), and with an empty requirementGroups the well-matched STS core is not declared as a required pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: Foreground the STS 3xx core as a declared spine (values/computers/policy/AI) rather than presenting a broad HSS elective menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. STS 364 Sustainability Policy -> STS 363 Sustainability Studies is a sensible internal build; the deepest chain (6) otherwise reflects the ENGL/GER gateway, appropriate for a minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a small number of clearly sequenced STS pairings (intro STS -> methods -> capstone) to make progression explicit.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null so the credit requirement is not published, and creditReconcileDelta is null (an expected caveat for a menu-style minor with no parsed term plan). Courses are uniformly 3 credits, so the menu is internally even but the total is opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement and the split between a required STS core and thematic electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive, but the program page has no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits, and the parsed sample is truncated so the STS identity is under-represented on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description and surface the STS 3xx core explicitly so the page reflects the minor's actual focus.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: STS offerings are markedly current: STS 350 Computers and Society, STS 375 AI and the Human Mind, STS 351 Minds and Machines, STS 312 Technology and Policy, and STS 363 Sustainability map directly to today's debates on AI governance, data, and climate. The main gap is an explicit data-justice / algorithmic-accountability offering.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or modernize a data-and-algorithmic-society course extending STS 350 Computers and Society into governance and fairness.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and substantive across STS/PHIL/PSY courses; none are flagged thin (PSY 361 carries an abbreviated title).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; STS 364 -> STS 363 and PSY 361 -> PSY 359 are clear. Most electives cite a GER category rather than a specific STS prerequisite, a mild ambiguity, and PHIL 331 lists HUM 102 where siblings list ENGL 102.

missingTopics.rationale: STS 304 Qualitative and STS 307 Quantitative Research Methods exist in the catalog; a methods requirement should be an explicit part of the pathway.
Beyond STS 350 Computers and Society, dedicated coverage of algorithmic governance and data justice is missing.
STS 312 Technology and Policy is a seed, but funding, regulation, and science-governance depth are limited.
STS 363 Sustainability and STS 360 Ethics and the Environment exist; ensure energy-transition and climate-technology coverage is included.
Biomedicine-in-society is thin; link PHIL 351 Biomedical Ethics with a health-technology STS offering.

emergingTrends.rationale: STS 375 AI and the Human Mind and STS 351 Minds and Machines position the minor well; modernize with current AI-governance material.
STS 363 Sustainability Studies and STS 364 Sustainability Policy can anchor a stronger energy/climate track.
Extend STS 350 Computers and Society into platform power, surveillance, and data rights.
STS 376 Cyborg Society and PSY 359 Cyberpsychology connect the minor to embodied human-technology relations.

emergingTrends.trend: Public trust in science & science communication
emergingTrends.rationale: Timely post-pandemic; pair STS with COM courses to address misinformation and expertise.

topSuggestions: Foreground the STS 3xx core (Technology and Human Values, Computers and Society, AI and the Human Mind, Technology and Policy) as a declared spine.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and a required core-vs-elective structure.
State the total credit requirement.
Add or modernize a data-justice / algorithmic-accountability offering.
Make an STS research-methods course (STS 304/307) an explicit part of the pathway.

technology-gender-and-diversity-minor-45.name: Technology, Gender and Diversity Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A genuine on-theme core exists: STS 342 Gender, Technology and Society, PSY 339 Psychology of Diversity, HRM 310 Managing Diversity and Inclusion, STS 352 Race and Ethnicity, and HIST 362 Sex, Gender, and the Law. But these sit inside the same broad STS/PSY pool (which also carries off-theme items like STS 315 Sports, Technology and Society and STS 365 Animal Intelligence), and with an empty requirementGroups no required core is declared.
dimensions.suggestion: Declare an on-theme required core (STS 342, PSY 339, HRM 310, STS 352, HIST 362) and separate general STS/PSY electives.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is clean: missingCourseCount is 0 (no dangling references), and chains carry consistent, explicit grade conditions. The deepest chain (5) is modest and comes from the ENGL/GER gateway and internal pairs like PSY 361 -> PSY 359; no dangling references appear.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a clear gateway (e.g., STS 201 or STS 342) so the identity courses anchor the rest of the menu.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null so the credit requirement is not published, and creditReconcileDelta is null (an expected caveat for a menu-style minor with no parsed term plan). Courses are uniformly 3 credits, so the menu is even but the total is opaque.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement and the split between the on-theme core and electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive, but the program page has no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits, and STS 325 appears with a truncated title ('ST:'), so the minor's focus is not clearly presented.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description, resolve truncated special-topics titles, and group the on-theme core ahead of general electives.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The theme is highly current for a technical university: STS 342 Gender, Technology and Society, PSY 339 Psychology of Diversity, and HRM 310 Managing Diversity and Inclusion map to inclusive design, workforce equity, and algorithmic fairness debates. Gaps are a dedicated algorithmic-bias course and broader diversity coverage (LGBTQ, disability).
dimensions.suggestion: Add an algorithmic-fairness / data-feminism course linking STS 342 with STS 350 Computers and Society and STS 375 AI and the Human Mind.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and substantive; none are flagged thin or missing, though STS 325 shows a truncated title ('ST:') and PSY 361 an abbreviated one.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; internal chains such as PSY 361 -> PSY 359 are clear. Most electives cite a GER category rather than a specific prerequisite, a mild ambiguity.

missingTopics.rationale: Beyond STS 342, foundational feminist-STS and intersectionality theory is not represented.
Designing technology for diverse users (universal design, accessibility, inclusive HCI) is absent though central to the theme.
Data and AI bias is missing; a natural connection to STS 350 Computers and Society and STS 375 AI and the Human Mind.
HRM 310 touches workplace inclusion, but a dedicated STEM-equity/pipeline course is missing.
Diversity coverage centers on gender and race (STS 342, STS 352, HIST 362); queer and disability perspectives are not represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: The front line of technology-and-diversity work; extend STS 342 and STS 350 into fairness auditing.
Universal design and assistive technology are fast-growing practice areas the minor does not yet cover.
Pair STS 342 Gender, Technology and Society with data methods (STS 307) for a critical-data track.
Link to STS 375 AI and the Human Mind to address representation and bias in AI systems.
Build on HRM 310 with measurable DEI practice as employers formalize inclusion programs.

topSuggestions: Define an on-theme required core (STS 342, PSY 339, HRM 310, STS 352, HIST 362) and separate general electives.
Add algorithmic-bias/data-feminism and inclusive-design courses.
Publish explicit learning outcomes.
State the total credit requirement.
Broaden diversity coverage (LGBTQ, disability) beyond gender and race.

theatre-arts-and-technology-minor-451.name: Theatre Arts and Technology Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The parsed course pool bears essentially no relationship to the program title: all 17 referenced courses are PSY, STS, HIST, HRM, or LIT (e.g., PSY 321 Social Psychology, STS 376 Cyborg Society, HRM 310 Managing Diversity, LIT 386 Science Fiction), and not a single theatre, performance, dramaturgy, or stagecraft course appears. With an empty requirementGroups there is also no declared structure. This is a serious coherence failure and, most likely, a data-extraction gap that must be corrected.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the minor with actual theatre courses (acting/directing, dramaturgy, technical theatre) or fix the catalog data, then declare a required core.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: For the courses that are present the prerequisite graph is clean (missingCourseCount=0, sensible 'C or higher' conditions, deepest chain 5). However, for a Theatre Arts and Technology minor the absence of any production skills ladder (e.g., introductory -> advanced lighting/sound/design) is itself a structural deficiency the graph cannot show.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a sequenced technical-theatre ladder (foundations -> advanced design/production) with explicit prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the parsed data (the page carries only a name and a course menu, with an empty requirementGroups). For a catalog page this absence is itself a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 explicit, measurable program learning outcomes stating what a completer of the minor should know and be able to do.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null so the requirement is unpublished, and creditReconcileDelta is null (an expected caveat for a menu-style minor with no parsed plan). More fundamentally, with only 17 off-theme courses it is unclear how a student would actually assemble a theatre credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the total credit requirement and, once theatre courses are added, define the core-vs-elective credit split.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page has no description, no requirementGroups, and no stated credits, and the small 17-course pool does not match the program title, so the catalog page does not communicate what the minor is.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a program description and a legible plan of study that actually reflects theatre arts and technology.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The theatre-technology field centers on lighting, sound, scenic/projection design, stage management, and increasingly immersive/virtual production - none of which appear; the borrowed PSY/STS technology courses (e.g., STS 375 AI and the Human Mind) do not substitute for production skills. As presented, the minor offers no preparation for theatre or entertainment-technology careers.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce industry-standard production coursework (lighting, sound, projection/media design, stage management) reflecting current theatre practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: The descriptions of the courses that are present are substantive and none are flagged thin or missing (PSY 361 shows an abbreviated title); however the score is capped because none of the described courses is a theatre course, so the descriptions do not represent the program's stated subject.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: prerequisiteRaw is present with consistent 'C or higher' conditions and 0 dangling references; PSY 361 -> PSY 359 is a clear internal chain. Most electives cite a GER category rather than a specific prerequisite, a mild ambiguity.

missingTopics.rationale: Acting, directing, dramaturgy, and script analysis are entirely absent from the pool despite the program title.
Lighting, sound, and set/scenic design and stage management - the 'technology' the title promises - are missing.
Projection mapping, video scenography, and show-control tools (e.g., QLab) are not represented.
Creation-based methods for making new work are absent.
LIT 386 Science Fiction is the only literature course; there is no dramatic-literature or theatre-history grounding.

emergingTrends.rationale: Virtual production and AR/VR staging are a natural NJIT tech-arts niche the minor could own once theatre courses exist.
Live digital scenography with tools like QLab, Isadora, or TouchDesigner is now standard in professional theatre technology.
Game-engine (Unreal) stagecraft and digital puppetry are reshaping design and would distinguish a technology-focused theatre minor.
Object-based and spatial audio are increasingly central to performance technology.
Connect LIT 386 Science Fiction and STS 375 AI and the Human Mind to generative scenography and performance futures.

topSuggestions: Critical: populate the minor with real theatre courses (acting/directing, dramaturgy, stagecraft) or correct the catalog data - the current pool contains no theatre content and does not match the title.
Add the technology core the title promises: lighting, sound, projection/media design, and virtual/immersive production.
Publish explicit learning outcomes and a required production sequence.
State the total credit requirement and define a core-vs-elective structure.
Add theatre history and dramatic literature for artistic grounding.
